segments.text: It’s hard for us humans to make any kind of clean predictions about highly nonlinear dynamical systems. But again, to your point, we might be very surprised what classical learning systems might be able to do about even fluid.

translation.text: 我们人类很难对高度非线性动力系统做出任何清晰的预测。但同样，就你的观点而言，我们可能会非常惊讶经典学习系统甚至能够对流体进行处理。

segments.text: Yes, exactly. I mean, fluid dynamics, Navier-Stokes equations, these are traditionally thought of as very, very difficult intractable problems to do on classical systems.

translation.text: 是的，完全正确。我的意思是，流体动力学、纳维-斯托克斯方程，这些传统上被认为是在经典系统上解决非常非常困难的棘手问题。

segments.text: They take enormous amounts of compute, weather prediction systems. These kinds of things all involve fluid dynamics calculations.

translation.text: 它们需要大量的计算和天气预报系统。这类事情都涉及流体动力学计算。

segments.text: But again, if you look at something like Veo, our video generation model, it can model liquids quite well, surprisingly well.

translation.text: 但同样，如果你看看像我们的视频生成模型 Veo 这样的东西，它可以很好地模拟液体，效果出奇地好。

segments.text: And materials, specular lighting, I love the ones where there’s people who generate videos where there’s clear liquids going through hydraulic presses and then it’s being squeezed out.

translation.text: 还有材质、镜面照明，我喜欢那些制作视频的人，其中有透明液体通过液压机，然后被挤出。

segments.text: I used to write physics engines and graphics engines in my early days in gaming, and I know it’s just so painstakingly hard to build programs that can do that.

translation.text: 我在游戏行业的早期曾编写过物理引擎和图形引擎，而且我知道构建可以做到这一点的程序非常困难。

segments.text: And yet somehow these systems are reverse engineering from just watching YouTube videos. So presumably what’s happening is it’s extracting some underlying structure around how these materials behave.

translation.text: 然而不知何故，这些系统只是通过观看 YouTube 视频进行逆向工程。因此，大概正在发生的事情是围绕这些材料的行为提取一些底层结构。

segments.text: So perhaps there is some kind of lower dimensional manifold that can be learned if we actually fully understood what’s going on under the hood.

translation.text: 因此，如果我们真正完全理解幕后发生的事情，也许可以学习某种低维流形。

segments.text: That’s maybe true of most of reality.

translation.text: 现实中的大部分情况也许都是如此。

segments.text: The following is a conversation with Demis Hassabis, his second time on the podcast. He is the leader of Google DeepMind and is now a Nobel Prize winner.

translation.text: 以下是与 德米斯·哈萨比斯 的对话，这是他第二次参加播客。他是谷歌DeepMind的领导者，现在是诺贝尔奖获得者。

segments.text: Demis is one of the most brilliant and fascinating minds in the world today working on understanding and building intelligence and exploring the big mysteries of our universe.

translation.text: 戴米斯是当今世界上最聪明、最迷人的人之一，致力于理解和构建智能以及探索宇宙的巨大奥秘。

segments.text: This was truly an honor and a pleasure for me.

translation.text: 这对我来说确实是一种荣幸和快乐。

segments.text: This is the Lex Fridman Podcast. To support it, please check out our sponsors in the description and consider subscribing to this channel.

translation.text: 这是莱克斯·弗里德曼播客。为了支持它，请查看说明中的赞助商并考虑订阅此频道。

segments.text: And now, dear friends, here’s Demis Hassabis.

translation.text: 现在，亲爱的朋友们，这是黛米斯·哈萨比斯。

segments.text: In your Nobel Prize lecture, you propose what I think is a super interesting conjecture that “any pattern that can be generated or found in nature can be efficiently discovered and modeled by a classical learning algorithm.”

translation.text: 在您的诺贝尔奖演讲中，您提出了一个我认为非常有趣的猜想：“自然界中可以生成或发现的任何模式都可以通过经典学习算法有效地发现和建模。”

segments.text: What kind of patterns or systems might be included in that? Biology, chemistry, physics, maybe cosmology, neuroscience? What are we talking about?

translation.text: 其中可能包含什么样的模式或系统？生物学、化学、物理学，也许还有宇宙学、神经科学？我们在说什么？

segments.text: Sure. Well, look, I felt that it’s sort of a tradition, I think, of Nobel Prize lectures that you’re supposed to be a little bit provocative and I wanted to follow that tradition.

translation.text: 当然。嗯，听着，我觉得这是诺贝尔奖演讲的一种传统，你应该有点挑衅性，我想遵循这个传统。

segments.text: What I was talking about there is if you take a step back and you look at all the work that we’ve done, especially with the Alpha X projects, so I’m thinking AlphaGo, of course, AlphaFold, what they really are is we are building models of very combinatorially,

translation.text: 我所说的是，如果你退后一步，看看我们所做的所有工作，特别是 Alpha X 项目，所以我想到的是 AlphaGo，当然还有 AlphaFold，它们真正的本质是我们正在构建非常组合的模型，

segments.text: high dimensional spaces that if you try to brute force a solution, find the best move and go, or find the exact shape of a protein, and if you enumerated all the possibilities, there wouldn’t be enough time in the time of the universe.

translation.text: 在高维空间中，如果你尝试暴力解决，找到最好的移动方式，或者找到蛋白质的确切形状，如果你列举了所有的可能性，宇宙的时间就不够了。

segments.text: So you have to do something much smarter. And what we did in both cases was build models of those environments and that guided the search in a smart way and that makes it tractable.

translation.text: 所以你必须做一些更聪明的事情。在这两种情况下，我们所做的都是构建这些环境的模型，并以智能的方式指导搜索，使其易于处理。

segments.text: So if you think about protein folding, which is obviously a natural system, why should that be possible? How does physics do that?

translation.text: 因此，如果你考虑一下蛋白质折叠，这显然是一个自然系统，为什么这可能呢？物理学是如何做到这一点的？

segments.text: Proteins fold in milliseconds in our bodies, so somehow physics solves this problem that we’ve now also solved computationally. And I think the reason that’s possible is that in nature, natural systems have structure because they were subject to evolutionary processes that shape them.

translation.text: 蛋白质在我们体内的折叠时间为几毫秒，因此物理学以某种方式解决了这个问题，我们现在也通过计算解决了这个问题。我认为这可能的原因是，在自然界中，自然系统具有结构，因为它们受到塑造它们的进化过程的影响。

segments.text: And if that’s true, then you can maybe learn what that structure is.

translation.text: 如果这是真的，那么你也许可以了解这个结构是什么。

segments.text: This perspective I think is a really interesting one. You’ve hinted it at it, which is almost like crudely stated, anything that can be evolved can be efficiently modeled.

translation.text: 我认为这个观点非常有趣。你已经暗示过，这几乎就像粗略地说，任何可以进化的东西都可以有效地建模。

segments.text: Think there’s some truth to that?

translation.text: 你认为这有一定道理吗？

segments.text: Yeah. I sometimes call it survival of the stablest or something like that because of course there’s evolution for life, living things, but there’s also, if you think about geological times, so the shape of mountains, that’s been shaped by weathering processes over thousands of years, but then you can even take it cosmological,

translation.text: 是的。我有时称之为“最稳定的生存”或类似的东西，因为当然有生命、生物的进化，但如果你考虑地质时代，那么还有山脉的形状，它是由数千年的风化过程塑造的，但你甚至可以把它理解为宇宙学的，

segments.text: the orbits of planets, the shapes of asteroids. These have all been survived kind of processes that have acted on them many, many times.

translation.text: 行星的轨道，小行星的形状。这些都经历了很多很多次的过程而幸存下来。

segments.text: If that’s true, then there should be some sort of pattern that you can kind of reverse learn and a kind of manifold really that helps you search to the right solution, to the right shape and actually allow you to predict things about it in an efficient way because it’s not a random pattern.

translation.text: 如果这是真的，那么应该有某种你可以反向学习的模式，以及一种真正可以帮助你搜索正确的解决方案、正确的形状的流形，并且实际上允许你以有效的方式预测有关它的事情，因为它不是随机模式。

segments.text: So it may not be possible for man-made things or abstract things like factorizing large numbers because unless there’s patterns in the number space, which there might be, but if there’s not and it’s uniform, then there’s no pattern to learn, there’s no model to learn that will help you search.

translation.text: 因此，人造事物或抽象事物（例如分解大数）可能是不可能的，因为除非数字空间中存在模式（可能存在），但如果不存在并且是统一的，那么就没有可学习的模式，也没有可以帮助您搜索的模型。

segments.text: So you have to do brute force. So in that case you maybe need a quantum computer, something like this. But in most things in nature that we’re interested in are not like that.

translation.text: 所以你必须用蛮力。所以在这种情况下你可能需要一台量子计算机，像这样的东西。但我们感兴趣的自然界大多数事物并不是这样的。

segments.text: They have structure that evolved for a reason and survived over time. And if that’s true, I think that’s potentially learnable by a neural network.

translation.text: 它们的结构是出于某种原因而进化并随着时间的推移而生存下来的。如果这是真的，我认为神经网络可能可以学习这一点。

segments.text: It’s like nature is doing a search process and it’s so fascinating that in that search process, it’s creating systems that could be efficiently modeled.

translation.text: 就像大自然正在进行搜索过程一样，它是如此令人着迷，在这个搜索过程中，它正在创建可以有效建模的系统。

segments.text: That’s right. Yeah.

translation.text: 这是正确的。是的。

segments.text: So interesting.

translation.text: 太有趣了。

segments.text: So they can be efficiently rediscovered or recovered because nature’s not random. Everything that we see around us, including the elements that are more stable, all of those things, they’re subject to some kind of selection process pressure.

translation.text: 因此，它们可以被有效地重新发现或恢复，因为自然不是随机的。我们在周围看到的一切，包括更稳定的元素，所有这些东西，它们都受到某种选择过程的压力。

segments.text: Do you think because you’re also a fan of theoretical computer science and complexity, do you think we can come up with a complexity class, like a complexity zoo type of class where maybe it’s the set of learnable systems, the set of learnable natural systems, LNS.

translation.text: 你是否认为因为你也是理论计算机科学和复杂性的粉丝，你认为我们可以提出一个复杂性类，就像复杂性动物园类型的类，其中可能是一组可学习的系统，一组可学习的自然系统，LNS。

segments.text: This is a Demis Hassabis new class of systems that could be actually learnable by classical systems in this kind of way, natural systems that can be modeled efficiently.

translation.text: 这是 德米斯·哈萨比斯 的一类新系统，经典系统实际上可以通过这种方式进行学习，即可以有效建模的自然系统。

segments.text: Yeah, I mean I’ve always been fascinated by the P equals NP question and what is model-able by classical systems, i.e. non-quantum systems, Turing machines in effect.

translation.text: 是的，我的意思是我一直对 P 等于 NP 问题以及经典系统（即非量子系统、图灵机）可建模的内容着迷。

segments.text: And that’s exactly what I’m working on actually in my few moments of spare time with a few colleagues about should there be maybe a new class or problem that is solvable by this type of neural network process and kind of mapped onto these natural systems,

translation.text: 这正是我在业余时间和几位同事一起研究的问题，是否应该有一个新的类或问题可以通过这种类型的神经网络过程来解决，并映射到这些自然系统上，

segments.text: so the things that exist in physics and have structure. So I think that could be a very interesting new way of thinking about it. And it sort of fits with the way I think about physics in general, which is that I think information is primary, information is the most sort of fundamental unit of the universe, more fundamental than energy and matter.

translation.text: 因此，存在于物理学中并具有结构的事物。所以我认为这可能是一种非常有趣的新思考方式。这有点符合我对物理学的一般看法，即我认为信息是首要的，信息是宇宙最基本的单位，比能量和物质更基本。

segments.text: I think they can all be converted into each other, but I think of the universe as a kind of informational system.

translation.text: 我认为它们都可以相互转换，但我认为宇宙是一种信息系统。

segments.text: So when you think of the universe as an informational system, then the P equals NP question is a physics question.

translation.text: 因此，当你将宇宙视为一个信息系统时，那么 P 等于 NP 问题就是一个物理问题。

segments.text: That’s right.

translation.text: 这是正确的。

segments.text: And is a question that can help us actually solve the entirety of this whole thing going on.

translation.text: 这是一个可以帮助我们真正解决整个事件的问题。

segments.text: Yeah, I think it’s one of the most fundamental questions actually if you think of physics as informational and the answer to that, I think it’s going to be very enlightening.

translation.text: 是的，我认为这实际上是最基本的问题之一，如果你将物理学视为信息性的，那么它的答案将非常具有启发性。

segments.text: More specific to the P and MP question, again, some of the stuff we’re saying is kind of crazy right now just like the Christian Anfinsen Nobel Prize speech, controversial thing that he said sounded crazy and then you went and got a Nobel Prize for this with John Jumper, solved the problem.

translation.text: 更具体地说，关于 P 和 MP 问题，我们现在所说的一些东西有点疯狂，就像克里斯蒂安·安芬森诺贝尔奖演讲一样，他所说的有争议的事情听起来很疯狂，然后你和约翰·詹珀一起获得了诺贝尔奖，解决了问题。

segments.text: So let me just stick to the P equals NP. Do you think there’s something in this thing we’re talking about that could be shown if you can do something like a polynomial time or constant time compute ahead of time and construct this gigantic model, then you can solve some of these extremely difficult problems in a theoretical computer science kind of way?

translation.text: 所以让我坚持 P 等于 NP。你认为我们正在谈论的这件事是否可以证明，如果你可以提前进行多项式时间或常数时间计算并构建这个巨大的模型，那么你可以用理论计算机科学的方式解决其中一些极其困难的问题？

segments.text: Yeah, I think that there are actually a huge class of problems that could be couched in this way, the way we did AlphaGo and the way we did AlphaFold, where you model what the dynamics of the system is, the properties of that system, the environment that you are trying to understand,

translation.text: 是的，我认为实际上有一大类问题可以用这种方式来表达，就像我们做 AlphaGo 的方式和我们做 AlphaFold 的方式，你可以在其中建模系统的动态是什么，该系统的属性，你试图理解的环境，

segments.text: and then that makes the search for the solution or the prediction of the next step efficient. Basically polynomial times, so tractable by a classical system, which a neural network is.

translation.text: 然后这使得对解决方案的搜索或下一步的预测变得高效。基本上是多项式时间，很容易被经典系统（神经网络）处理。

segments.text: It runs on normal computers, right? Classical computers, Turing machines in effect. And I think it’s one of the most interesting questions there is, is how far can that paradigm go?

translation.text: 它可以在普通计算机上运行，​​对吗？经典计算机，实际上是图灵机。我认为这是最有趣的问题之一，这种范式能走多远？

segments.text: I think we’ve proven, and the AI community in general that classical systems, Turing machines can go a lot further than we previously thought.

translation.text: 我认为我们和整个人工智能社区已经证明，经典系统、图灵机可以比我们之前想象的走得更远。

segments.text: They can do things like model the structures of proteins and play go to better than world champion level. And a lot of people would’ve thought maybe 10, 20 years ago that was decades away, or maybe you would need some sort of quantum machines to quantum systems to be able to do things like protein folding.

translation.text: 他们可以做诸如蛋白质结构建模之类的事情，并且可以达到比世界冠军水平更好的水平。很多人可能会认为 10、20 年前那是几十年后的事了，或者也许你需要某种量子机器到量子系统才能完成蛋白质折叠等事情。

segments.text: And so I think we haven’t really even sort of scratched the surface yet of what classical systems so-called could do.

translation.text: 所以我认为我们还没有真正触及所谓的经典系统所能做的事情的表面。

segments.text: And of course, AGI being built on a neural network system on top of a neural network system on top of a classical computer would be the ultimate expression of that.

translation.text: 当然，建立在经典计算机之上的神经网络系统之上的通用人工智能将是这一点的最终体现。

segments.text: And I think the limit, what the bounds of that kind of system, what it can do, it’s a very interesting question and directly speaks to the P equals NP question.

translation.text: 我认为极限，这种系统的界限是什么，它能做什么，这是一个非常有趣的问题，直接涉及到 P 等于 NP 问题。

segments.text: What do you think, again, hypothetical, might be outside of this? Maybe emergent phenomena? If you look at cellular automata, you have extremely simple systems and then some complexity emerges.

translation.text: 再次假设，您认为除此之外可能还有什么？也许是涌现现象？如果你观察元胞自动机，你会发现系统非常简单，但随后就会出现一些复杂性。

segments.text: Maybe that would be outside or even would you guess even that might be amenable to efficient modeling by a classical machine?

translation.text: 也许那是外部的，或者甚至你会猜测这可能适合经典机器的有效建模吗？

segments.text: Yeah, I think those systems would be right on the boundary. So I think most emergent systems, cellular automata, things like that could be model-able by a classical system.

translation.text: 是的，我认为这些系统就在边界上。所以我认为大多数新兴系统、元胞自动机之类的东西都可以通过经典系统进行建模。

segments.text: You just sort of do a forward simulation of it and it’d probably be efficient enough. Of course there’s the question of things like chaotic systems where the initial conditions really matter and then you get to some uncorrelated end state.

translation.text: 你只需对其进行前向模拟，它可能就足够有效了。当然，存在像混沌系统这样的问题，其中初始条件确实很重要，然后你会达到一些不相关的最终状态。

segments.text: Now those could be difficult to model. So I think these are kind of the open questions, but I think when you step back and look at what we’ve done with the systems and the problems that we’ve solved, and then you look at things like Veo 3 on video generation sort of rendering physics and lighting and things like that,

translation.text: 现在这些可能很难建模。所以我认为这些都是悬而未决的问题，但我认为当你退一步看看我们对系统做了什么以及我们已经解决的问题，然后你看看像 Veo 3 这样的视频生成渲染物理和照明之类的东西，

segments.text: really core fundamental things in physics, it’s pretty interesting. I think it’s telling us something quite fundamental about how the universe is structured in my opinion.

translation.text: 确实是物理学的核心基础知识，非常有趣。我认为它告诉我们一些关于我认为宇宙是如何构造的非常基本的东西。

segments.text: So in a way that’s what I want to build AGI for is to help us as scientists answer these questions like P equals NP.

translation.text: 所以在某种程度上，我想要建立 AGI 的目的是帮助我们科学家回答这些问题，比如 P 等于 NP。

segments.text: Yeah, I think we might be continuously surprised about what is model-able by classical computers. I mean AlphaFold 3 on the interaction side is surprising that you can make any kind of progress on that direction.

translation.text: 是的，我认为我们可能会对经典计算机可建模的内容不断感到惊讶。我的意思是 AlphaFold 3 在交互方面令人惊讶，你可以在这个方向上取得任何进展。

segments.text: AlphaGenome is surprising that you can map the genetic code to the function. Kind of playing with the emergent kind of phenomena, you think there’s so many combinatorial options and then here you go, you can find the kernel that is efficiently model-able.

translation.text: AlphaGenome 令人惊讶的是您可以将遗传密码映射到功能。在处理新兴现象时，您认为有很多组合选项，然后您就可以找到可有效建模的内核。

segments.text: Yes, because there’s some structure, there’s some landscape in the energy landscape or whatever it is that you can follow, some gradient you can follow. And of course what neural networks are very good at is following gradients.

translation.text: 是的，因为有一些结构，有一些能量景观中的景观或者任何你可以遵循的东西，一些你可以遵循的梯度。当然，神经网络非常擅长的是遵循梯度。

segments.text: And so if there’s one to follow and you can specify the objective function correctly, you don’t have to deal with all that complexity, which I think is how we maybe have naively thought about it for decades, those problems.

translation.text: 因此，如果有一个可遵循的目标函数，并且您可以正确指定目标函数，那么您就不必处理所有这些复杂性，我认为这就是我们几十年来天真地思考这些问题的方式。

segments.text: If you just enumerate all the possibilities, it looks totally intractable and there’s many, many problems like that.

translation.text: 如果你只是列举所有的可能性，看起来非常棘手，而且这样的问题还有很多很多。

segments.text: And then you think, “Well, it’s like 10 to 300 possible protein structures, 10 to the 170 possible go positions. All of these are way more than atoms in the universe, so how could one possibly find the right solution or predict the next step?”

translation.text: 然后你会想，“嗯，这就像 10 到 300 种可能的蛋白质结构，10 到 170 种可能的位置。所有这些都比宇宙中的原子要多得多，那么我们怎么可能找到正确的解决方案或预测下一步呢？”

segments.text: But it turns out that it is possible. And of course reality in nature does do it. Proteins do fold.

translation.text: 但事实证明这是可能的。当然，自然界的现实确实做到了这一点。蛋白质确实会折叠。

segments.text: So that gives you confidence that there must be, if we understood how physics was doing that in a sense and we could mimic that process, i.e. model that process, it should be possible on our classical systems is basically what the conjecture is about.

translation.text: 因此，这给了你信心，如果我们理解物理学在某种意义上是如何做到这一点的，并且我们可以模仿该过程，即对该过程进行建模，那么在我们的经典系统上应该是可能的，这基本上就是猜想的内容。

segments.text: And of course there’s nonlinear dynamical systems, highly nonlinear dynamical systems, everything involving fluid. I recently had a conversation with Terence Tao who mathematically contends with a very difficult aspect of systems that have some singularities in them that break the mathematics, and it’s just hard for us humans to make any kind of clean predictions about highly nonlinear dynamical systems.

translation.text: 当然，还有非线性动力系统、高度非线性动力系统，一切都涉及流体。我最近与陶哲轩进行了一次对话，他在数学上与系统的一个非常困难的方面进行了斗争，这些系统中存在一些打破数学的奇点，而我们人类很难对高度非线性动力系统做出任何清晰的预测。

segments.text: But again, to your point, we might be very surprised what classical learning systems might be able to do about even fluid.

translation.text: 但同样，就你的观点而言，我们可能会非常惊讶经典学习系统甚至能够对流体进行处理。

segments.text: Yes, exactly. I mean fluid dynamics, Navier-Stokes equations, these are traditionally thought of as very, very difficult, intractable kind of problems to do on classical systems.

translation.text: 是的，完全正确。我指的是流体动力学、纳维-斯托克斯方程，这些传统上被认为是在经典系统上解决非常非常困难、棘手的问题。

segments.text: They take enormous amounts of compute, weather prediction systems. These kind of things all involve fluid dynamics calculations. But again, if you look at something like Veo, our video generation model, it can model liquids quite well, surprisingly well.

translation.text: 它们需要大量的计算和天气预报系统。这类事情都涉及到流体动力学计算。但同样，如果你看看像我们的视频生成模型 Veo 这样的东西，它可以很好地模拟液体，效果出奇地好。

segments.text: And materials, specular lighting, I love the ones where there’s people who generate videos where there’s clear liquids going through hydraulic presses and then it’s being squeezed out.

translation.text: 还有材质、镜面照明，我喜欢那些制作视频的人，其中有透明液体通过液压机，然后被挤出。

segments.text: I used to write physics engines and graphics engines in my early days in gaming, and I know it’s just so painstakingly hard to build programs that can do that.

translation.text: 我在游戏行业的早期曾编写过物理引擎和图形引擎，而且我知道构建可以做到这一点的程序非常困难。

segments.text: And yet somehow these systems are reverse engineering from just watching YouTube videos. So presumably what’s happening is it’s extracting some underlying structure around how these materials behave.

translation.text: 然而不知何故，这些系统只是通过观看 YouTube 视频进行逆向工程。因此，大概正在发生的事情是围绕这些材料的行为提取一些底层结构。

segments.text: So perhaps there is some kind of lower dimensional manifold that can be learned if we actually fully understood what’s going on under the hood.

translation.text: 因此，如果我们真正完全理解幕后发生的事情，也许可以学习某种低维流形。

segments.text: That’s maybe true of most of reality.

translation.text: 现实中的大部分情况也许都是如此。

segments.text: Yeah, I’ve been continuously precisely by this aspect of Veo 3. I think a lot of people highlight different aspects including the comedic and the mean and all that kind of stuff.

translation.text: 是的，我一直在关注 Veo 3 的这一方面。我认为很多人都强调不同的方面，包括喜剧性和平均性等等。

segments.text: And then the ultra realistic ability to capture humans in a really nice way that’s compelling and feels close to reality, and then combine that with native audio.

translation.text: 然后是超现实的能力，以一种非常好的方式捕捉人类，这种方式令人信服并且感觉接近现实，然后将其与原生音频相结合。

segments.text: All of those are marvelous things about Veo 3, but exactly the thing you’re mentioning, which is the physics.

translation.text: 所有这些都是 Veo 3 的奇妙之处，但正是你所提到的，那就是物理学。

segments.text: Yeah.

translation.text: 是的。

segments.text: It’s not perfect, but it’s pretty damn good. And then the really interesting scientific question is what is it understanding about our world in order to be able to do that?

translation.text: 它并不完美，但已经非常好了。然后，真正有趣的科学问题是，为了能够做到这一点，它对我们的世界有什么了解？

segments.text: Because if the cynical, take with diffusion models, there’s no way to understands anything. But I don’t think you can generate that kind of video without understanding.

translation.text: 因为如果愤世嫉俗地采用扩散模型，就无法理解任何事情。但我认为你无法在不理解的情况下生成这种视频。

segments.text: And then our own philosophical notion of what it means to understand then is brought to the surface. To what degree do you think Veo 3 understands our world?

translation.text: 然后我们自己关于理解那时意味着什么的哲学概念就浮出水面。您认为 Veo 3 在多大程度上了解我们的世界？

segments.text: I think to the extent that it can predict the next frames in a coherent way, that is a form of understanding, not in the anthropomorphic version of, it’s not some kind of deep philosophical understanding of what’s going on, I don’t think these systems have that,

translation.text: 我认为它可以以连贯的方式预测下一帧，这是一种理解形式，而不是拟人化版本，它不是对正在发生的事情的某种深刻的哲学理解，我不认为这些系统具有这种能力，

segments.text: but they certainly have modeled enough of the dynamics, put it that way, that they can pretty accurately generate whatever it is, eight seconds of consistent video that by eye, at least at a glance, is quite hard to distinguish what the issues are.

translation.text: 但他们确实已经对动态进行了足够的建模，也就是说，他们可以非常准确地生成无论是什么，八秒的一致视频，通过肉眼，至少乍一看，很难区分问题是什么。

segments.text: And imagine that in two or three more years’ time, that’s the thing I’m thinking about and how incredible that will look given where we’ve come from the early versions of that one or two years ago.

translation.text: 想象一下，再过两三年，这就是我正在考虑的事情，考虑到我们在一两年前的早期版本，这将看起来多么令人难以置信。

segments.text: And so the rate of progress is incredible. And I think I’m like you is like a lot of people love all of the stand-up comedians and that actually captures a lot of human dynamics very well and body language, but actually the thing I’m most impressed with and fascinated by is the physics behavior, the lighting and materials and liquids.

translation.text: 所以进步的速度是令人难以置信的。我想我就像你们一样，很多人都喜欢所有的单口喜剧演员，它们实际上很好地捕捉了很多人类动态和肢体语言，但实际上，我印象最深刻和最着迷的是物理行为、灯光、材料和液体。

segments.text: And it’s pretty amazing that it can do that. And I think that shows that it has some notion of at least intuitive physics, how things are supposed to work intuitively, maybe the way that a human child would understand physics, right, as opposed to a PhD student really being able to unpack all the equations.

translation.text: 它能做到这一点真是太神奇了。我认为这表明它至少有一些直观的物理概念，事物应该如何直观地工作，也许是人类孩子理解物理的方式，对吧，而不是博士生真正能够解开所有方程。

segments.text: It’s more of an intuitive physics understanding.

translation.text: 这更多的是一种直观的物理理解。

segments.text: Well, that intuitive physics understanding, that’s the base layer, that’s the thing people sometimes call a common-sense. It really understands something. I think that really surprised a lot of people.

translation.text: 嗯，直观的物理理解，这是基础层，这就是人们有时所说的常识。确实是明白了一些东西。我认为这确实让很多人感到惊讶。

segments.text: It blows my mind that I just didn’t think it would be possible to generate that level of realism without understanding. There’s this notion that you can only understand the physical world by having an embodied AI system, a robot that interacts with that world.

translation.text: 令我震惊的是，我只是认为在不理解的情况下不可能产生那种程度的现实主义。有一种观点认为，只有拥有具体的人工智能系统（即与物理世界交互的机器人）才能理解物理世界。

segments.text: That’s the only way to construct an understanding of that world. But Veo 3 is directly challenging that it feels like.

translation.text: 这是构建对这个世界的理解的唯一方法。但 Veo 3 给人的感觉是直接挑战。

segments.text: Yes, and it’s very interesting, even if you were to ask me five, 10 years ago, I would’ve said, even though I was immersed in all of this, I would’ve said, “Well, yeah, you probably need to understand intuitive physics.

translation.text: 是的，这非常有趣，即使你在五、十年前问我，我也会说，即使我沉浸在这一切之中，我也会说，“嗯，是的，你可能需要理解直觉物理学。

segments.text: If I push this off the table, this glass, it will maybe shatter and the liquid will spill out. So we know all of these things.” But I thought that, and there’s a lot of theories in neuroscience, it’s called action in perception where you need to act in the world to really, truly perceive it in a deep way.

translation.text: 如果我把它从桌子上推开，这个玻璃杯，它可能会碎裂，液体会溢出。所以我们知道所有这些事情。”但我认为，神经科学中有很多理论，这被称为感知中的行动，你需要在世界上采取行动，才能真正、真正地深刻地感知它。

segments.text: And there was a lot of theories about you’d need embodied intelligence or robotics or something, or maybe at least simulated action so that you would understand things like intuitive physics.

translation.text: 有很多理论说你需要具身智能或机器人技术之类的东西，或者至少需要模拟动作，这样你才能理解直觉物理学等东西。

segments.text: But it seems like you can understand it through passive observation, which is pretty surprising to me. And again, I think hints at something underlying about the nature of reality in my opinion, beyond just the cool videos that it generates.

translation.text: 但似乎你可以通过被动观察来理解它，这让我很惊讶。再说一次，我认为在我看来，这暗示了现实本质的一些潜在的东西，而不仅仅是它生成的很酷的视频。

segments.text: And of course there’s next stages is maybe even making those videos interactive so one can actually step into them and move around them, which would be really mind-blowing, especially given my games background.

translation.text: 当然，下一阶段甚至可能使这些视频具有互动性，这样人们就可以真正步入其中并在其中移动，这将非常令人兴奋，尤其是考虑到我的游戏背景。

segments.text: So you can imagine. And then I think we’re starting to get towards what I would call a world model, a model of how the world works, the mechanics of the world, the physics of the world, and the things in that world.

translation.text: 所以你可以想象。然后我认为我们开始走向我所说的世界模型，世界如何运作、世界的力学、世界的物理以及那个世界中的事物的模型。

segments.text: And of course that’s what you would need for a true AGI system.

translation.text: 当然，这就是真正的 AGI 系统所需要的。

segments.text: I have to talk to you about video games. So you are being a bit trolley. I think you’re having more and more fun on Twitter, on X, which is great to see.

translation.text: 我得和你聊聊电子游戏。你最近有点调皮，越来越喜欢在 Twitter，也就是 X 上逗大家了，很高兴看到这一点。

segments.text: So a guy named Jimmy Apples tweeted, “Let me play a video game of my Veo 3 videos already. Google cooked so good.

translation.text: 于是，一个名叫 Jimmy Apples 的人发推文说：“让我玩一下我的 Veo 3 视频的视频游戏吧。Google 做得太好了。”

segments.text: Playable world models wen?” And then you co-tweeted that with, “Now, wouldn’t that be something?” So how hard is it to build game worlds with AI?

translation.text: “可玩的世界模型什么时候来？”然后你转发并评论道：“那不是很有意思吗？”那么，用人工智能构建游戏世界究竟有多难？

segments.text: Maybe can you look out into the future feature of video games five, 10 years out? What do you think that looks like?

translation.text: 也许您能展望一下五年、十年后电子游戏的未来特征吗？你觉得那看起来像什么？

segments.text: Well, games were my first love really. And doing AI for games was the first thing I did professionally in my teenage years and with the first major AI systems that I built and I always want to scratch that itch one day and come back to that.

translation.text: 嗯，游戏确实是我的初恋。为游戏开发人工智能是我在青少年时期专业做的第一件事，也是我构建的第一个主要人工智能系统，我总是希望有一天能解决这个问题，然后再回来。

segments.text: And I will do, I think, and I think I sort of dream about what would I have done back in the nineties if I’d had access to the kind of AI systems we have today?

translation.text: 我想，我会做的，我想，我想，如果我能够使用我们今天拥有的那种人工智能系统，我会在九十年代做什么？

segments.text: And I think you could build absolutely mind-blowing games.

translation.text: 我认为你可以构建绝对令人兴奋的游戏。

segments.text: And I think the next stage is I always used to love making, all the games I’ve made are open world games, so they’re games where there’s a simulation and then there’s AI characters, and then the player interacts with that simulation and the simulation adapts to the way the player plays.

translation.text: 我认为下一阶段是我一直习惯于制作，我制作的所有游戏都是开放世界游戏，所以它们是有模拟的游戏，然后有人工智能角色，然后玩家与模拟互动，模拟会适应玩家的游戏方式。

segments.text: And I always thought they were the coolest games because, so games like Theme Park that I worked on where everybody’s game experience would be unique to them because you’re kind of co-creating the game.

translation.text: 我一直认为它们是最酷的游戏，因为我制作的像主题公园这样的游戏，每个人的游戏体验对他们来说都是独一无二的，因为你是在共同创造游戏。

segments.text: We set up the parameters, we set up initial conditions, and then you as the player immersed in it, and then you are co-creating it with the simulation.

translation.text: 我们设置参数，设置初始条件，然后你作为玩家沉浸其中，然后你与模拟共同创建它。

segments.text: But of course it’s very hard to program open world games. You’ve got to be able to create content whichever direction the player goes in, and you want it to be compelling no matter what the player chooses.

translation.text: 但当然，对开放世界游戏进行编程是非常困难的。无论玩家进入哪个方向，您都必须能够创建内容，并且无论玩家选择什么，您都希望它具有吸引力。

segments.text: And so it was always quite difficult to build things like cellular automata actually, type of those kind of classical systems, which created some emergent behavior, but they’re always a little bit fragile, a little bit limited.

translation.text: 因此，实际上构建像元胞自动机这样的东西总是相当困难的，这种经典系统的类型会产生一些紧急行为，但它们总是有点脆弱，有点有限。

segments.text: Now we are maybe on the cusp in the next few years, five, 10 years of having AI systems that can truly create around your imagination, can dynamically change the story and storytell the narrative around and make it dramatic no matter what you end up choosing.

translation.text: 现在，我们可能正处于未来几年、五年、十年的风口浪尖，人工智能系统可以真正围绕你的想象力进行创造，可以动态地改变故事并讲述周围的叙述，并使其变得戏剧化，无论你最终选择什么。

segments.text: So it’s like the ultimate choose your own adventure sort of game. And I think maybe we are within reach, if you think of a kind of interactive version of Veo and then wind that forward five to 10 years and imagine how good it’s going to be.

translation.text: 所以这就像终极选择你自己的冒险游戏。我认为，如果你想到一种 Veo 的交互式版本，然后将其向前推进 5 到 10 年，想象一下它会有多好，也许我们就可以实现。

segments.text: Yeah. So you said a lot of super interesting stuff there. So one, the open world, built into that is a deep personalization the way you’ve described it.

translation.text: 是的。所以你在那里说了很多超级有趣的事情。因此，开放世界是一种深度个性化，就像你所描述的那样。

segments.text: So it’s not just that it’s open world, that you can open any door and there’ll be something there, it’s that it’s the choice of which door you open in an unconstrained way defines the worlds you see.

translation.text: 所以，这不仅仅是因为它是一个开放的世界，你可以打开任何一扇门，那里就会有一些东西，而是你以不受限制的方式打开哪扇门的选择定义了你所看到的世界。

segments.text: So some games try to do that, they give you choice, but it’s really just an illusion of choice because you only, like Stanley Parable, a game I actually played, it’s really, there’s a couple of doors and it really just takes you down a narrative.

translation.text: 所以有些游戏试图做到这一点，他们给你选择，但这实际上只是一种选择的幻觉，因为你只是，就像《斯坦利寓言》一样，我实际上玩过一款游戏，它真的有几扇门，它真的只是让你记下一个故事。

segments.text: Stanley Parable is a great video game. I recommend people play it, that in a meta way, mocks the illusion of choice, and there’s philosophical notions of free will and so on.

translation.text: 史丹利寓言是一款很棒的视频游戏。我建议人们玩它，以元的方式嘲笑选择的幻觉，并且有自由意志的哲学概念等等。

segments.text: But I do, one of my favorite games of Elder Scrolls is Daggerfall I believe, that they really played with a random generation of the dungeons of if you can step in and they give you this feeling of an open world.

translation.text: 但我确实，我最喜欢的《上古卷轴》游戏之一是《Daggerfall》，我相信他们真的玩了随机生成的地下城，如果你能介入，他们会给你一种开放世界的感觉。

segments.text: And there you mentioned interactivity. You don’t need to interact. That’s the first step because you don’t need to interact that much.

translation.text: 你在那里提到了交互性。你不需要互动。这是第一步，因为您不需要进行太多互动。

segments.text: You just, when you open the door, whatever you see is randomly generated for you. And that’s already an incredible experience because you might be the only person to ever see that.

translation.text: 你只是，当你打开门时，你看到的任何东西都是随机生成的。这已经是一次令人难以置信的体验，因为您可能是唯一见过这种情况的人。

segments.text: Yeah, exactly. But what you’d like is a little bit better than just sort of a random generation. So you’d like, and also better than a simple AB hard coded choice, right?

translation.text: 是的，完全正确。但你想要的比随机生成要好一点。所以你会想要，而且比简单的 AB 硬编码选择更好，对吧？

segments.text: That’s not really open world, as you say. It’s just giving you the illusion of choice. What you want to be able to do is potentially anything in that game environment.

translation.text: 正如你所说，这并不是真正的开放世界。它只是给你一种选择的错觉。您想要做的可能是该游戏环境中的任何事情。

segments.text: And I think the only way you can do that is to have generated systems, systems that will generate that on the fly. Of course, you can’t create infinite amounts of game assets.

translation.text: 我认为你能做到这一点的唯一方法就是拥有生成系统，能够动态生成它的系统。当然，你不可能创造无限量的游戏资产。

segments.text: It’s expensive enough already how AAA games are made today. And that was obvious to us back in the nineties when I was working on all these games.

translation.text: 如今制作 AAA 级游戏的成本已经够高的了。早在九十年代，当我开发所有这些游戏时，这一点对我们来说就很明显了。

segments.text: I think maybe Black & White was the game that I worked on early stages of that, that had still probably the best AI, learning AI, in it. It was an early reinforcement learning system that you were looking after this mythical creature and growing it and nurturing it.

translation.text: 我想也许《黑与白》是我在早期阶段开发的游戏，它可能仍然拥有最好的人工智能，即学习人工智能。这是一个早期的强化学习系统，你正在照顾这个神话生物，培育它并培育它。

segments.text: And depending how you treated it, it would treat the villagers in that world the same way. So if you were mean to it, it would be mean.

translation.text: 而根据你如何对待它，它也会以同样的方式对待那个世界的村民。所以如果你对它很刻薄，那就很刻薄了。

segments.text: If you were good, it would be protective. And so it was really a reflection of the way you played it. So actually all of the, I’ve been working on simulations and AI through the medium of games at the beginning of my career, and really the whole of what I do today,

translation.text: 如果你表现好的话，就会有保护作用。所以这确实反映了你的演奏方式。所以实际上，在我职业生涯的初期，我一直在通过游戏媒介从事模拟和人工智能工作，以及我今天所做的全部工作，

segments.text: it’s still a follow on from those early more hard coded ways of doing the AI to now fully general learning systems that are trying to achieve the same thing.

translation.text: 它仍然是早期更硬编码的人工智能方式的延续，现在是试图实现同样目标的完全通用的学习系统。

segments.text: Yeah, it is been interesting, hilarious, and fun to watch you and Elon obviously itching to create games because you’re both gamers.

translation.text: 是的，看到你和埃隆显然渴望创造游戏，这真是有趣、搞笑、有趣，因为你们都是游戏玩家。

segments.text: And one of the sad aspects of your incredible success in so many domains of science, like serious adult stuff, that you might not have time to really create a game, you might end up creating the tooling that others will create the game.

translation.text: 你在如此多的科学领域（比如严肃的成人事物）取得了令人难以置信的成功，其中一个可悲的方面是，你可能没有时间真正创建游戏，你最终可能会创建其他人将创建游戏的工具。

segments.text: You have to watch others create the thing you’ve always dreamed of. Do you think it’s possible you can somehow in your extremely busy schedule actually find time to create something like Black & White, an actual video game where you could make the childhood dream become reality?

translation.text: 你必须看着别人创造你一直梦想的东西。你是否认为你有可能在极其繁忙的日程中找到时间来创造像《黑与白》这样的东西，一个真正的视频游戏，让你童年的梦想成为现实？

segments.text: Well, there’s two things where I think about that is maybe with vibe coding as it gets better and there’s a possibility that I could, one could do that actually in your spare time.

translation.text: 嗯，我想到了两件事，一是随着氛围编码变得更好，二是我可以在业余时间真正做到这一点。

segments.text: So I’m quite excited about that as that would be my project if I got the time to do some vibe coding. I’m actually itching to do that. And then the other thing is maybe it’s a sabbatical after AGI has been safely stewarded into the world and delivered into the world.

translation.text: 所以我对此感到非常兴奋，因为如果我有时间做一些氛围编码，这将是我的项目。我其实很想这么做。另一件事是，在 AGI 被安全地托管到世界并交付给世界之后，这可能是一个休假。

segments.text: That, and then working on my physics theory as we talked about at the beginning, those would be my two post-AGI projects, let’s call it that way.

translation.text: 然后，正如我们在开始时讨论的那样，研究我的物理理论，这将是我的两个后 AGI 项目，让我们这样称呼它。

segments.text: I would love to see which you choose, solving the problem that some of the smartest people in human history contended with, P equals NP, or creating a cool video game.

translation.text: 我很想看看你会选择哪一个，是解决人类历史上一些最聪明的人所面临的问题，P 等于 NP，还是创造一款很酷的视频游戏。

segments.text: But in my world, they’d be related because it would be an open world simulated game as realistic as possible. So what is the universe? That’s speaking to the same question and P equals NP.

translation.text: 但在我的世界里，它们是相关的，因为这将是一个尽可能真实的开放世界模拟游戏。那么宇宙是什么？这是同一个问题，P 等于 NP。

segments.text: I think all these things are related, at least in my mind.

translation.text: 我认为所有这些事情都是相关的，至少在我看来是这样。

segments.text: I mean in a really serious way, video games sometimes are looked down upon as just this fun side activity.

translation.text: 我的意思是，以一种非常严肃的方式，电子游戏有时被视为只是一种有趣的副业活动。

segments.text: But especially as AI does more and more of the difficult, boring tasks, something we in modern world called work, video games is the thing in which we may find meaning, in which we may find what to do with our time.

translation.text: 但尤其是当人工智能承担越来越多困难、无聊的任务时，我们在现代世界称之为工作，视频游戏是我们可以找到意义的东西，我们可以在其中找到如何打发时间的东西。

segments.text: You could create incredibly rich, meaningful experiences. That’s what human life is. And then in video games, you can create more sophisticated, more diverse ways of living.

translation.text: 您可以创造极其丰富、有意义的体验。这就是人类的生活。然后在视频游戏中，你可以创造更复杂、更多样化的生活方式。

segments.text: Right? That’s the point?

translation.text: 正确的？这就是重点吗？

segments.text: I think so. I mean, those of us who love games and I still do is it’s almost can let your imagination run wild, right?

translation.text: 我想是的。我的意思是，对于我们这些热爱游戏的人来说，我仍然喜欢它，因为它几乎可以让你的想象力尽情发挥，对吗？

segments.text: I used to love games and working on games so much because it’s the fusion, especially in the nineties and early two thousands, the sort of golden era and maybe the eighties of the games industry.

translation.text: 我曾经非常喜欢游戏并从事游戏工作，因为它是融合，特别是在九十年代和两千世纪初，游戏行业的黄金时代，也许是八十年代。

segments.text: And it was all being discovered. New genres were being discovered. We weren’t just making games, we felt we were creating a new entertainment medium that never existed before.

translation.text: 而这一切都被发现了。新的流派不断被发现。我们不只是制作游戏，我们觉得我们正在创造一种以前从未存在过的新娱乐媒体。

segments.text: Especially with these open world games and simulation games where you, as the player, were co-creating the story. There’s no other media, entertainment media, where you do that, where you as the audience actually co-create the story.

translation.text: 尤其是在这些开放世界游戏和模拟游戏中，你作为玩家，正在共同创造故事。没有其他媒体、娱乐媒体可以让你作为观众真正共同创造故事。

segments.text: And of course now with multiplayer games as well, it can be a very social activity and can explore all kinds of interesting worlds in that.

translation.text: 当然，现在也有了多人游戏，它可以是一项非常社交的活动，可以探索其中各种有趣的世界。

segments.text: But on the other hand, it’s very important to also enjoy and experience the physical world. But the question is then I think we’re going to have to confront the question again of what is the fundamental nature of reality?

translation.text: 但另一方面，享受和体验物质世界也非常重要。但问题是，我认为我们将不得不再次面对这样的问题：现实的基本本质是什么？

segments.text: What is going to be the difference between these increasingly realistic simulations and multiplayer ones and emergent and what we do in the real world?

translation.text: 这些越来越现实的模拟和多人游戏以及新兴的模拟和我们在现实世界中所做的事情之间会有什么区别？

segments.text: Yeah, there’s clearly a huge amount of value to experiencing the real world, nature. There’s also a huge amount of value in experiencing other humans directly in person the way we’re sitting here today, but we need to really scientifically rigorously answer the question why and which aspect of that can be mapped into the virtual world.

translation.text: 是的，体验现实世界、自然显然具有巨大的价值。以我们今天坐在这里的方式直接亲身体验其他人类也具有巨大的价值，但我们需要真正科学地、严格地回答为什么以及哪些方面可以映射到虚拟世界的问题。

segments.text: Exactly.

translation.text: 确切地。

segments.text: It’s not enough to say, “Yeah, you should go touch grass and hang out in nature.” It’s like why exactly is that valuable?

translation.text: 仅仅说“是的，你应该去触摸草地并在大自然中闲逛”是不够的。就像为什么那么有价值？

segments.text: Yes. And I guess that’s maybe the thing that’s been haunting me or obsessing me from the beginning of my career. If you think about all the different things I’ve done, they’re all related in that way.

translation.text: 是的。我想这可能是从我职业生涯之初就一直困扰着我或让我着迷的事情。如果你想想我做过的所有不同的事情，它们都是以这种方式相关的。

segments.text: The simulation, nature of reality, and what is the bounds of what can be modeled.

translation.text: 模拟、现实的本质以及可建模的范围。

segments.text: Sorry for the ridiculous question, but so far, what is the greatest video game of all time? What’s up there?

translation.text: 抱歉问了这个荒谬的问题，但到目前为止，有史以来最伟大的视频游戏是什么？那里有什么？

segments.text: Well, my favorite one of all time is Civilization, I have to say. That was the Civilization I and Civilization II, my favorite games of all time.

translation.text: 嗯，我不得不说，我最喜欢的就是《文明》。那就是《文明一》和《文明二》，我一直以来最喜欢的游戏。

segments.text: I can only assume you’ve avoided the most recent one because it would probably, that would be your sabbatical. You would disappear.

translation.text: 我只能假设你已经避开了最近的一次，因为那很可能是你的休假。你就会消失。

segments.text: Yes, exactly. They take a lot of time, these Civilization games, so I’ve got to be careful with them.

translation.text: 是的，完全正确。这些《文明》游戏需要花费很多时间，所以我必须小心对待它们。

segments.text: Fun question. You and Elon seem to be somehow solid gamers. Is there a connection between being great at gaming and being great leaders of AI companies?

translation.text: 有趣的问题。你和埃隆似乎都是可靠的游戏玩家。擅长游戏和成为人工智能公司的伟大领导者之间有联系吗？

segments.text: I don’t know. It’s an interesting one. I mean, we both love games and it’s interesting, he wrote games as well to start off with.

translation.text: 我不知道。这是一件有趣的事。我的意思是，我们都喜欢游戏，这很有趣，他一开始也写游戏。

segments.text: Probably, especially in the era I grew up in where home computers just became a thing in the late eighties and nineties, especially in the UK, I had a spectrum and then a Commodore Amiga 500, which was my favorite computer ever.

translation.text: 可能，尤其是在我成长的时代，家用电脑在八十年代末和九十年代才刚刚流行，尤其是在英国，我有一个频谱，然后是 Commodore Amiga 500，这是我最喜欢的电脑。

segments.text: And that’s why I learned all my programming. And of course, it’s a very fun thing to program, is to program games. So I think it’s a great way to learn programming, probably still is.

translation.text: 这就是我学习所有编程的原因。当然，编程是一件非常有趣的事情，就是编写游戏。所以我认为这是学习编程的好方法，可能仍然是。

segments.text: And then of course, I immediately took it in directions of AI and simulations, so I was able to express my interest in games and my wider scientific interests all together.

translation.text: 当然，我立即将其转向人工智能和模拟方向，因此我能够同时表达我对游戏的兴趣和更广泛的科学兴趣。

segments.text: And my sort of wider scientific interests all together. And then the final thing I think that’s great about games is it fuses artistic design, art, with the most cutting edge programming.

translation.text: 还有我更广泛的科学兴趣。我认为游戏的最后一点是它融合了艺术设计、艺术和最前沿的编程。

segments.text: So again, in the nineties, all of the most interesting technical advances were happening in gaming, whether that was AI, graphics, physics engines, hardware, even GPUs of course were designed for gaming originally.

translation.text: 再说一遍，在九十年代，所有最有趣的技术进步都发生在游戏领域，无论是人工智能、图形、物理引擎、硬件，当然甚至 GPU 最初都是为游戏设计的。

segments.text: So everything that was pushing computing forward in the nineties was due to gaming. So interestingly, that was where the forefront of research was going on and it was this incredible fusion with art.

translation.text: 因此，九十年代推动计算发展的一切都归功于游戏。有趣的是，这就是最前沿的研究正在进行的地方，也是与艺术令人难以置信的融合。

segments.text: Graphics, but also music, and just the whole new media of storytelling. And I love that. For me, it’s this sort of multidisciplinary kind of effort is again something I’ve enjoyed my whole life.

translation.text: 图形，还有音乐，以及讲故事的全新媒体。我喜欢这样。对我来说，这种多学科的努力又是我一生所享受的。

segments.text: I have to ask you, I almost forgot about one of the many, and I would say one of the most incredible things recently that somehow didn’t yet get enough attention is AlphaEvolve.

translation.text: 我不得不问你，我几乎忘记了其中之一，我想说最近最令人难以置信的事情之一，但不知何故尚未得到足够的关注，那就是 AlphaEvolve。

segments.text: We talked about Evolution a little bit, but it’s the Google DeepMind system that evolves algorithms. Are these kinds of Evolution-like techniques promising as a component of future super intelligence systems?

translation.text: 我们稍微讨论了进化，但进化算法的是 Google DeepMind 系统。这些类似进化的技术是否有希望成为未来超级智能系统的组成部分？

segments.text: So for people who don’t know, it’s kind of, I don’t know if it’s fair to say it’s LLM guided Evolution search because Evolution algorithms are doing the search and LLMs are telling you where.

translation.text: 所以对于那些不知道的人来说，我不知道说这是大语言模型（LLM）引导的进化搜索是否公平，因为进化算法正在进行搜索，大语言模型（LLM）告诉你在哪里。

segments.text: Yes. Yes, exactly. So LLMs are kind of proposing some possible solutions and then you use evolutionary computing on top to find some novel part of the search space.

translation.text: 是的。是的，完全正确。所以大语言模型（LLM）是提出一些可能的解决方案，然后你在上面使用进化计算来找到搜索空间的一些新颖部分。

segments.text: So actually I think it’s an example of very promising directions where you combine LLMs or foundation models with other computational techniques.

translation.text: 所以实际上我认为这是一个非常有前途的方向的例子，你可以将大语言模型（LLM）或基础模型与其他计算技术相结合。

segments.text: Evolutionary methods is one, but you could also imagine Monte Carlo tree search. Basically many types of search algorithms or reasoning algorithms sort of on top of or using the foundation models as a basis.

translation.text: 进化方法是其中之一，但您也可以想象蒙特卡罗树搜索。基本上，许多类型的搜索算法或推理算法都建立在基础模型之上或使用基础模型作为基础。

segments.text: So I actually think there’s quite a lot of interesting things to be discovered probably with these sort of hybrid systems, let’s call them.

translation.text: 所以我实际上认为，通过这些混合系统，我们可能会发现很多有趣的事情，让我们称之为混合系统。

segments.text: But not to romanticize Evolution.

translation.text: 但不要将进化论浪漫化。

segments.text: Yeah.

translation.text: 是的。

segments.text: I’m only human, but you think there’s some value in whatever that mechanism is? Because we already talked about natural systems. Do you think where there’s a lot of low-hanging fruit of us understanding, being able to model, being able to simulate Evolution and then using that, whatever we understand about that nature-inspired mechanism, to then do search better and better and better?

translation.text: 我只是人类，但你认为无论这个机制是什么都有其价值吗？因为我们已经讨论过自然系统。你认为我们理解、能够建模、能够模拟进化，然后使用它，无论我们对自然启发机制的理解是什么，有很多唾手可得的成果，然后越来越好地进行搜索？

segments.text: Yes. So if you think about, again, breaking down the solar systems we’ve built to their really fundamental core, you’ve got the model of the underlying dynamics of the system.

translation.text: 是的。因此，如果你再次考虑一下，将我们构建的太阳系分解到其真正的基本核心，你就会得到系统底层动力学的模型。

segments.text: And then if you want to discover something new, something novel that hasn’t been seen before, then you need some kind of search process on top to take you to a novel of the search space.

translation.text: 然后，如果你想发现一些新的东西，一些以前从未见过的新颖的东西，那么你需要某种顶部的搜索过程来带你进入搜索空间的小说。

segments.text: And you can do that in a number of ways. Evolutionary computing is one. With AlphaGo, we just use Monte Carlo Tree Search and that’s what found move 37, the new never seen before strategy in Go.

translation.text: 您可以通过多种方式做到这一点。进化计算就是其中之一。对于 AlphaGo，我们只使用蒙特卡罗树搜索，这就是找到第37手的方法，这是围棋中以前从未见过的新策略。

segments.text: And so that’s how you can go beyond potentially what is already known. So the model can model everything that you currently know about, all the data that you currently have.

translation.text: 这就是你如何能够超越已知的范围。因此，该模型可以对您当前了解的所有内容以及您当前拥有的所有数据进行建模。

segments.text: But then how do you go beyond that? So that starts to speak about the ideas of creativity.

translation.text: 但你如何超越这个范围呢？那么这就开始谈论创造力的想法了。

segments.text: How can these systems create something new? In fact discover something new? Obviously this is super relevant for scientific discovery or pushing met science and medicine forward, which we want to do with these systems.

translation.text: 这些系统如何创造新事物？事实上发现一些新东西吗？显然，这与科学发现或推动气象科学和医学向前发展非常相关，我们希望利用这些系统来实现这一点。

segments.text: And you can actually bolt on some fairly simple search systems on top of these models and get you into a new region of space. Of course, you also have to make sure that you’re not searching that space totally randomly.

translation.text: 实际上，您可以在这些模型之上安装一些相当简单的搜索系统，然后进入一个新的空间区域。当然，您还必须确保您不是完全随机地搜索该空间。

segments.text: It would be too big. So you have to have some objective function that you’re trying to optimize and hill climb towards and that guides that search.

translation.text: 它太大了。因此，你必须有一些目标函数，你要尝试优化和爬山，并指导搜索。

segments.text: But there’s some mechanism of Evolution that are interesting maybe in the space of programs. But then the space of programs an extremely important space, because you can probably generalize to everything.

translation.text: 但在程序领域，进化的某些机制可能很有趣。但程序空间是一个极其重要的空间，因为你可能可以推广到一切。

segments.text: But for example, mutation. So it’s not just Monte Carlo Tree Search where it’s like a search. You could every once in a while-

translation.text: 但例如，突变。因此，蒙特卡罗树搜索不仅仅是搜索。你可以每隔一段时间-

segments.text: Combine things.

translation.text: 结合事物。

segments.text: Combine things?

translation.text: 结合事物？

segments.text: Yeah.

translation.text: 是的。

segments.text: Things, like the components of a thing.

translation.text: 事物，就像事物的组成部分。

segments.text: Yes.

translation.text: 是的。

segments.text: So then what Evolution is really good at is not just the natural selection, it’s combining things and building increasingly complex hierarchical systems.

translation.text: 那么进化真正擅长的不仅仅是自然选择，它还结合事物并构建日益复杂的层次系统。

segments.text: So that component is super interesting, especially with AlphaEvolve and the space of programs.

translation.text: 所以这个组件非常有趣，尤其是 AlphaEvolve 和程序空间。

segments.text: Yeah, exactly. So you can get a bit of an extra property out of evolutionary systems, which is some new emerging capability may come about, right?

translation.text: 是的，完全正确。所以你可以从进化系统中获得一些额外的属性，这是可能会出现的一些新的能力，对吗？

segments.text: Of course like happened with life, interestingly with naive, traditional evolutionary computing methods without LLMs and the modern AI, the problem with them, they were very well studied in the nineties and early two thousands and some promising results, but the problem was they could never work out how to evolve new properties,

translation.text: 当然，就像生活中发生的那样，有趣的是，没有大语言模型（LLM）和现代人工智能的天真、传统的进化计算方法，它们的问题是，它们在九十年代和两千年初期得到了很好的研究，并取得了一些有希望的结果，但问题是他们永远无法弄清楚如何进化新的属性，

segments.text: new emerging properties. You always had a sort of subset of the properties that you put into the system, but maybe if we combine them with these foundation models, perhaps we can overcome that limitation.

translation.text: 新出现的属性。你总是有一些属性的子集放入系统中，但如果我们将它们与这些基础模型结合起来，也许我们可以克服这个限制。

segments.text: Obviously naturally evolution clearly did. It did evolve new capabilities. So bacteria to where we are now.

translation.text: 显然，自然进化显然做到了。它确实发展出了新的能力。所以细菌到了我们现在的位置。

segments.text: So clearly that it must be possible with evolutionary systems to generate new patterns, going back to the first thing we talked about and new capabilities and emerging properties, and maybe we’re on the cusp of discovering how to do that.

translation.text: 很明显，进化系统一定有可能产生新的模式，回到我们谈论的第一件事以及新的能力和新兴的属性，也许我们正处于发现如何做到这一点的风口浪尖。

segments.text: Yeah, listen, AlphaEvolve is one of the coolest things I’ve ever seen. I’ve on my desk at home, most of my time is spent on that computer is just programming.

translation.text: 是的，听着，AlphaEvolve 是我见过的最酷的东西之一。我在家里的办公桌上，大部分时间都花在了那台电脑上，只是编程。

segments.text: And next to the three screens is a skull of a Tiktaalik, which is one of the early organisms that crawled out of the water onto land.

translation.text: 三个屏幕旁边是提塔利克鱼的头骨，提塔利克鱼是最早从水中爬到陆地上的生物之一。

segments.text: And I just kind of watch that little guy. It’s like whatever the competition mechanism of Evolution is, it’s quite incredible.

translation.text: 我只是看着那个小家伙。就好像进化论的竞争机制是什么，这真是太不可思议了。

segments.text: Yes.

translation.text: 是的。

segments.text: It’s truly, truly incredible. Now whether that’s exactly the thing we need to do to do our search, but never dismiss the power of nature, what it did here.

translation.text: 这真的非常非常令人难以置信。现在，这是否正是我们进行搜索所需要做的事情，但永远不要忽视自然的力量，它在这里所做的一切。

segments.text: And it’s amazing, which is a relatively simple algorithm, right? Effectively, and it can generate all of this immense complexity emerges obviously running over 4 billion years of time.

translation.text: 令人惊奇的是，这是一个相对简单的算法，对吧？实际上，它可以产生所有这些巨大的复杂性，显然是在 40 亿年的时间里出现的。

segments.text: But you can think about that as again, a search process that ran over the physics substrate of the universe for a long amount of computational time, but then it generated all this incredible rich diversity.

translation.text: 但你可以再次思考这一点，一个搜索过程在宇宙的物理基础上运行了很长的计算时间，但随后它产生了所有这些令人难以置信的丰富多样性。

segments.text: So many questions I want to ask you. So one, you do have a dream, one of the natural systems you want to try to model is a cell.

translation.text: 很多问题我想问你。所以，你确实有一个梦想，你想要尝试建模的自然系统之一是细胞。

segments.text: That’s a beautiful dream. I could ask you about that. I also just for that purpose on the AI scientist front just broadly, so there’s a essay from Daniel Cocotaglio, Scott Alexander and others that online steps along the way to get to ASI and it has a lot of interesting ideas in it,

translation.text: 那是一个美丽的梦。我可以问你这个问题。我也只是为了这个目的，在人工智能科学家方面广泛地进行，所以 Daniel Cocotaglio、Scott Alexander 和其他人写了一篇文章，在线介绍了通往 ASI 的过程，其中有很多有趣的想法，

segments.text: one of which is including a superhuman coder and a superhuman AI researcher. And in that there’s a term of research taste that’s really interesting.

translation.text: 其中之一包括一名超人编码员和一名超人人工智能研究员。其中有一个非常有趣的研究品味术语。

segments.text: So in everything you’ve seen, do you think it’s possible for AI systems to have research taste to help you in the way that AI co-scientist does, to help steer human brilliant scientists and then potentially by itself to figure out what are the directions where you want to generate truly novel ideas?

translation.text: 那么，在你所看到的一切中，你认为人工智能系统有可能具有研究品味，以人工智能联合科学家的方式帮助你，帮助引导人类杰出的科学家，然后有可能自己找出你想要产生真正新颖的想法的方向是什么？

segments.text: That seems to be a really important component of how to do great science?

translation.text: 这似乎是如何进行伟大科学研究的一个非常重要的组成部分？

segments.text: Yeah, I think that’s going to be one of the hardest things to mimic or model is this idea of taste or judgment. I think that’s what separates the great scientists from the good scientists.

translation.text: 是的，我认为品味或判断的想法将是最难模仿或模仿的事情之一。我认为这就是伟大科学家和优秀科学家的区别。

segments.text: All professional scientists are good technically, otherwise they wouldn’t have made it that far in academia and things like that. But then do you have the taste to sniff out what the right direction is, what the right experiment is, what the right question is?

translation.text: 所有专业科学家的技术都很好，否则他们不会在学术界取得那么大的成就。但你是否有品味去嗅出什么是正确的方向、什么是正确的实验、什么是正确的问题？

segments.text: So picking the right question is the hardest part of science and making the right hypothesis. And that’s what today’s systems definitely they can’t do.

translation.text: 因此，选择正确的问题并做出正确的假设是科学中最困难的部分。而这正是当今的系统绝对无法做到的。

segments.text: So I often say it’s harder to come up with a conjecture, a really good conjecture than it is to solve it. So we may have systems soon that can solve pretty hard conjectures.

translation.text: 所以我经常说，提出一个猜想，一个真正好的猜想比解决它更难。因此，我们可能很快就会拥有可以解决相当困难的猜想的系统。

segments.text: A maths Olympiad problems, where Alpha Proof last year our system got silver medal in that really hard problems. Maybe eventually we’ll better solve a Millennium Prize kind of problem.

translation.text: 去年，我们的系统在数学奥林匹克竞赛中的 Alpha Proof 难题中获得了银牌。也许最终我们会更好地解决千禧年奖之类的问题。

segments.text: But could a system have come up with a conjecture worthy of study that someone like Terence Tao would’ve gone? “You know what, that’s a really deep question about the nature of maths or the nature of numbers or the nature of physics.”

translation.text: 但是，一个系统能否提出一个值得研究的猜想，认为像陶哲轩这样的人会离开呢？ “你知道吗，这是一个关于数学本质、数字本质或物理本质的非常深刻的问题。”

segments.text: And that is far harder type of creativity. And we don’t really know. Today’s systems clearly can’t do that.

translation.text: 这是一种更难的创造力。我们真的不知道。今天的系统显然无法做到这一点。

segments.text: And we’re not quite sure what that mechanism would be. This kind of leap of imagination like Einstein had when he came up with special relativity and then general relativity with the knowledge he had at the time.

translation.text: 我们不太确定这个机制是什么。这种想象力的飞跃，就像爱因斯坦用当时的知识提出狭义相对论，然后提出广义相对论一样。

segments.text: For conjecture, you want to come up with a thing that’s interesting, it’s amenable to proof?

translation.text: 对于猜想，你想提出一个有趣的东西，它可以被证明吗？

segments.text: Yes.

translation.text: 是的。

segments.text: So it’s easy to come up with a thing that’s extremely difficult. It’s easy to come up with a thing that’s extremely easy, but at that very edge-

translation.text: 所以想出一件极其困难的事情很容易。想出一件极其简单的事情很容易，但就在那个边缘——

segments.text: That sweet spot of basically advancing the science and splitting the hypothesis space into two, ideally. Right? Whether if it’s true or not true, you’ve learned something really useful and that’s hard.

translation.text: 理想情况下，基本上推进科学发展并将假设空间一分为二的最佳点。正确的？不管这是真的还是假的，你都学到了一些真正有用的东西，但这很难。

segments.text: And making something that’s also falsifiable and within the technologies that you currently have available. So it’s a very creative process, actually.

translation.text: 并在你当前可用的技术范围内做出一些可证伪的东西。所以这实际上是一个非常有创意的过程。

segments.text: A highly creative process that I think just a kind of naive search on top of a model won’t be enough for that.

translation.text: 这是一个高度创造性的过程，我认为仅仅在模型之上进行简单的搜索是不够的。

segments.text: The idea of splitting the hypothesis space in two is super interesting. So I’ve heard you say that there’s basically no failure or failure is extremely valuable if you construct the questions right, if you construct the experiments right, if you design them right, that failure or success are both useful, so perhaps because it splits the hypothesis basically in two, it’s like a binary search?

translation.text: 将假设空间一分为二的想法非常有趣。所以我听你说，基本上没有失败，或者失败是非常有价值的，如果你正确构建问题，如果你正确构建实验，如果你设计正确，那么失败或成功都是有用的，所以也许因为它基本上将假设一分为二，就像二分搜索？

segments.text: Yes, that’s right. So when you do real Blue Sky research, there’s no such thing as failure really. As long as you are picking experiments and hypotheses that meaningfully split the hypothesis space and you learn something.

translation.text: 是的，没错。因此，当你进行真正的蓝天研究时，真的不存在失败这样的事情。只要你选择有意义地分割假设空间的实验和假设，你就能学到一些东西。

segments.text: You can learn something kind of equally valuable from an experiment that doesn’t work. That should tell you if you’ve designed the experiment well and your hypotheses are interesting, it should tell you a lot about where to go next.

translation.text: 你可以从无效的实验中学到同样有价值的东西。这应该告诉你如果你的实验设计得很好并且你的假设很有趣，它应该告诉你很多关于下一步该做什么的信息。

segments.text: And then you’re effectively doing a search process and using that information in very helpful ways.

translation.text: 然后您就可以有效地进行搜索过程并以非常有用的方式使用该信息。

segments.text: So to go to your dream of modeling a cell, what are the big challenges that lay ahead for us to make that happen?

translation.text: 那么，为了实现细胞建模的梦想，我们面临哪些巨大挑战？

segments.text: We should maybe highlight that in AlphaFold, I mean there’s just so many leaps. So AlphaFold solved, if it’s fair to say, protein folding.

translation.text: 我们也许应该强调一点，在 AlphaFold 中，我的意思是有太多的飞跃。公平地说，AlphaFold 解决了蛋白质折叠问题。

segments.text: And there’s so many incredible things we could talk about there, including the open sourcing, everything you’ve released AlphaFold 3 is doing protein, RNA, DNA interactions, which is super complicated and fascinating.

translation.text: 我们可以在那里谈论很多令人难以置信的事情，包括开源，你发布的 AlphaFold 3 的所有内容都在进行蛋白质、RNA、DNA 相互作用，这是超级复杂和令人着迷的。

segments.text: It’s amenable to modeling. AlphaGenome predicts how small genetic changes if we think about single mutations, how they link to actual function.

translation.text: 它适合建模。如果我们考虑单个突变，AlphaGenome 可以预测基因变化有多小，以及它们如何与实际功能联系起来。

segments.text: So it seems like it’s creeping along to sophisticated to much more complicated things like a cell. But a cell has a lot of really complicated components.

translation.text: 因此，它似乎正在逐渐变得复杂，变得更加复杂，比如细胞。但细胞有很多非常复杂的组件。

segments.text: So what I’ve tried to do throughout my career is I have these really grand dreams and then I try to, as you’ve noticed, but I try to break them down.

translation.text: 所以，在我的整个职业生涯中，我一直在努力做的是，我有这些非常宏伟的梦想，然后我会努力实现，正如你所注意到的，但我会努力打破它们。

segments.text: It’s easy to have a kind of crazily ambitious dream, but the trick is how do you break it down into manageable, achievable, interim steps that are meaningful and useful in their own right?

translation.text: 拥有一种疯狂的雄心勃勃的梦想很容易，但诀窍是如何将其分解为可管理、可实现、本身有意义且有用的临时步骤？

segments.text: And so Virtual Cell, which is what I call the project of modeling a cell, I’ve had this idea of wanting to do that for maybe more like 25 years.

translation.text: 因此，Virtual Cell，也就是我所说的细胞建模项目，我想这样做的想法可能已经有 25 年了。

segments.text: And I used to talk with Paul Nurse, who is a bit of a mentor of mine in biology. He runs the founded the Crick Institute and won the Nobel Prize in 2001.

translation.text: 我曾经和保罗·纳斯（Paul Nurse）交谈过，他可以说是我在生物学方面的导师。他创办了克里克研究所，并于 2001 年获得诺贝尔奖。

segments.text: We’ve been talking about it since the nineties, and I used to come back to it every five years. It’s like, what would you need to model the full internals of a cell so that you could do experiments on the virtual cell and what those experiment in silico and those predictions would be useful for you to save you a lot of time in the wet lab.

translation.text: 我们从九十年代起就一直在谈论这个问题，我过去每五年都会回顾一下这个问题。就像，你需要什么来模拟细胞的完整内部结构，以便你可以在虚拟细胞上进行实验，以及这些计算机实验和这些预测对你有用，可以节省你在湿实验室中的大量时间。

segments.text: That would be the dream.

translation.text: 那将是梦想。

segments.text: Maybe you could a hundred x speed up experiments by doing most of it in silico the search in silico, and then you do the validation step in the wet lab.

translation.text: 也许您可以通过在计算机中进行大部分搜索（在计算机中进行搜索）来将实验速度加快一百倍，然后在湿实验室中进行验证步骤。

segments.text: That’s the dream. But maybe now, finally, so I was trying to build these components, AlphaFold being one, that would allow you eventually to model the full interaction, a full simulation of a cell, and I’d probably start with a yeast cell.

translation.text: 这就是梦想。但也许现在，最后，我试图构建这些组件，AlphaFold 就是其中之一，它最终可以让你对完整的交互进行建模，对细胞进行完整的模拟，我可能会从酵母细胞开始。

segments.text: And partly that’s what Paul Nurse studied because the yeast cell is like a full organism, that’s a single cell. So it’s the kind of simplest single cell organism.

translation.text: 保罗·纳斯所研究的部分内容是因为酵母细胞就像一个完整的有机体，是一个单细胞。所以它是一种最简单的单细胞生物。

segments.text: And so it’s not just a cell, it’s a full organism.

translation.text: 所以它不仅仅是一个细胞，它是一个完整的有机体。

segments.text: And yeast is very well understood. And so that would be a good candidate for a kind of full simulated model. Now AlphaFold is the solution to the kind of static picture of what does a 3D structure protein look like?

translation.text: 酵母是很好理解的。因此，这将是一种完整模拟模型的良好候选者。现在 AlphaFold 是 3D 结构蛋白质的静态图片的解决方案？

segments.text: A static picture of it. But we know that biology, all the interesting things happen with the dynamics, the interactions, and that’s what AlphaFold 3 is, the first step towards is modeling those interactions.

translation.text: 它的静态图片。但我们知道，在生物学中，所有有趣的事情都是通过动力学、相互作用而发生的，这就是 AlphaFold 3 的本质，第一步就是对这些相互作用进行建模。

segments.text: So first of all, pair wise proteins with proteins, proteins with RNA and DNA. But then the next step after that would be modeling maybe a whole pathway, maybe like the tour pathway that’s involved in cancer or something like this.

translation.text: 首先，将蛋白质与蛋白质、蛋白质与 RNA 和 DNA 配对。但接下来的下一步可能是对整个路径进行建模，也许像与癌症或类似的东西有关的旅行路径。

segments.text: And then eventually you might be able to model a whole cell.

translation.text: 最终你也许能够对整个细胞进行建模。

segments.text: Also, there’s another complexity here that stuff in a cell happens at different time scales. Is that tricky? Protein folding is super fast.

translation.text: 此外，这里还有另一个复杂性，即细胞中的物质发生在不同的时间尺度。这很棘手吗？蛋白质折叠速度超快。

segments.text: I don’t know all the biological mechanisms, but some of them take a long time. And so the levels of interaction has a different temporal scale that you have to be able to model.

translation.text: 我不知道所有的生物学机制，但其中一些需要很长时间。因此，交互级别具有不同的时间尺度，您必须能够对其进行建模。

segments.text: So that would be hard. So you’d probably need several simulated systems that can interact at these different temporal dynamics, or at least maybe it’s like a hierarchical system so you can jump up or down the different temporal stages.

translation.text: 所以那会很难。因此，您可能需要几个可以在这些不同的时间动态下交互的模拟系统，或者至少可能像一个分层系统，这样您就可以在不同的时间阶段上跳或下跳。

segments.text: So can you avoid… One of the challenges here is not avoid simulating, for example, the quantum mechanical aspects of any of this, right?

translation.text: 那么你能避免……这里的挑战之一不是避免模拟，例如，其中任何一个的量子力学方面，对吧？

segments.text: You want to not over model. You can skip ahead to just model the really high level things that get you a really good estimate of what’s going to happen.

translation.text: 你不想过度建模。您可以直接跳到对真正高级的事物进行建模，这可以让您对将要发生的情况有一个很好的估计。

segments.text: Yes. So you got to make a decision when you’re modeling any natural system, what is the cutoff level of the granularity that you’re going to model it to?

translation.text: 是的。因此，在对任何自然系统进行建模时，您必须做出决定，您要对其进行建模的粒度的截止级别是多少？

segments.text: And then it captures the dynamics that you’re interested in. So probably for a cell I would hope that would be the protein level, and that one wouldn’t have to go down to the atomic level.

translation.text: 然后它捕获您感兴趣的动态。因此，对于细胞来说，我可能希望是蛋白质水平，而不必深入到原子水平。

segments.text: So of course that’s where AlphaFold stock kicks in. So that would be kind of the basis, and then you’d build these higher level simulations that take those as building blocks and then you get the emergent behavior.

translation.text: 当然，这就是 AlphaFold 股票发挥作用的地方。所以这将是一种基础，然后你将构建这些更高级别的模拟，将它们作为构建块，然后你就会得到紧急行为。

segments.text: I apologize for the pothead questions ahead of time, but do you think we’ll be able to simulate a model, the origin of life?

translation.text: 我为提前提出的问题表示歉意，但你认为我们能够模拟生命起源的模型吗？

segments.text: So being able to simulate the first from non-living organisms, the birth of a living organism?

translation.text: 那么能够模拟第一个从非生物体，到生物体的诞生吗？

segments.text: I think that’s one of course one of the deepest and most fascinating questions. I love that area of biology. There’s people, there’s a great book by Nick Lane, one of the top experts in this area called The Ten Great Inventions of Evolution.

translation.text: 我认为这当然是最深刻、最有趣的问题之一。我喜欢生物学的那个领域。有人，尼克·莱恩（Nick Lane）写了一本很棒的书，他是该领域的顶级专家之一，名为《进化的十大发明》。

segments.text: I think it’s fantastic. And it also speaks to what the great filters might be, prior or are they ahead of us? I think they’re most likely in the past, if you read that book of how unlikely to go have any life at all.

translation.text: 我认为这太棒了。它还说明了伟大的过滤器可能是什么，先于我们还是领先于我们？如果你读过那本关于根本不可能拥有任何生活的书，我认为它们很可能已经过去了。

segments.text: And then single cell to multi-cell seems an unbelievably big jump that took a billion years, I think on earth to do, right? So it shows you how hard it was.

translation.text: 然后单细胞到多细胞似乎是一个令人难以置信的大跳跃，花了十亿年的时间，我想这在地球上是做不到的，对吗？所以它告诉你这是多么困难。

segments.text: Right? Bacteria were super happy for a very long time.

translation.text: 正确的？细菌在很长一段时间内都非常高兴。

segments.text: For a very long time before they captured mitochondria somehow, right? I don’t see why not, why AI couldn’t help with that. Some kind of simulation.

translation.text: 在他们以某种方式捕获线粒体之前很长一段时间，对吧？我不明白为什么不，为什么人工智能对此无能为力。某种模拟。

segments.text: Again, it’s a bit of a search process through a combinatorial space. Here’s all the chemical soup that you start with, the primordial soup, that maybe was on earth near these hot vents.

translation.text: 同样，这有点像通过组合空间进行搜索的过程。这是你开始使用的所有化学汤，原始汤，可能就在地球上这些热喷口附近。

segments.text: Here’s some initial conditions. Can you generate something that looks like a cell? So perhaps that would be a next stage after the virtual cell project is well, how could something like that emerge from the chemical soup?

translation.text: 这是一些初始条件。你能生成一些看起来像细胞的东西吗？因此，也许这将是虚拟细胞项目顺利完成后的下一个阶段，这样的东西怎么可能从化学汤中出现呢？

segments.text: Well, I would love it if there was a Move 37 for the origin of life. I think that’s one of the great mysteries. I think ultimately what we’ll figure out is their continuum.

translation.text: 好吧，如果生命起源有第 37 招，我会很高兴。我认为这是最大的谜团之一。我认为最终我们会弄清楚它们的连续体。

segments.text: There’s no such thing as a line between non-living and living. But if we can make that rigorous.

translation.text: 非生命与生命之间不存在界限。但如果我们能够做到严格的话。

segments.text: Yes.

translation.text: 是的。

segments.text: That the very thing from the Big Bang to today has been the same process. If you can break down that wall that we’ve constructed in our minds of the actual origin from non-living to living, and it’s not a line that it’s a continuum that connects physics and chemistry and biology.

translation.text: 从大爆炸到今天的事情都是同样的过程。如果你能打破我们在头脑中建造的从非生命到生命的实际起源的那堵墙，它就不是一条线，它是连接物理、化学和生物学的连续体。

segments.text: There’s no line.

translation.text: 没有线路。

segments.text: I mean, this is my whole reason why I worked on AI and AGI my whole life, because I think it can be the ultimate tool to help us answer these kinds of questions.

translation.text: 我的意思是，这就是我一生致力于人工智能和通用人工智能的全部原因，因为我认为它可以成为帮助我们回答此类问题的终极工具。

segments.text: And I don’t really understand why the average person doesn’t worry about this stuff more. How can we not have a good definition of life and not living a non-living and the nature of time and let alone consciousness and gravity and all these things and quantum mechanics weirdness?

translation.text: 我真的不明白为什么普通人不更担心这些事情。我们怎么能不能对生命有一个好的定义，而不是非生命和时间的本质，更不用说意识和重力以及所有这些东西和量子力学的怪异了？

segments.text: It’s just to me, I’ve always had this sort of screaming at me in my face and it’s getting louder. It’s like, what is going on here?

translation.text: 只是对我来说，我的脸上总是有人对我尖叫，而且声音越来越大。就像，这里发生了什么事？

segments.text: And I mean that in the deeper sense, the nature of reality, which has to be the ultimate question that would answer all of these things.

translation.text: 我的意思是，从更深层的意义上说，现实的本质，必须是回答所有这些问题的终极问题。

segments.text: It’s sort of crazy if you think about it. We can stare at each other and all these living things all the time. We can inspect it microscopes and take it apart almost down to the atomic level.

translation.text: 如果你仔细想想，这有点疯狂。我们可以一直凝视彼此以及所有这些生物。我们可以用显微镜检查它，​​并将其拆解到几乎原子水平。

segments.text: And yet we still can’t answer that clearly in a simple way. That question of how do you define living? It’s kind of amazing.

translation.text: 然而我们仍然无法用简单的方式清楚地回答这个问题。你如何定义生活这个问题？这真是太神奇了。

segments.text: Yeah, living, you can kind of talk your way out of thinking about. But consciousness, we have this very obviously subjective, conscious experience like we’re at the center of our own world and feels like something.

translation.text: 是的，活着，你可以通过谈论自己的方式来摆脱思考。但是意识，我们有这种非常明显主观的、有意识的体验，就像我们处于自己世界的中心，感觉就像是某种东西。

segments.text: And then how are you not screaming at the mystery of it all? I mean, but really humans have been contending with the mystery of the world around them for a long, long… There’s a lot of mysteries like what’s up with the sun and the rain?

translation.text: 那么你怎么能不对这一切的奥秘尖叫呢？我的意思是，但实际上，人类长期以来一直在与周围世界的奥秘作斗争……有很多奥秘，比如太阳和雨水是怎么回事？

segments.text: What’s that about? And then last year we had a lot of rain, and this year we don’t have rain. What did we do wrong?

translation.text: 那是关于什么的？去年我们下了很多雨，今年我们没有下雨。我们做错了什么？

segments.text: Humans have been asking that question for a long time.

translation.text: 人类长期以来一直在问这个问题。

segments.text: Exactly. So I guess we’ve developed a lot of mechanisms to cope with these deep mysteries that we can’t fully, we can see, but we can’t fully understand and we have to just get on with daily life.

translation.text: 确切地。所以我想我们已经开发出了很多机制来应对这些我们无法完全理解、我们可以看到、但我们无法完全理解的深层奥秘，我们只能继续日常生活。

segments.text: And we keep ourselves busy in a way. In a way, did we keep ourselves distracted?

translation.text: 我们在某种程度上让自己忙碌起来。在某种程度上，我们是否让自己分心了？

segments.text: I mean, weather is one of the most important questions of human history. We still, that’s the go-to small talk direction of the weather.

translation.text: 我的意思是，天气是人类历史上最重要的问题之一。我们仍然认为，这是天气的闲聊方向。

segments.text: Yes. Especially in England.

translation.text: 是的。尤其是在英格兰。

segments.text: And then which is famously is an extremely difficult system to model. And even that system, Google DeepMind has made progress on.

translation.text: 众所周知，这是一个极其难以建模的系统。即使是这个系统，谷歌 DeepMind 也取得了进展。

segments.text: Yes, we’ve created the best weather prediction systems in the world and they’re better than traditional fluid dynamics sort of systems that usually calculated on massive supercomputers takes days to calculate it.

translation.text: 是的，我们已经创建了世界上最好的天气预报系统，它们比传统的流体动力学系统更好，这些系统通常在大型超级计算机上需要几天的时间才能计算出来。

segments.text: And we’ve managed to model a lot of the weather dynamics with neural network systems, with our WeatherNet system. And again, it’s interesting that those kinds of dynamics can be modeled even though very complicated, almost bordering on chaotic systems in some cases.

translation.text: 我们已经成功地利用神经网络系统和 WeatherNet 系统对许多天气动态进行了建模。有趣的是，即使这些动态非常复杂，在某些情况下几乎接近混沌系统，也可以对其进行建模。

segments.text: A lot of the interesting aspects of that can be modeled by these neural network systems, including very recently we had cyclone prediction of where paths of hurricanes might go.

translation.text: 其中许多有趣的方面都可以通过这些神经网络系统进行建模，包括最近我们对飓风可能走向的路径进行了气旋预测。

segments.text: Of course, super useful, super important for the world and it’s super important to do that very timely and very quickly and as well as accurately.

translation.text: 当然，这对世界来说非常有用、非常重要，而且非常及时、非常快速、准确地做到这一点非常重要。

segments.text: And I think it’s very promising direction again, of simulating so that you can run forward predictions and simulations of very complicated real world systems.

translation.text: 我认为这又是一个非常有前途的方向，通过模拟，您可以对非常复杂的现实世界系统进行预测和模拟。

segments.text: I should mention that I’ve gotten a chance in Texas to meet a community of folks called the Storm Chasers. And what’s really incredible about them, I need to talk to them more, is they’re extremely tech-savvy because what they have to do is they have to use models to predict where the storm is.

translation.text: 我应该提到的是，我有机会在德克萨斯州见到了一个名为“风暴追逐者”的社区。他们真正令人难以置信的是，我需要与他们进行更多交谈，他们非常精通技术，因为他们要做的就是使用模型来预测风暴的位置。

segments.text: So it’s this beautiful mix of crazy enough to go into the eye of the storm and in order to protect your life and predict where the extreme events are going to be, they have to have increasingly sophisticated models of weather.

translation.text: 因此，这是一种足够疯狂的美丽组合，足以进入风暴眼，为了保护您的生命并预测极端事件将在哪里发生，他们必须拥有越来越复杂的天气模型。

segments.text: Yeah.

translation.text: 是的。

segments.text: It is a beautiful balance of being in it as living organisms and the cutting edge of science. They actually might be using DeepMind systems.

translation.text: 这是生物体与科学前沿之间的完美平衡。他们实际上可能正在使用 DeepMind 系统。

segments.text: Yeah. But hopefully they are. And I’d love to join them in one of those checks.

translation.text: 是的。但希望他们是。我很乐意和他们一起参加其中一张支票。

segments.text: They look amazing. Right. That’s great to actually experience it one time.

translation.text: 他们看起来棒极了。正确的。能够实际体验一次真是太好了。

segments.text: Exactly. And then also to experience the correct prediction where something will come and how it’s going to evolve.

translation.text: 确切地。然后还要体验正确的预测某件事将在哪里发生以及它将如何演变。

segments.text: It’s incredible. You’ve estimated that we’ll have AGI by 2030, so there’s interesting questions around that. How will we actually know that we got there and what may be the move quote, “Move 37” of AGI.

translation.text: 太不可思议了。你估计我们会在 2030 年实现 AGI，这带来了一些有趣的问题：我们究竟如何判断它已经实现？AGI 的“第37手”又会是什么？

segments.text: My estimate is sort of 50% chance by in the next five years, so by 2030 let’s say. So I think there’s a good chance that that could happen.

translation.text: 我的估计是在未来五年内，也就是到 2030 年，这种可能性有 50%。所以我认为这很有可能发生。

segments.text: Part of it is what is your definition of AGI? Of course people arguing about that now and mind’s quite a high bar and always has been of can we match the cognitive functions that the brain has?

translation.text: 其中一部分是你对 AGI 的定义是什么？当然，人们现在争论的问题是，头脑的门槛相当高，并且一直如此，我们能否与大脑具有的认知功能相匹配？

segments.text: So we know our brains are pretty much general Turing machines approximate, and of course we created incredible modern civilization with our minds. So that also speaks to how general the brain is.

translation.text: 所以我们知道我们的大脑几乎与通用图灵机相似，当然我们用我们的思想创造了令人难以置信的现代文明。这也说明了大脑的通用性。

segments.text: And for us to know we have a true AGI, we would have to make sure that it has all those capabilities. It isn’t kind of a jagged intelligence where some things, it’s really good at, like today’s systems, but other things it’s really flawed at.

translation.text: 为了让我们知道我们拥有真正的通用人工智能，我们必须确保它具有所有这些功能。它并不是一种参差不齐的智能，它在某些事情上确实很擅长，比如今天的系统，但在其他事情上它确实有缺陷。

segments.text: And that’s what we currently have with today’s systems. They’re not consistent. So you’d want that consistency of intelligence across the board.

translation.text: 这就是我们目前的系统所拥有的。它们并不一致。所以你会希望情报全面一致。

segments.text: And then we have some missing, I think, capabilities like the true invention capabilities and creativity that we were talking about earlier. So you’d want to see those.

translation.text: 我认为，我们还缺少一些能力，比如我们之前讨论的真正的发明能力和创造力。所以你想看看这些。

segments.text: How you test that? I think you just test it. One way to do it would be kind of brute force test of tens of thousands of cognitive tasks that we know that humans can do.

translation.text: 你如何测试？我认为你只是测试一下。一种方法是对我们知道人类可以完成的数以万计的认知任务进行强力测试。

segments.text: And maybe also make the system available to a few hundred of the world’s top experts, the Terence Taos of each subject area and give them a month or two and see if they can find an obvious flaw in the system.

translation.text: 也许还可以将该系统提供给数百名世界顶级专家（每个学科领域的特伦斯·陶斯），并给他们一两个月的时间，看看他们是否能找到系统中的明显缺陷。

segments.text: And if they can’t, then I think you can be pretty confident we have a fully general system.
