translation.text: 如果他们不能，那么我想你可以非常有信心我们拥有一个完全通用的系统。

segments.text: Maybe to push back a little bit, it seems like humans are really incredible as the intelligence improves across all domains to take it for granted, like you mentioned, Terence Tao, these brilliant experts.

translation.text: 也许退一步来说，人类似乎真的很不可思议，因为所有领域的智力都在提高，认为这是理所当然的，就像你提到的，陶哲轩，这些才华横溢的专家。

segments.text: They might quickly in a span of weeks, take for granted all the incredible things it can do and then focus in on, well, aha, right there.

translation.text: 他们可能会在几周内迅速认为它可以做的所有令人难以置信的事情是理所当然的，然后专注于，嗯，啊哈，就在那里。

segments.text: I consider myself, first of all, human. I identify as human. Some people listen to me talk and they’re like, “That guy is not good at talking the stuttering.”

translation.text: 我首先认为自己是一个人。我认为自己是人类。有些人听我说话，就会说：“这家伙说话不怎么会结巴。”

segments.text: So even humans have obvious across domains limits, even just outside of calc, mathematics and physics and so on.

translation.text: 因此，即使人类也有明显的跨领域限制，即使只是在计算、数学和物理等领域之外。

segments.text: I wonder if it will take something like a Move 37, so on the positive side versus a barrage of 10,000 cognitive tasks where it’ll be one or two where it’s like, holy shit, this is special.

translation.text: 我想知道是否需要像 Move 37 这样的东西，所以从积极的一面来看，与一万个认知任务相比，其中只有一两个会让人觉得，天啊，这很特别。

segments.text: So I think that. Exactly. So I think there’s the sort of blanket testing to just make sure you’ve got the consistency.

translation.text: 所以我认为。确切地。所以我认为需要进行全面测试来确保一致性。

segments.text: But I think there are the sort of lighthouse moments like the Move 37 that I would be looking for. So one would be inventing a new conjecture or a new hypothesis about physics like Einstein did.

translation.text: 但我认为有像 Move 37 这样的灯塔时刻是我所寻找的。因此，人们会像爱因斯坦那样发明一种关于物理学的新猜想或新假设。

segments.text: So maybe you could even run the back test of that very rigorously, have a cut-off of 1900 and then give the system everything that was written up to 1900 and then see if it could come up with special relativity and general relativity, right?

translation.text: 所以也许你甚至可以非常严格地进行回溯测试，以 1900 为截止点，然后给系统提供 1900 之前写入的所有内容，然后看看它是否可以得出狭义相对论和广义相对论，对吗？

segments.text: Like Einstein did. That would be an interesting test. Another one would be can it invent a game like Go?

translation.text: 就像爱因斯坦那样。那将是一个有趣的测试。另一个问题是它能发明像围棋这样的游戏吗？

segments.text: Go not just come up with Move 37, a new strategy, but can it invent a game that’s as deep as aesthetically beautiful, as elegant as Go?

translation.text: Go 不仅提出了第37手新策略，而且它能否发明一种像围棋一样有深度、美观、优雅的游戏？

segments.text: And those are the sorts of things I would be looking out for. And probably a system being able to do several of those things for it to be very general, not just one domain.

translation.text: 这些都是我会关注的事情。一个系统可能能够做其中几件事，因为它是非常通用的，而不仅仅是一个领域。

segments.text: And so I think that would be the signs at least that I would be looking for, that we’ve got a system that’s AGI level and then maybe to fill that out, you would also check their consistency, make sure there’s no holes in that system either.

translation.text: 所以我认为这至少是我要寻找的迹象，我们有一个 AGI 级别的系统，然后也许要填写它，你还要检查它们的一致性，确保该系统中也没有漏洞。

segments.text: Yeah, something like a new conjecture or scientific discovery. That would be a cool feeling.

translation.text: 是的，就像一个新的猜想或科学发现。那会是一种很酷的感觉。

segments.text: Yeah, that would be amazing. So it’s not just helping us do that, but actually coming up with something brand new.

translation.text: 是的，那太棒了。所以它不仅帮助我们做到这一点，而且实际上提出了一些全新的东西。

segments.text: And you would be in the room for that.

translation.text: 你也会在场。

segments.text: Absolutely.

translation.text: 绝对地。

segments.text: It would be probably two or three months before announcing it. And you would just be sitting there trying not to Tweet.

translation.text: 大概要过两三个月才会公布。而你只是坐在那里，尽量不发推文。

segments.text: Something like that. Exactly. It’s like what is this amazing new physics idea?

translation.text: 类似的事情。确切地。这就像这个令人惊奇的新物理想法是什么？

segments.text: And then we would probably check it with world experts in that domain and validate it and go through its workings. And I guess it would be explaining its workings too.

translation.text: 然后我们可能会与该领域的世界专家一起检查并验证它并检查其工作原理。我想它也会解释它的工作原理。

segments.text: Yeah. It would be an amazing moment.

translation.text: 是的。这将是一个美妙的时刻。

segments.text: Do you worry that we as humans, even expert humans, like you might miss it? Might miss-

translation.text: 您是否担心我们作为人类，甚至像您一样的专家，可能会错过它？可能会错过——

segments.text: Well, it may be pretty complicated. So it could be, the analogy I give there is I don’t think it will be totally mysterious to the best human scientists, but it may be a bit like, for example in chess,

translation.text: 嗯，这可能相当复杂。所以可能是这样，我给出的类比是，我不认为这对于最优秀的人类科学家来说是完全神秘的，但它可能有点像，例如在国际象棋中，

segments.text: if I was to talk to Garry Kasparov for Magnus Carlsen and play a game with them and they make a brilliant move, I might not be able to come up with that move.

translation.text: 如果我要与马格努斯·卡尔森的加里·卡斯帕罗夫交谈并与他们玩一场游戏，而他们做出了一个精彩的举动，我可能无法想出那个举动。

segments.text: But they could explain why afterwards that move made sense. And we would be to understand it to some degree, not to the level they do, but if they were good at explaining, which is actually part of intelligence too, is being able to explain in a simple way that what you’re thinking about,

translation.text: 但他们可以解释为什么后来这一举动是有意义的。我们会在某种程度上理解它，而不是达到他们的水平，但如果他们善于解释，这实际上也是智力的一部分，能够以简单的方式解释你正在思考的事情，

segments.text: I think that that will be very possible for the best human scientists.

translation.text: 我认为这对于最优秀的人类科学家来说是很有可能的。

segments.text: But I wonder, maybe you can educate me on the side of Go, I wonder if there’s moves from Magnus or Garry where they at first will dismiss it as a bad move?

translation.text: 但我想知道，也许你可以在围棋方面教育我，我想知道马格努斯或加里是否有一些举动，他们一开始会认为这是一个糟糕的举动？

segments.text: Yeah, sure, it could be. But then afterwards they’ll figure out with their intuition why this works. And then empirically, the nice thing about games is, one of the great things about games is it’s a sort of scientific test.

translation.text: 是的，当然，可能是这样。但之后他们会凭直觉弄清楚为什么这是有效的。然后从经验来看，游戏的好处是，游戏的伟大之处之一是它是一种科学测试。

segments.text: Do you win the game or not win? And then that tells you, okay, that move in the end was good, that strategy was good. And then you can go back and analyze that and explain even to yourself a little bit more why.

translation.text: 比赛你赢还是没赢？然后这告诉你，好吧，最终的举动是好的，策略是好的。然后你可以回过头来分析这一点，甚至向自己解释一下原因。

segments.text: Explore around it, and that’s how chess analysis and things like that works. So perhaps that’s why my brain works like that because I’ve been doing that since I was four and it’s sort of hardcore training in that way.

translation.text: 围绕它进行探索，这就是国际象棋分析之类的工作原理。所以也许这就是为什么我的大脑会这样工作，因为我从四岁起就一直这样做，这是一种硬核训练。

segments.text: But even now when I generate code, there is this kind of nuanced, fascinating contention that’s happening where I might at first identify as a set of generated code is incorrect in some interesting nuanced ways.

translation.text: 但即使是现在，当我生成代码时，也会发生这种微妙的、令人着迷的争论，我可能首先会认为一组生成的代码在某些有趣的细微差别方面是不正确的。

segments.text: But then I always have to ask the question, is there a deeper insight here that I’m the one who’s incorrect? And that’s going to, as the systems get more and more intelligent, you’re going to have to contend with that.

translation.text: 但我总是要问一个问题，是否有更深层次的洞察力表明我是错误的？随着系统变得越来越智能，你将不得不应对这个问题。

segments.text: It’s like, is this a bug or a feature, what you just came up with?

translation.text: 就像，这是你刚刚想出的一个错误还是一个功能？

segments.text: Yeah. And they’re going to be pretty complicated to do, but of course it will be, you can imagine also AI systems that are producing that code or whatever that is, and then human programmers looking at it, but also not unaided with the help of AI tools as well.

translation.text: 是的。做起来会非常复杂，但当然，你也可以想象人工智能系统正在生成代码或其他任何东西，然后人类程序员查看它，但也不能没有人工智能工具的帮助。

segments.text: So it’s going to be kind of an interesting, maybe different AI tools to the ones the monitoring tools are the ones that generated it.

translation.text: 因此，这将是一种有趣的、可能与生成它的监控工具不同的人工智能工具。

segments.text: So if we look at that AGI system, sorry to bring it back up, but AlphaEvolve, it’s super cool. So AlphaEvolve enables on the programming side, something like recursive self-improvement potentially.

translation.text: 因此，如果我们看看那个 AGI 系统，很抱歉将其重新启动，但是 AlphaEvolve，它非常酷。因此，AlphaEvolve 在编程方面实现了类似递归自我改进的潜力。

segments.text: If you can imagine what that AGI system, maybe not the first version, but a few versions beyond that, what does that actually look like?

translation.text: 如果你能想象那个 AGI 系统是什么，也许不是第一个版本，而是除此之外的几个版本，它实际上是什么样子？

segments.text: Do you think it’ll be simple? Do you think it’ll be something like a self-improving-

translation.text: 你认为这会很简单吗？你认为这会是一种自我提升吗？

segments.text: Like, do you think it’ll be simple? Do you think it’ll be something like a self-improving program and a simple one?

translation.text: 比如说，你认为这会很简单吗？你认为这会是一个自我完善的程序并且是一个简单的程序吗？

segments.text: I mean potentially that’s possible. I would say I’m not sure it’s even desirable because that’s a kind of hard takeoff scenario. But these current systems like Alpha Evolve, they have human in the loop deciding on various things, there’re separate hybrid systems that interact.

translation.text: 我的意思是这有可能。我想说我不确定它是否可取，因为这是一种艰难的起飞场景。但目前的这些系统，比如 Alpha Evolve，它们有人类在循环中决定各种事情，有独立的混合系统进行交互。

segments.text: One could imagine eventually doing that end to end. I don’t see why that wouldn’t be possible, but right now I think the systems are not good enough to do that in terms of coming up with the architecture of the code.

translation.text: 人们可以想象最终会端到端地做到这一点。我不明白为什么这是不可能的，但现在我认为系统在提出代码架构方面还不够好，无法做到这一点。

segments.text: And again, it’s a little bit reconnected to this idea of coming up with a new conjectural hypothesis, how they’re good if you give them very specific instructions about what you’re trying to do, but if you give them a very vague high level instruction, that wouldn’t work currently.

translation.text: 再说一次，这与提出一个新的猜想假设的想法重新联系起来，如果你给他们关于你想要做什么的非常具体的指示，他们会如何很好，但如果你给他们一个非常模糊的高级指示，那目前是行不通的。

segments.text: And I think that’s related to this idea of invent a game as good as Go, right?

translation.text: 我认为这与发明一款像围棋一样好的游戏的想法有关，对吧？

segments.text: Imagine that was the prompt. That’s pretty. And so the current systems wouldn’t know I think what to do with that, how to narrow that down to something tractable.

translation.text: 想象一下这就是提示。那很漂亮。因此，当前的系统不知道我想如何处理它，如何将其范围缩小到易于处理的范围。

segments.text: And I think there’s similar, look, just make a better version of yourself. That’s too unconstrained. But we’ve done it.

translation.text: 我认为也有类似的地方，看看，只是让自己变得更好。这也太不受约束了吧。但我们已经做到了。

segments.text: And as you know with AlphaVol, like things like faster matrix multiplication, so when you hone it down to very specific thing you want, it’s very good at incrementally improving that.

translation.text: 正如你所知道的 AlphaVol，就像更快的矩阵乘法之类的东西，所以当你把它磨练到你想要的非常具体的东西时，它非常擅长逐步改进。

segments.text: But at the moment these are more incremental improvements, sort of small iterations. Whereas if you wanted a big leap in understanding, you’d need a much larger advance.

translation.text: 但目前这些都是增量改进，有点小迭代。然而，如果你想要在理解上有一个大的飞跃，你就需要更大的进步。

segments.text: Yeah. But it could also be sort of the pushback against hard takeoff scenario. It could be just a sequence of incremental improvements, like matrix multiplication.

translation.text: 是的。但这也可能是对硬起飞情景的阻碍。它可能只是一系列渐进式改进，例如矩阵乘法。

segments.text: It has to sit there for days thinking how to incrementally improve a thing and it does solve recursively. And as you do more and more improvement, it’ll slow down.

translation.text: 它必须在那里呆上几天思考如何逐步改进某件事，并且它确实递归地解决了问题。当你做越来越多的改进时，它会减慢。

segments.text: So there be, the path to AGI won’t be like a gradual improvement over time.

translation.text: 因此，通向 AGI 的道路不会随着时间的推移而逐渐改善。

segments.text: Yes. If it was just incremental improvements, that’s how it would look. So the question is, could it come up with a new leap like the Transformers architecture?

translation.text: 是的。如果只是渐进式改进，那就是这样的。那么问题来了，它能否像Transformer 架构一样，带来新的飞跃呢？

segments.text: Could it have done that back in 2017 when we did it and Brain did it? And it’s not clear that these systems, something our AlphaVol wouldn’t be able to do, make such a big leap.

translation.text: 早在 2017 年，当我们和 Brain 都做到了的时候，它就能做到这一点吗？目前尚不清楚这些系统（我们的 AlphaVol 无法做到）是否会取得如此大的飞跃。

segments.text: So for sure these systems are good. We have systems I think that can do incremental hill climbing, and that’s a kind of bigger question about is that all that’s needed from here, or do we actually need one or two more big breakthroughs.

translation.text: 所以可以肯定的是，这些系统都是好的。我认为我们有可以渐进爬山的系统，这是一个更大的问题是，这里需要什么，或者我们实际上需要一两个更大的突破。

segments.text: And can the same kind of systems provide the breakthroughs also? So make it a bunch of S-curves like incremental improvement, but also every once in a while, leaps.

translation.text: 同类系统也能提供突破吗？因此，让它成为一堆S曲线，就像渐进式改进一样，但也每隔一段时间，跳跃一次。

segments.text: Yeah, I don’t think anyone has systems that can have shown, unequivocally those big leaps that we have a lot of systems that do the hill climbing of the S-curve that you’re currently on.

translation.text: 是的，我认为没有人拥有可以明确显示那些巨大飞跃的系统，我们有很多系统可以完成您目前所处的 S 曲线爬坡。

segments.text: And that would be the move 37 is a leap.

translation.text: 这就是第37手是一个飞跃。

segments.text: Yeah, I think it would be a leap, something like that.

translation.text: 是的，我认为这将是一个飞跃，类似的事情。

segments.text: Do you think the scaling laws are holding strong on the pre-training/post-training test time compute? Do you on the flip side of that, anticipate AI progress hitting a wall?

translation.text: 你认为扩展定律在预训练、后训练以及测试时计算方面仍然有效吗？反过来说，你是否预计人工智能的发展会撞上瓶颈？

segments.text: We certainly feel there’s a lot more room just in the scaling. So actually all steps pre-training, post-training, and inference time.

translation.text: 我们当然认为在扩展方面还有更多的空间。所以实际上所有步骤都是训练前、训练后和推理时间。

segments.text: So there’s sort of three scalings that are happening concurrently. And again there, it’s about how innovative you can be and we pride ourselves on having the broadest and deepest research bench.

translation.text: 因此，三种扩展同时发生。再说一遍，这关乎您的创新能力，我们为拥有最广泛和最深入的研究平台而感到自豪。

segments.text: We have amazing, incredible researchers and people like Noam Shazir who came up with Transformers and Dave Silver who led the AlphaGo project and so on.

translation.text: 我们有令人惊叹的、令人难以置信的研究人员和像诺姆·沙齐尔（Noam Shazir）这样的人，他提出了Transformer，戴夫·西尔弗（Dave Silver）领导了 AlphaGo 项目等等。

segments.text: And that research base means that if some new breakthrough is required, like an AlphaGo or Transformers, I would back us to be the place that does that.

translation.text: 这个研究基地意味着，如果需要一些新的突破，比如 AlphaGo 或Transformer，我会支持我们成为实现这一目标的地方。

segments.text: So I’m actually quite like it when the terrain gets harder, right? Because then it veers more from just engineering to true research, and research plus engineering, and that’s our sweet spot and I think that’s harder.

translation.text: 所以我实际上很喜欢地形变得更困难，对吧？因为这样它就会更多地从工程转向真正的研究，以及研究加工程，这就是我们的最佳点，我认为这更难。

segments.text: It’s harder to invent things than to fast follow.

translation.text: 发明事物比快速追随更难。

segments.text: And so we don’t know, I would say it’s kind of 50/50 whether new things are needed or whether the scaling the existing stuff is going to be enough.

translation.text: 所以我们不知道，我想说这是 50/50 是否需要新东西或者扩展现有东西是否足够。

segments.text: And so, in true kind of empirical fashion, we are pushing both of those as hard as possible. The new blue sky, ideas and maybe about half our resources are on that.

translation.text: 因此，以真正的实证方式，我们正在尽可能努力地推动这两个目标。新的蓝天、创意以及我们大约一半的资源都在于此。

segments.text: And then scaling to the max, the current capabilities. And we’re still seeing some fantastic progress on each different version of Gemini.

translation.text: 然后扩展到当前能力的最大值。我们仍然看到 Gemini 的每个不同版本都取得了一些惊人的进展。

segments.text: That’s interesting the way you put it in terms of the deep bench, that if progress towards AGI is more than just scaling compute, so the engineering side of the problem, and is more on the scientific side where there’s breakthroughs needed, then you feel confident DeepMind as well,

translation.text: 从深度工作台的角度来看，这很有趣，如果 AGI 的进展不仅仅是扩展计算，那么问题的工程方面，更多的是需要突破的科学方面，那么你也会对 DeepMind 充满信心，

segments.text: Google DeepMind as well positioned to kick ass in that domain.

translation.text: 谷歌 DeepMind 在该领域也处于领先地位。

segments.text: Well, I mean if you look at the history of the last decade or 15 years, it’s been maybe, I don’t know, 80-90% of the breakthroughs that underpins modern AI field today was from originally Google Brain, Google Research and DeepMind.

translation.text: 嗯，我的意思是，如果你看看过去 10 年或 15 年的历史，我不知道，支撑当今现代人工智能领域的 80-90% 的突破可能最初来自 Google Brain、Google Research 和 DeepMind。

segments.text: So yeah, I would back that to continue hopefully.

translation.text: 所以是的，我会支持这一点并希望继续下去。

segments.text: So on the data side, are you concerned about running out of high quality data, especially high quality human data?

translation.text: 那么在数据方面，您是否担心高质量数据，尤其是高质量的人类数据用完？

segments.text: I’m not very worried about that. Partly because I think there’s enough data, and it’s been proven to get the systems to be pretty good. And this goes back to simulations again.

translation.text: 我对此不太担心。部分原因是我认为有足够的数据，并且已被证明可以使系统变得非常好。这又回到了模拟。

segments.text: Do you have enough data to make simulations, so that you can create more synthetic data that are from the right distribution? Obviously that’s the key.

translation.text: 您是否有足够的数据来进行模拟，以便您可以创建更多来自正确分布的合成数据？显然这是关键。

segments.text: So you need enough real-world data in order to be able to create those kinds of data generators, and I think that we’re at that step at the moment.

translation.text: 因此，您需要足够的现实世界数据才能创建此类数据生成器，我认为我们目前正处于这一步。

segments.text: Yeah, you’ve done a lot of incredible stuff on the side of science and biology, doing a lot with not so much data.

translation.text: 是的，你在科学和生物学方面做了很多令人难以置信的事情，但用的数据并不多。

segments.text: Yeah.

translation.text: 是的。

segments.text: I mean it’s still a lot of data, but I guess enough to-

translation.text: 我的意思是它仍然有很多数据，但我想足以-

segments.text: To get that going. Exactly. Exactly

translation.text: 为了让这一切顺利进行。确切地。确切地

segments.text: Yeah. How crucial is the scaling of compute to building AGI? That’s an engineering question.

translation.text: 是的。计算扩展对于构建 AGI 有多重要？这是一个工程问题。

segments.text: It’s almost a geopolitical question because it also integrated into that is supply chains and energy. A thing that you care a lot about, which is potentially fusion.

translation.text: 这几乎是一个地缘政治问题，因为它也融入了供应链和能源。你非常关心的一件事，就是潜在的融合。

segments.text: So innovating on the side of energy also. Do you think we’re going to keep scaling compute?

translation.text: 因此，能源方面也进行了创新。您认为我们会继续扩展计算吗？

segments.text: I think so, for several reasons. I think compute, there’s the amount of compute you have for training, often it needs to be co-located, so actually even bandwidth constraints between data centers can affect that.

translation.text: 我这么认为，有几个原因。我认为计算，有用于训练的计算量，通常需要位于同一地点，所以实际上即使数据中心之间的带宽限制也会影响这一点。

segments.text: So there’s additional constraints even there and that’s important for training, obviously the largest models you can, but there’s also because now AI systems are in products and being used by billions of people around the world, you need a ton of inference compute now.

translation.text: 因此，即使在那里也存在额外的限制，这对于训练来说很重要，显然是你可以训练的最大模型，但也因为现在人工智能系统已经进入产品并被全世界数十亿人使用，所以你现在需要大量的推理计算。

segments.text: And then on top of that there’s the thinking systems, the new paradigm of the last year that where they get smarter, the longer amount of inference time you give them at test time.

translation.text: 除此之外还有思维系统，去年的新范式，它们变得更聪明，你在测试时给它们的推理时间就越长。

segments.text: So all of those things need a lot of compute and I don’t really see that slowing down, and as AI systems become better, they’ll become more useful and there’ll be more demand for them.

translation.text: 因此，所有这些事情都需要大量的计算，而且我并没有真正看到这种速度会放缓，而且随着人工智能系统变得更好，它们将变得更有用，对它们的需求也会更大。

segments.text: So both from the training side, the training side actually is only just one part of that. It may even become the smaller part of what’s needed in the overall compute that’s required.

translation.text: 所以无论是从培训方面来说，培训方面实际上只是其中的一部分。它甚至可能成为整个计算所需的较小部分。

segments.text: Yeah, that’s one sort of almost meme-y kind of thing, which is the success in the incredible aspects of VL3. People kind of make fun of the more successful it becomes, the servers are sweating.

translation.text: 是的，这就是一种近乎迷因的事情，这就是 VL3 令人难以置信的成功。人们有点取笑它变得越成功，服务器就流汗了。

segments.text: Yes.

translation.text: 是的。

segments.text: The inference.

translation.text: 推论。

segments.text: Yeah, yeah, exactly. We did a little video of the servers frying eggs and things. That’s right.

translation.text: 是的，是的，完全正确。我们制作了一段服务器煎鸡蛋之类的小视频。这是正确的。

segments.text: And we are going to have to figure out how to do that. There’s a lot of interesting hardware innovations that we do as we have our own TPU line and we’re looking at inference-only things, inference-only chips and how we can make those more efficient.

translation.text: 我们必须弄清楚如何做到这一点。我们做了很多有趣的硬件创新，因为我们拥有自己的 TPU 系列，我们正在研究仅推理的东西、仅推理的芯片以及如何提高这些创新的效率。

segments.text: We’re also very interested in building AI systems and we have done the help with energy usage, so help data center energy like for the cooling systems be efficient, grid optimization, and then eventually things like helping with plasma-containment fusion reactors.

translation.text: 我们也对构建人工智能系统非常感兴趣，并且我们已经在能源使用方面提供了帮助，因此帮助数据中心能源（例如冷却系统）变得高效，电网优化，然后最终帮助等离子体包容聚变反应堆。

segments.text: We’ve done lots of work on that with Commonwealth Fusion, and also one could imagine reactor design.

translation.text: 我们已经通过 Commonwealth Fusion 做了很多这方面的工作，人们也可以想象反应堆的设计。

segments.text: And then material design I think is one of the most exciting. New types of solar material, solar panel material room temperature superconductors has always been on my list of dream breakthroughs, and optimal batteries.

translation.text: 我认为材料设计是最令人兴奋的之一。新型太阳能材料、太阳能电池板材料、室温超导体一直是我梦想突破的清单，也是最佳电池。

segments.text: And I think a solution to any one of those things would be absolutely revolutionary for climate and energy usage. And we’re probably close, and again in the next five years to having AI systems that can materially help with those problems.

translation.text: 我认为解决其中任何一个问题对于气候和能源使用来说绝对是革命性的。我们可能会在未来五年内再次接近拥有能够为解决这些问题提供实质性帮助的人工智能系统。

segments.text: If you were to bet, sorry for the ridiculous question, but what is the main source of energy in 20, 30, 40 years. Do you think it’s going to be nuclear fusion?

translation.text: 如果你要打赌，抱歉问了这个可笑的问题，但是20年、30年、40年的主要能源来源是什么？你认为这会是核聚变吗？

segments.text: I think fusion and solar are the two that I would bet on. Solar, I mean it’s the fusion reactor in the sky of course, and I think really the problem there is batteries and transmission.

translation.text: 我认为聚变和太阳能是我押注的两个。太阳能，我的意思是它当然是天空中的聚变反应堆，我认为真正的问题在于电池和传输。

segments.text: So as well as more efficient, more and more efficient solar material perhaps eventually in space, these kind of Dyson Sphere type ideas.

translation.text: 因此，以及更高效、越来越高效的太阳能材料也许最终会出现在太空中，这些戴森球类型的想法。

segments.text: And fusion I think is definitely doable, it seems, if we have the right design of reactor and we can control the plasma and fast enough and so on, and I think both of those things will actually get solved.

translation.text: 我认为聚变绝对是可行的，如果我们有正确的反应堆设计，我们可以控制等离子体并足够快等等，我认为这两件事实际上都会得到解决。

segments.text: So we’ll probably have at least those are probably the two primary sources of renewable, clean, almost free or perhaps free energy.

translation.text: 因此，我们可能至少会拥有可再生、清洁、几乎免费或可能免费能源的两种主要来源。

segments.text: What a time to be alive. If I traveled into the future with you a hundred years from now, how much would you be surprised if we’ve passed a type one Kardashev scale civilization?

translation.text: 活着是多么美好的时光啊。如果我和你一起穿越到一百年后的未来，如果我们已经过了卡尔达肖夫级文明，你会感到多么惊讶？

segments.text: I would not be that surprised if it was a hundred-year timescale from here. I mean I think it’s pretty clear if we crack the energy problems in one of the ways we’ve just discussed or very efficient solar, then if energy is kind of free and renewable and clean, then that solves a whole bunch of other problems.

translation.text: 如果时间跨度是一百年，我不会感到惊讶。我的意思是，我认为很明显，如果我们以我们刚才讨论的方式之一解决能源问题，或者非常高效的太阳能，那么如果能源是免费的、可再生的和清洁的，那么这就解决了一大堆其他问题。

segments.text: So for example, the water access problem goes away because you can just use desalination. We have the technology, it’s just too expensive. So only fairly wealthy countries like Singapore and Israel and so on actually use it.

translation.text: 例如，用水问题就消失了，因为你可以使用海水淡化。我们有技术，只是太贵了。所以只有新加坡和以色列等相当富裕的国家才会真正使用它。

segments.text: But if it was cheap, then all countries that have a coast could, but also you’d have unlimited rocket fuel. You could just separate seawater out into hydrogen and oxygen using energy and that’s rocket fuel.

translation.text: 但如果它很便宜，那么所有拥有海岸的国家都可以，而且你还会拥有无限的火箭燃料。你可以利用能量将海水分离成氢气和氧气，这就是火箭燃料。

segments.text: So combined with Elon’s, amazing self landing rockets, then it could be you sort of like a bus service to space. So that opens up incredible new resources and domains.

translation.text: 因此，与埃隆令人惊叹的自着陆火箭相结合，那么它可能有点像通往太空的巴士服务。这样就开辟了令人难以置信的新资源和领域。

segments.text: Asteroid mining I think will become a thing, and maximum human flourishing to the stars. That’s what I dream about as well is like Carl Sagan’s sort of idea of bringing consciousness to the universe, waking up the universe.

translation.text: 我认为小行星采矿将成​​为一件事，人类将在星星上实现最大程度的繁荣。这也是我的梦想，就像卡尔·萨根的那种将意识带入宇宙、唤醒宇宙的想法。

segments.text: And I think human civilization will do that in the full sense of time if we get AI right, and crack some of these problems with it.

translation.text: 我认为，如果我们正确使用人工智能，并用它解决其中一些问题，人类文明将在完全意义上做到这一点。

segments.text: Yeah, I wonder what it would look like if you’re just a tourist flying through space. You would probably notice earth because if you solve the energy problem, you would see a lot of space rockets probably.

translation.text: 是的，我想知道如果你只是一个穿越太空的游客会是什么样子。你可能会注意到地球，因为如果你解决了能源问题，你可能会看到很多太空火箭。

segments.text: So it would be traffic here in London, but in space.

translation.text: 因此，这将是伦敦这里的交通，但在太空中。

segments.text: Yes, exactly.

translation.text: 是的，完全正确。

segments.text: It’s just a lot of rockets. And then you would probably see floating in space, some kind of source of energy like solar potentially.

translation.text: 这只是很多火箭。然后你可能会看到漂浮在太空中的某种能源，例如太阳能。

segments.text: So earth would just look more on the surface, more technological. And then you would use the power of that energy then to preserve the natural…

translation.text: 所以地球表面上看起来会更加科技化。然后你会利用这种能量的力量来保护自然……

segments.text: Yes.

translation.text: 是的。

segments.text: Like the rainforest and all that kind of stuff.

translation.text: 就像热带雨林和所有类似的东西。

segments.text: Exactly. Because for the first time in human history we wouldn’t be resource constrained. And I think that could be amazing new era for humanity where it’s not zero-sum, right?

translation.text: 确切地。因为在人类历史上，我们第一次不再受到资源限制。我认为这对人类来说可能是一个令人惊奇的新时代，它不是零和的，对吗？

segments.text: I have this land, you don’t have it. Or if the tigers have their forest, then the local villages can’t, what are they going to use?

translation.text: 这片土地我有，你没有。或者说，如果老虎有森林，当地村庄却没有，那他们拿什么去利用呢？

segments.text: I think that this will help a lot. No, it won’t solve all problems because there’s still other human foibles that will still exist, but it will at least remove one, I think one of the big vectors, which is scarcity of resources, including land and more materials and energy.

translation.text: 我认为这会有很大帮助。不，它不会解决所有问题，因为人类的其他弱点仍然存在，但它至少会消除一个，我认为是最大的矢量之一，那就是资源的稀缺，包括土地和更多的材料和能源。

segments.text: And we should be sometimes call it another call about this kind of radical abundance era, where there’s plenty of resources to go around.

translation.text: 有时我们应该称其为关于这种激进的富足时代的另一种召唤，那里有大量的资源可供使用。

segments.text: Of course the next big question is making sure that that’s fairly, shared fairly and everyone in society benefits from that.

translation.text: 当然，下一个大问题是确保公平、公平地分享，并且社会中的每个人都能从中受益。

segments.text: So there is something about human nature where I go, its like Borat, like my neighbor. You start trouble.

translation.text: 所以我所到之处都有一些关于人性的东西，就像波拉特，就像我的邻居。你就开始惹麻烦了。

segments.text: We do start conflicts and that’s why games throughout, as I’m learning actually more and more, even in ancient history, serve the purpose of pushing people away from war, actually hot war.

translation.text: 我们确实会引发冲突，这就是为什么游戏始终是为了让人们远离战争，实际上是热战，我实际上越来越多地了解到，即使在古代历史中也是如此。

segments.text: So maybe we can figure out increasingly sophisticated video games that pull us, they give us that… Scratch the itch of conflict, whatever that is, but us, the human nature.

translation.text: 所以也许我们可以找出越来越复杂的电子游戏来吸引我们，它们给我们带来……挠痒痒的冲突，不管是什么，但我们，人性。

segments.text: Like… Yeah.

translation.text: 就像……是的。

segments.text: And then avoid the actual hot wars that would come with increasingly sophisticated technologies because we’re now, we’ve long passed the stage where the weapons we’re able to create can actually just destroy all of human civilization.

translation.text: 然后避免日益复杂的技术带来的实际热战，因为我们现在已经过了我们能够制造的武器实际上可以摧毁所有人类文明的阶段。

segments.text: So that’s no longer a great way to start with your neighbor. It’s better to play a game of chess.

translation.text: 因此，这不再是从邻居开始的好方法。最好还是下一盘象棋。

segments.text: Or football.

translation.text: 或者足球。

segments.text: Or football. Yeah.

translation.text: 或者足球。是的。

segments.text: And I think that’s what my modern sport is and I love football watching it and I just feel like, and I used to play it a lot as well, and it’s very visceral in its tribal,

translation.text: 我认为这就是我的现代运动，我喜欢看足球，我只是觉得，我过去也经常踢足球，它的部落风格非常发自内心，

segments.text: and I think it does channel a lot of those energies into which I think is a kind of human need to belong to some group, but into a fun way, a healthy way and not destructive way, kind of constructive thing.

translation.text: 我认为它确实引导了很多能量，我认为这是人类属于某个群体的一种需要，但以一种有趣的方式，一种健康的方式而不是破坏性的方式，一种建设性的方式。

segments.text: And I think going back to games again is I think they’re originally why they’re so great as well for kids to play things like chess is they’re great little microcosm simulations of the world.

translation.text: 我认为再次回到游戏，我认为它们最初是为什么它们对孩子们玩象棋这样的东西如此伟大的原因是它们是世界的伟大的小微观模拟。

segments.text: They’re simulations of the world too. They’re simplified versions of some real world situation, whether it’s poker or Go or chess, different aspects or diplomacy, different of the real world.

translation.text: 它们也是对世界的模拟。它们是一些现实世界情况的简化版本，无论是扑克、围棋还是国际象棋，不同的方面或外交，与现实世界不同。

segments.text: And it allows you to practice at them too, because how many times do you get to practice a massive decision moment in your life? What job to take, what university to go to?

translation.text: 它也可以让你练习它们，因为你一生中有多少次可以练习重大决策时刻？做什么工作，去哪所大学？

segments.text: You get maybe, I don’t know, a dozen or so key decisions one has to make and you’ve got to make those as best as you can.

translation.text: 我不知道，你可能需要做出十几个关键决定，而你必须尽你所能做出最好的决定。

segments.text: And games is a kind of safe environment, repeatable environment where you can get better at your decision-making process, and it maybe has this additional benefit of channeling some energies into more creative and constructive pursuits.

translation.text: 游戏是一种安全的、可重复的环境，你可以在其中更好地制定决策过程，而且它可能还具有将一些精力引导到更具创造性和建设性的追求上的额外好处。

segments.text: Well I think it’s also really important to practice losing and winning.

translation.text: 嗯，我认为练习输和赢也非常重要。

segments.text: Right.

translation.text: 正确的。

segments.text: Losing is a really, that’s why I love games. That’s why I love even things like Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu where you can get your kicked in a safe environment over and over.

translation.text: 失败确实是一种痛苦，这就是我热爱游戏的原因。这就是为什么我什至喜欢巴西柔术这样的东西，在那里你可以在安全的环境中一遍又一遍地踢你的拳。

segments.text: It reminds you about physics, about the way the world works about sometimes you lose, sometimes you win, you can still be friends with everybody.

translation.text: 它让你想起物理学，想起世界的运作方式，有时你输了，有时你赢了，你仍然可以和每个人成为朋友。

segments.text: But that feeling of losing, I mean it’s a weird one for us humans to really make sense of. That’s just part of life.

translation.text: 但那种失落的感觉，我的意思是，对于我们人类来说，真正理解这种感觉很奇怪。这只是生活的一部分。

segments.text: That is a fundamental part of life is losing.

translation.text: 这是生命的一个基本部分正在失去。

segments.text: And I think the martial arts as I understand it, but also in things like light chess is at least the way I took it’s a lot to do with self-improvement, self-knowledge.

translation.text: 我认为我所理解的武术，以及像轻棋这样的东西，至少是我所采取的方式，这与自我完善、自我认识有很大关系。

segments.text: That, okay, so I did this thing. It’s not about really beating the other person, it’s about maximizing your own potential.

translation.text: 那好吧，所以我做了这件事。并不是要真正打败别人，而是要最大限度地发挥自己的潜力。

segments.text: If you do it in a healthy way, you learn to use victory and losses in a way. Don’t get carried away with victory and think you’re just the best in the world.

translation.text: 如果你以健康的方式去做，你就会学会以某种方式利用胜利和失败。不要因胜利而得意忘形，认为自己是世界上最好的。

segments.text: And the losses keep you humble, and always knowing there’s always something more to learn. There’s always a bigger expert that you can mentor you. I think you learn that I’m pretty sure in martial arts.

translation.text: 失败让你保持谦虚，并且始终知道总有更多东西需要学习。总有一位更优秀的专家可以指导您。我想你知道我对武术很有把握。

segments.text: And I think that’s also the way that at least I was trained in chess. And so, in the same way, and it can be very hardcore and very important and of course you want to win, but you also need to learn how to deal with setbacks in a healthy way, and wire that feeling that you have when you lose something into a constructive thing of,

translation.text: 我认为至少我接受国际象棋训练的方式也是如此。所以，以同样的方式，它可以是非常核心和非常重要的，当然你想赢，但你也需要学习如何以健康的方式处理挫折，并将当你失去某些东西时的感觉转化为建设性的东西，

segments.text: next time I’m going to improve this or get better at this.

translation.text: 下次我会改进这个或者在这方面做得更好。

segments.text: There is something that’s a source of happiness, a source of meaning that improvements that… It’s not about the winning or losing.

translation.text: 有些东西是幸福的源泉，是意义的源泉，可以改善……这与输赢无关。

segments.text: Yes, the mastery. There’s nothing more satisfying in a way. It’s like, oh wow, this thing I couldn’t do before.

translation.text: 是的，精通。从某种程度上来说，没有什么比这更令人满意的了。就像，哦哇，这件事我以前做不到。

segments.text: Now I can. And again, games and physical sports and mental sports, their ways of measuring their beautiful, because you can measure that progress.

translation.text: 现在我可以了。再说一次，游戏、体育运动和心理运动，是他们衡量美丽的方式，因为你可以衡量进步。

segments.text: Yeah, there’s something about I guess why I love role-playing games, like the number go up of on the skill tree, literally that is a source of meaning for us humans, whatever our-

translation.text: 是的，我想我为什么喜欢角色扮演游戏，比如技能树上的数字，从字面上看，这对我们人类来说是意义的来源，无论我们 -

segments.text: Yeah, we’re quite addicted to this sort of, these numbers going up. And maybe that’s why we made games like that because obviously that is something we’re hill climbing systems ourselves, right?

translation.text: 是的，我们非常沉迷于这种数字的上升。也许这就是我们制作这样的游戏的原因，因为显然这就是我们自己的爬山系统，对吧？

segments.text: Yeah. It would be quite sad if we didn’t have any mechanism-

translation.text: 是的。如果我们没有任何机制那就太可悲了——

segments.text: Color belts, we do this everywhere, where we just have thing that…

translation.text: 色带，我们到处都这样做，我们只是有这样的东西……

segments.text: And I don’t want to dismiss that. There is a source of deep meaning across humans.

translation.text: 我不想否认这一点。人类之间存在着深刻的意义之源。

segments.text: So one of the incredible stories on the business, on the leadership side is what Google has done over the past year. So I think it’s fair to say that Google was losing on the LLM product side a year ago with Gemini 1.5 And now it’s winning, which… I’m Joe Biden.

translation.text: 因此，在商业和领导力方面，最令人难以置信的故事之一就是谷歌在过去一年中所做的事情。所以我认为可以公平地说，谷歌一年前在 Gemini 1.5 的 LLM 产品方面输了，但现在它赢了，这……我是乔·拜登。

segments.text: And you took the helm and you led this effort. What did it take to go from let’s say quote-unquote losing to quote-unquote winning, in the span of a year?

translation.text: 你掌舵并领导了这项工作。在一年的时间里，从惨败到胜利，经历了什么？

segments.text: Yeah, well firstly it’s absolutely incredible team that we have led by Corey and Jeff Dean and Oriole and the amazing team we have on Gemini.

translation.text: 是的。首先，我们拥有一支非常出色的团队，由 Koray、Jeff Dean、Oriol 等人领导，还有负责 Gemini 的优秀团队。

segments.text: Absolutely. So you can’t do it without the best talent. And of course we have a lot of great compute as well.

translation.text: 绝对地。因此，如果没有最优秀的人才，你就无法做到这一点。当然，我们也有很多出色的计算能力。

segments.text: But then it’s the research culture we’ve created and basically coming together both different groups in Google that was Google Brain, World-class team, and then the old DeepMind, and pulling together all the best people and the best ideas and gathering around to make the absolute greater system we could.

translation.text: 但我们创造了一种研究文化，基本上将谷歌的两个不同团队聚集在一起，即谷歌大脑、世界一流的团队，然后是旧的 DeepMind，汇集所有最优秀的人才和最好的想法，并聚集在一起，打造我们能够打造的绝对更伟大的系统。

segments.text: And it was been hard, but we’re all very competitive and we love research. This is so fun to do, and it’s great to see our trajectory.

translation.text: 这很艰难，但我们都非常有竞争力，而且我们热爱研究。这样做非常有趣，很高兴看到我们的发展轨迹。

segments.text: It wasn’t a given, but we’re very pleased with where we are and the rate of progress is the most important thing.

translation.text: 这不是理所当然的，但我们对目前的情况非常满意，进展速度是最重要的。

segments.text: So if you look at where we’ve come to from two years ago to one year ago to now, I think we call it relentless progress.

translation.text: 因此，如果你看看我们从两年前到一年前到现在所取得的进展，我认为我们称之为不懈的进步。

segments.text: Along with relentless shipping of that progress is being very successful and it’s unbelievably competitive, the whole space, the whole AI space, with some of the greatest entrepreneurs and leaders and companies in the world, all competing now because everyone’s realized how important AI is.

translation.text: 随着这一进步的不断推进，非常成功，并且竞争非常激烈，整个领域、整个人工智能领域，以及世界上一些最伟大的企业家、领导者和公司，现在都在竞争，因为每个人都意识到人工智能的重要性。

segments.text: And it’s very been pleasing for us to see that progress.

translation.text: 我们很高兴看到这一进展。

segments.text: Google’s a gigantic company. Can you speak to the natural things that happen in that case is the bureaucracy that emerges? You want to be careful the natural, there’s meetings and there’s managers and that.

translation.text: 谷歌是一家大公司。您能谈谈在那种情况下发生的自然事情就是出现的官僚主义吗？你自然要小心，有会议，有经理等等。

segments.text: What are some of the challenges from a leadership perspective, breaking through that in order to, like you said, ship? Like the number of products, Gemini related products that has been shipped over the past years is insane.

translation.text: 从领导力的角度来看，要突破这些挑战以便像您所说的那样进行交付，有哪些挑战？与产品数量一样，过去几年 Gemini 相关产品的出货量也是疯狂的。

segments.text: Right? Yeah, exactly. That’s what relentlessness looks like.

translation.text: 正确的？是的，完全正确。这就是无情的样子。

segments.text: I think it’s a question of any big company ends up having a lot of layers of management and things like that is sort of the nature of how it works.

translation.text: 我认为这是一个问题，任何大公司最终都会有很多管理层，类似的事情就是它运作的本质。

segments.text: But I still operate and I was always operating with old DeepMind as a start-up still. A large one, but still as a start-up.

translation.text: 但我仍然在运营，而且我一直在以初创公司的形式运营旧的 DeepMind。规模很大，但仍是一家初创公司。

segments.text: And that’s what we still act like today with Google DeepMind. And acting with decisiveness and the energy that you get from the best smaller organizations.

translation.text: 这就是我们今天对 Google DeepMind 的做法。并以从最好的小型组织那里获得的果断行动和活力。

segments.text: And we try to get the best of both worlds where we have this incredible, billions of users surfaces and credible products that we can power up with our AI and our research and that’s amazing and that’s very few places in the world you can get that,

translation.text: 我们试图充分利用这两个世界，我们拥有令人难以置信的数十亿用户界面和可靠的产品，我们可以通过我们的人工智能和研究来增强这些产品，这真是太棒了，世界上很少有地方可以做到这一点，

segments.text: do incredible world-class research on the one hand and then plug it in and improve billions of people’s lives the next day. That’s a pretty amazing combination.

translation.text: 一方面进行令人难以置信的世界级研究，然后第二天将其投入使用并改善数十亿人的生活。这是一个非常惊人的组合。

segments.text: And we’re continually fighting and cutting away bureaucracy to allow the research culture and the relentless shipping culture to flourish. And I think we’ve got a pretty good balance, whilst being responsible with it, as you have to be as a large company and also with a number of huge product surfaces that we have.

translation.text: 我们不断地与官僚作风作斗争，让研究文化和不懈的航运文化蓬勃发展。我认为我们在对此负责的同时取得了很好的平衡，因为你必须是一家大公司，并且我们拥有许多巨大的产品表面。

segments.text: So a funny thing you mentioned about the surface with the billion, I had a conversation with a guy named, brilliant guy here at the British Museum, called Irvin Finkel.

translation.text: 你提到的关于十亿表面的有趣的事情是，我和大英博物馆的一位名叫欧文·芬克尔（Irvin Finkel）的才华横溢的人进行了交谈。

segments.text: He’s a world expert at cuneiforms, which is a ancient writing on tablets and he doesn’t know about ChatGPT or Gemini, he doesn’t even know about AI, but this first encounter with this AI is AI mode on Google.

translation.text: 他是楔形文字的世界专家，楔形文字是平板电脑上的一种古老文字，他不知道 ChatGPT 或 Gemini，他甚至不知道人工智能，但第一次接触这种人工智能是谷歌的人工智能模式。

segments.text: Yes.

translation.text: 是的。

segments.text: He’s like, is that what you’re talking about, this AI mode? And it’s just a reminder that there’s a large part of the world that doesn’t know about this AI thing.

translation.text: 他说，这就是你所说的人工智能模式吗？这只是提醒我们，世界上有很大一部分人不了解人工智能这个东西。

segments.text: Yeah, I know. It’s funny. If you live on X and Twitter and I mean it’s sort of at least my feed, it’s all AI.

translation.text: 是的，我知道。这很有趣。如果你生活在 X 和 Twitter 上，我的意思是，这至少是我的动态，那都是人工智能。

segments.text: And there’s certain places where in the valley and certain pockets where everyone’s just, all they’re thinking about is AI, but a lot of the normal world hasn’t come across it yet.

translation.text: 在山谷的某些地方和某些地方，每个人都只是思考人工智能，但很多正常世界还没有遇到过它。

segments.text: And that’s a great responsibility to their first interaction. The grand scale of the rural, India or anywhere across the world you get to…

translation.text: 这对他们的第一次互动来说是一个巨大的责任。印度或世界任何地方的乡村规模宏大……

segments.text: And we want it to be as good as possible and in a lot of cases it’s just under the hood powering, making something like maps or search work better.

translation.text: 我们希望它尽可能好，在很多情况下，它只是在幕后提供动力，使地图或搜索等功能更好地工作。

segments.text: And ideally for a lot of those people should just be seamless. It’s just new technology that makes their lives more productive and helps them.

translation.text: 理想情况下，对于很多人来说应该是无缝的。新技术可以让他们的生活更加富有成效并为他们提供帮助。

segments.text: A bunch of folks on the Gemini product and engineering teams spoken extremely highly of you on another dimension, that I almost didn’t even expect. I kind of think of you as the deep scientists and caring about these big research scientific questions.

translation.text: Gemini 产品和工程团队的一群人在另一个层面对你给予了极高的评价，这是我几乎没有想到的。我认为你们是深层次的科学家，关心这些重大的研究科学问题。

segments.text: But they also said you’re a great product guy, like how to create a thing that a lot of people would use and enjoy using. So can you maybe speak to what it takes to create a AI based product that a lot of people enjoy using?

translation.text: 但他们也说你是一个很棒的产品人员，喜欢如何创造一个很多人会使用并喜欢使用的东西。那么您能否谈谈如何创建一个很多人喜欢使用的基于人工智能的产品？

segments.text: Yeah. Well, I mean, again, that comes back from my game design days where I used to design games for millions of gamers. People would forget about that.

translation.text: 是的。嗯，我的意思是，这又是从我的游戏设计时代开始的，当时我为数百万玩家设计游戏。人们会忘记这一点。

segments.text: I’ve had experience with cutting edge technology in product that is how games was in the nineties.

translation.text: 我在产品中拥有尖端技术的经验，就像九十年代的游戏一样。

segments.text: And so I love actually the combination of cutting edge research and then being applied in a product and to power a new experience.

translation.text: 因此，我实际上喜欢前沿研究的结合，然后将其应用到产品中并为新体验提供动力。

segments.text: And so, I think it’s the same skill really of imagining what it would be like to use it viscerally, and having good taste coming back to earlier.

translation.text: 因此，我认为这与发自内心地想象使用它会是什么样子以及早先拥有良好的品味实际上是相同的技能。

segments.text: The same thing that’s useful in science, I think can also be useful in product design.

translation.text: 我认为在科学中有用的东西在产品设计中也很有用。

segments.text: And I’ve just had a very, always been a sort of multidisciplinary person, so I don’t see the boundaries really between arts and sciences, or product and research.

translation.text: 我有一个非常非常多学科的人，所以我看不到艺术和科学、产品和研究之间的真正界限。

segments.text: It’s a continuum for me. I like working on products that are cutting edge. I wouldn’t be able to have cutting edge technology under the hood.

translation.text: 这对我来说是一个连续体。我喜欢研究前沿的产品。我无法拥有最先进的技术。

segments.text: I wouldn’t be excited about them if they were just run-of-the-mill products. It requires this invention, creativity, cap capability.

translation.text: 如果它们只是普通产品，我不会对它们感到兴奋。它需要这种发明、创造力、盖帽能力。

segments.text: What are some specific things you learned about when you, even on the LLM side, you’re interacting with Gemini? This doesn’t feel like, the layout, the interface, maybe the trade-off between the latency, how to present to the user, how long to wait and how that waiting is shown or the reason capabilities.

translation.text: 具体来说，在大语言模型这边，当你与 Gemini 交互时，你学到了什么？比如布局、界面、延迟之间的权衡，如何向用户呈现、等待多久、如何显示等待状态，以及推理能力等方面。

segments.text: There are some interesting things because like you said, it’s the very cutting edge. We don’t know how to present it correctly.

translation.text: 有一些有趣的事情，因为就像你说的，这是非常前沿的。我们不知道如何正确地呈现它。

segments.text: So is there some specific things you’ve learned?

translation.text: 那么你学到了一些具体的东西吗？

segments.text: I mean it’s such a false evolving space, evaluating this all the time, but where we are today is that you want to continually simplify things, whether that’s the interface or what you build on top of the model, you kind of want to get out of the way of the model.

translation.text: 我的意思是，这是一个错误的不断发展的空间，一直在评估这一点，但我们今天的情况是，你想要不断简化事物，无论是界面还是在模型之上构建的东西，你都想摆脱模型的束缚。

segments.text: The model train is coming down the track and it’s improving unbelievably fast. This relentless progress we talked about earlier.

translation.text: 火车模型正在沿着轨道行驶，并且改进速度快得令人难以置信。我们之前谈到过这种持续不断的进步。

segments.text: You look at 2.5 versus 1.5 and it’s just a gigantic improvement, and we expect that again for the future versions. And so the models are becoming more capable.

translation.text: 你看看 2.5 与 1.5 相比，这是一个巨大的改进，我们希望在未来的版本中再次实现这一点。因此，模型的功能变得越来越强大。

segments.text: So you’ve got, the interesting thing about the design space in today’s world, these AI first products is you’ve got to design not for what the thing can do today, the technology can do today, but in a year’s time.

translation.text: 所以，当今世界设计空间的有趣之处在于，这些人工智能优先的产品是你必须设计的不是今天的东西可以做什么，技术今天可以做什么，而是一年后的事情。

segments.text: So you actually have to be a very technical product person, because you’ve got to have a good intuition for and feel for, okay, that thing that I’m dreaming about now can’t be done today, but is the research track on schedule to basically intercept that in six months or a year’s time.

translation.text: 所以你实际上必须是一个非常技术性的产品人员，因为你必须有很好的直觉和感觉，好吧，我现在梦想的事情今天无法完成，但是按计划进行的研究轨迹基本上可以在六个月或一年的时间内拦截它。

segments.text: So you’ve kind of got to intercept where this highly changing technology’s going, as well as the new capabilities are coming online all the time that we didn’t realize before that can allow these research to work.

translation.text: 因此，你必须了解这种高度变化的技术的发展方向，以及我们之前没有意识到的新功能一直在上线，这可以让这些研究发挥作用。

segments.text: Or now we’ve got video generation, what do we do with that, this multimodal stuff.

translation.text: 或者现在我们有了视频生成功能，我们用它做什么，这种多模式的东西。

segments.text: Is it, one question I have is it really going to be the current UI that we have today, these text box chats? Seems very unlikely once you think about these super multimodal systems.

translation.text: 我的一个问题是，这些文本框聊天真的会成为我们今天拥有的当前用户界面吗？一旦你想到这些超级多式联运系统，这似乎不太可能。

segments.text: Shouldn’t it be something more like Minority Report where you are sort of vibing with it in a kind of collaborative way? It seems very restricted today.

translation.text: 难道它不应该更像《少数派报告》那样，让你以一种协作的方式与它产生共鸣吗？今天似乎受到很大限制。

segments.text: I think we’ll look back on today’s interfaces and products and systems as quite archaic in maybe in just a couple of years.

translation.text: 我认为，也许在短短几年内，我们就会回顾今天的界面、产品和系统，认为它们已经相当过时了。

segments.text: So I think there’s a lot of space actually for innovation to happen on the product side as well as the research side.

translation.text: 所以我认为在产品方面和研究方面实际上都有很大的创新空间。

segments.text: And then we are offline talking about the keyboard is, the open question is how, when and how much will we move to audio as the primary way of interacting with the machines around us versus typing stuff?

translation.text: 然后，我们离线谈论键盘时，悬而未决的问题是，我们将如何、何时以及在多大程度上转向音频作为与我们周围的机器交互的主要方式，而不是打字？

segments.text: Yeah, I mean typing is a very low bandwidth way of doing it, even if you’re a very fast typer. And I think we’re going to have to start utilizing other devices, whether that’s smart glasses, audio earbuds, and eventually maybe some sorts of neural devices, where we can increase the input and the output bandwidth to something maybe a 100x of what is today.

translation.text: 是的，我的意思是打字是一种非常低带宽的方式，即使你打字速度非常快。我认为我们将不得不开始利用其他设备，无论是智能眼镜、音频耳机，还是最终可能的某种神经设备，我们可以将输入和输出带宽增加到今天的 100 倍。

segments.text: I think that underappreciated art form is the interface design because I think you can not unlock the power of the intelligence of a system if you don’t have the right interface.

translation.text: 我认为被低估的艺术形式是界面设计，因为我认为如果没有正确的界面，就无法释放系统智能的力量。

segments.text: The interface is really the way you unlock its power. It’s such an interesting question of how to do that. So how you would think getting out of the way isn’t real art form.

translation.text: 界面确实是您释放其力量的方式。如何做到这一点是一个非常有趣的问题。所以你认为如何让路并不是真正的艺术形式。

segments.text: Yes. It’s the sort of thing that I guess Steve Jobs always talked about, right? It’s simplicity, beauty, and elegance that we want.

translation.text: 是的。我想这是史蒂夫·乔布斯经常谈论的事情，对吧？我们想要的是简单、美丽和优雅。

segments.text: And we’re not that nobody’s there yet, in my opinion. And that’s what I would like us to get to.

translation.text: 在我看来，我们还不是无人问津。这就是我希望我们达到的目标。

segments.text: Again, it sort of speaks to Go again as a game, the most elegant, beautiful game. Can you make an interface as beautiful as that?

translation.text: 再次，它再次将围棋视为一种游戏，最优雅、最美丽的游戏。你能做出这么漂亮的界面吗？

segments.text: Actually, I think we’re going to enter an era of AI-generated interfaces that are probably personalized to you, so it fits the way that you, your aesthetic, your feel, the way that your brain works and the AI kind of generates that depending on the task.

translation.text: 事实上，我认为我们将进入一个人工智能生成界面的时代，这些界面可能是针对你的个性化的，所以它适合你的方式、你的审美、你的感觉、你的大脑工作方式以及人工智能根据任务生成的方式。

segments.text: That feels like that’s probably the direction we’ll end up in.

translation.text: 感觉这可能就是我们最终的方向。

segments.text: Because some people are power users and they want every single parameter on the screen, everything based perhaps me with a keyboard-based navigation and to have shortcuts for everything.

translation.text: 因为有些人是高级用户，他们想要屏幕上的每一个参数，一切都基于键盘导航，并且有一切的快捷方式。

segments.text: And some people like the minimalism.

translation.text: 有些人喜欢极简主义。

segments.text: Just hide all of that complexity. Yeah, exactly.

translation.text: 只是隐藏所有这些复杂性。是的，完全正确。

segments.text: Yeah. Well, I’m glad you have a Steve Jobs mode in you as well. This is great.

translation.text: 是的。嗯，我很高兴你身上也有史蒂夫·乔布斯模式。这太棒了。

segments.text: Einstein mode, Steve Jobs mode.

translation.text: 爱因斯坦模式，史蒂夫·乔布斯模式。

segments.text: All right, let me try to trick you into answering a question. When will Gemini 3 come up? Is it before or after DTS-6?

translation.text: 好吧，让我试着诱导你回答一个问题：Gemini 3 什么时候发布？是在 DTS-6 之前还是之后？

segments.text: The world waits for both.

translation.text: 世界等待着两者。

segments.text: And what does it take to go from 2.5 To 3.0? Because it seems like there’s been a lot of releases of 2.5, which are already leaps in performance.

translation.text: 从 2.5 到 3.0 需要什么？因为2.5似乎已经发布了很多版本，性能已经有了飞跃。

segments.text: So what does it even mean to go to a new version? Is it about performance? Is it about a completely different flavor of an experience?

translation.text: 那么升级到新版本意味着什么呢？与性能有关吗？这是一种完全不同的体验吗？

segments.text: Yeah, well, so the way it works with our different version numbers is we try to collect, so maybe it takes roughly six months or something to do a new kind of full run and the full productization of a new version.

translation.text: 是的，嗯，所以它与我们不同的版本号一起工作的方式是我们尝试收集，所以也许需要大约六个月的时间才能进行新版本的全面运行和完整产品化。

segments.text: And during that time, lots of new interesting research iterations and ideas come up, and we sort of collect them all together that you could imagine the last six months worth of interesting ideas on the architecture front, maybe it’s on the data front, it’s like many different possible things.

translation.text: 在那段时间里，出现了许多新的有趣的研究迭代和想法，我们将它们全部收集在一起，你可以想象过去六个月在架构方面的有趣想法，也许是在数据方面，就像许多不同的可能的事情。

segments.text: And we package that all up, test which ones are likely to be useful for the next iteration, and then bundle that all together. And then we start the new giant hero training run.

translation.text: 我们将它们全部打包，测试哪些可能对下一次迭代有用，然后将它们捆绑在一起。然后我们开始新的巨人英雄训练。

segments.text: And then of course that gets monitored.

translation.text: 当然，这会受到监控。

segments.text: … run, right? And then of course that gets monitored and then at the end of the pre-training, then there’s all the post-training, there’s many different ways of doing that, different ways of patching it.

translation.text: ……跑吧？当然，这会受到监控，然后在预训练结束时，然后是所有的后训练，有很多不同的方法可以做到这一点，有不同的修补方法。

segments.text: So there’s a whole experimental phase there which you can also get a lot of gains out. And that’s where you see the version numbers usually referring to the base model, the pre-trained model, and then the interim versions of 2.5 and the different sizes and the different little additions.

translation.text: 因此，这里有一个完整的实验阶段，您也可以从中获得很多收益。在那里你可以看到版本号，通常指的是基础模型、预训练模型，然后是 2.5 的临时版本以及不同的大小和不同的小添加。

segments.text: They’re often patches or post-training ideas that can be done afterwards off the same basic architecture. And then of course on top of that, we also have different sizes, Pro and Flash and Flashlight that are often distilled from the biggest ones, the Flash model from the Pro model.

translation.text: 它们通常是补丁或训练后的想法，可以在相同的基本架构上完成。当然，除此之外，我们还有不同的尺寸，Pro、Flash 和 Flashlight，它们通常是从最大的型号中提取出来的，Flash 型号是从 Pro 型号中提取出来的。

segments.text: And that means we have a range of different choices. If you’re the developer, do you want to prioritize performance or speed and cost?

translation.text: 这意味着我们有一系列不同的选择。如果您是开发人员，您想优先考虑性能还是速度和成本？

segments.text: And we like to think of this Pareto frontier of on the one hand, the Y-axis is like performance, and then the X- axis is cost or latency and speed basically.

translation.text: 我们喜欢考虑这个帕累托边界，一方面，Y 轴就像性能，然后 X 轴基本上是成本或延迟和速度。

segments.text: And we have models that completely define the frontier. So whatever your trade-off is that you want as an individual user or as a developer, you should find one of our models satisfies that constraint.

translation.text: 我们拥有完全定义边界的模型。因此，无论您作为个人用户还是开发人员想要进行权衡，您都应该发现我们的模型之一满足该约束。

segments.text: So behind the version changes, there is a big run and then there’s just an insane complexity of productization.

translation.text: 因此，在版本更改的背后，有一个大的运行，然后就是产品化的疯狂复杂性。

segments.text: Then there’s the distillation of the different sizes along that Pareto front. And then as with each step you take, you realize there might be a cool product.

translation.text: 然后是沿着帕累托前沿的不同尺寸的蒸馏。然后，随着您迈出的每一步，您都会意识到可能会有一个很酷的产品。

segments.text: There’s side quests.

translation.text: 有支线任务。

segments.text: Yes, exactly.

translation.text: 是的，完全正确。

segments.text: And then you also don’t want to take too many side quests because then you have a million versions and a million products.

translation.text: 然后你也不想接受太多的支线任务，因为这样你就有了一百万个版本和一百万个产品。

segments.text: Yes, precisely.

translation.text: 是的，正是如此。

segments.text: It’s very unclear, but you also get super excited because it’s super cool. How does even look at VLs? Very cool.

translation.text: 虽然还不清楚，但你也会感到非常兴奋，因为它非常酷。如何看待 VL？非常酷。

segments.text: How does it fit into the bigger Thing?

translation.text: 它如何适应更大的事物？

segments.text: Yes, exactly. Exactly. And then you’re constantly this process of converging upstream, we call it ideas from the product surfaces or from the post-training and even further downstream and that, you upstream that into the core model training for the next run.

translation.text: 是的，完全正确。确切地。然后你不断地向上游汇聚的过程，我们称之为来自产品表面或训练后甚至更下游的想法，你将其上游到下一次运行的核心模型训练中。

segments.text: So then the main model, the main Gemini track becomes more and more general and eventually, AGI.

translation.text: 这样一来，主模型，也就是 Gemini 主线，会变得越来越通用，并最终走向 AGI。

segments.text: One hero run.

translation.text: 一位英雄奔跑。

segments.text: Yes, exactly. A few hero runs later.

translation.text: 是的，完全正确。一些英雄随后逃跑。

segments.text: Yeah. So sometimes when you release these new versions or every version, really, are benchmarks productive or counterproductive for showing the performance of a model?

translation.text: 是的。因此，有时当您发布这些新版本或每个版本时，基准测试对于显示模型的性能真的是有效还是适得其反？

segments.text: You need them, but it’s important that you don’t overfit to them. So they shouldn’t be the be all and end all. So there’s LMArena, or it used to be called LEMSYS, that’s one of them that turned out organically to be one of the main ways people like to test these systems, at least the chatbots.

translation.text: 您需要它们，但重要的是不要过度适应它们。所以它们不应该是一切。所以就有了 LMArena，或者它曾经被称为 LEMSYS，它是其中之一，最终成为人们喜欢测试这些系统（至少是聊天机器人）的主要方式之一。

segments.text: Obviously there’s loads of academic benchmarks that test mathematics and coding ability, general language ability, science ability and so on. And then we have our own internal benchmarks that we care about.

translation.text: 显然，有大量的学术基准测试数学和编码能力、通用语言能力、科学能力等。然后我们有自己关心的内部基准。

segments.text: It’s a multi objective optimization problem. You don’t want to be good at just one thing. We’re trying to build general systems that are good across the board, and you try and make no-regret improvements.

translation.text: 这是一个多目标优化问题。你不想只擅长一件事。我们正在尝试构建全面良好的通用系统，并且您尝试做出无悔的改进。

segments.text: So where you improve in coding, but it doesn’t reduce your performance in other areas. So that’s the hard part because of course you could put more coding data in or you could put more, I don’t know, gaming data in, but then does it make worse your language system or your translation systems and other things that you care about?

translation.text: 因此，您在编码方面有所改进，但不会降低您在其他领域的表现。所以这是困难的部分，因为当然你可以放入更多的编码数据，或者你可以放入更多的，我不知道，游戏数据，但是这会让你的语言系统或翻译系统和其他你关心的东西变得更糟吗？

segments.text: So you’ve got to continually monitor this increasingly larger and larger suite of benchmarks. And also when you stick them into products, these models, you also care about the direct usage and the direct stats and the signals that you’re getting from the end users, whether they’re coders or the average person using the chat interfaces.

translation.text: 因此，您必须持续监控这套越来越大的基准测试。而且，当您将它们应用到产品、这些模型中时，您还会关心直接使用情况、直接统计数据以及从最终用户那里获得的信号，无论他们是程序员还是使用聊天界面的普通人。

segments.text: Because ultimately, you want to measure the usefulness, but it’s so hard to convert that into a number. It’s really vibe based benchmarks across a large number of users.

translation.text: 因为最终，您想要衡量有用性，但很难将其转换为数字。这确实是针对大量用户的基于氛围的基准测试。

segments.text: And it’s hard to know and it would be just terrifying to me, you know have a much smarter model, but it’s just something vibe based.

translation.text: 这很难知道，这对我来说会很可怕，你知道有一个更聪明的模型，但这只是基于氛围的东西。

segments.text: It’s not quite working. That’s such a scary and everything you just said. It has to be smart and useful across so many domains.

translation.text: 这不太有效。你刚才所说的一切真是太可怕了。它必须在如此多的领域中智能且有用。

segments.text: So you get super excited all of a sudden solving programming problems you’ve never been able to solve before, but now it’s crappier poetry or something and it’s just, I don’t know, that’s a stressful.

translation.text: 所以你会突然变得超级兴奋，解决你以前从未解决过的编程问题，但现在它是更蹩脚的诗歌或其他东西，只是，我不知道，这是一种压力。

segments.text: That’s so difficult-

translation.text: 这太难了——

segments.text: To balance.

translation.text: 为了平衡。

segments.text: To balance and because you can’t really trust the benchmarks, you really have to trust the end users.

translation.text: 为了平衡，因为你不能真正信任基准，所以你真的必须信任最终用户。

segments.text: Yeah. And then other things that are even more esoteric come into play, like the style of the persona of the system, is it verbose?

translation.text: 是的。然后其他更深奥的事情开始发挥作用，比如系统角色的风格，是否冗长？

segments.text: Is it succinct? Is it humorous? And different people like different things.

translation.text: 是否简洁？是不是很幽默？而且不同的人喜欢不同的东西。

segments.text: So it’s very interesting. It’s almost like cutting edge part of psychology research or personality research. I used to do that in my PhD, like five factor personality, what do we actually want our systems to be like?

translation.text: 所以这很有趣。这几乎就像心理学研究或人格研究的前沿部分。我曾经在我的博士学位中这样做过，就像五因素人格一样，我们实际上希望我们的系统是什么样的？

segments.text: And different people will like different things as well. So these are all just new problems in product space that I don’t think I’ve ever really been tackled before, but we’re going to rapidly have to deal with now.

translation.text: 而且不同的人也会喜欢不同的东西。因此，这些都是产品领域的新问题，我认为以前从未真正解决过这些问题，但我们现在必须迅速解决。

segments.text: I think it’s a super fascinating space, developing the character of the thing and in so doing, it puts a mirror to ourselves, what are the kind of things that we like?

translation.text: 我认为这是一个超级迷人的空间，发展事物的特征，这样做，它给我们自己树立了一面镜子，我们喜欢什么样的事物？

segments.text: Because prompt engineering allows you to control a lot of those elements, but can the product make it easier for you to control the different flavors of those experiences, the different characters that you interact with?

translation.text: 因为即时工程允许您控制其中的许多元素，但是该产品能否使您更轻松地控制这些体验的不同风格以及与您互动的不同角色？

segments.text: Yeah, exactly.

translation.text: 是的，完全正确。

segments.text: So what’s the probability of Google DeepMind winning?

translation.text: 那么 Google DeepMind 获胜的概率有多大呢？

segments.text: Well, I see it as winning. I think winning is the wrong way to look at it given how important and consequential what it is we’re building.

translation.text: 嗯，我认为这是胜利。我认为，考虑到我们正在构建的东西的重要性和影响力，以获胜的方式来看待它是错误的。

segments.text: So funny enough, I try not to view it like a game or competition even though that’s a lot of my mindset. It’s about in my view, all of us or those of us at the leading edge or have a responsibility to steward this unbelievable technology that could be used for incredible good but also has risks, steward it safely into the world for the benefit of humanity.

translation.text: 很有趣的是，我尽量不把它当作一场游戏或比赛，尽管这是我的很多心态。在我看来，我们所有人或我们中处于领先地位的人或有责任管理这项令人难以置信的技术，它可以用于令人难以置信的好处，但也存在风险，为了人类的利益而安全地进入世界。

segments.text: That’s always what I’ve dreamed about and what we’ve always tried to do. And I hope that’s what eventually the community, maybe the international community will rally around when it becomes obvious that as we get closer and closer to AGI, that’s what’s needed.

translation.text: 这一直是我的梦想，也是我们一直努力去做的事情。我希望这就是最终社区，也许国际社会会团结起来，当我们越来越接近通用人工智能时，这就是我们所需要的。

segments.text: I agree with you. I think that’s beautifully put. You’ve said that you talk to and are on good terms with the leads of some of these labs.

translation.text: 我同意你的看法。我认为这说得很漂亮。您说过您与其中一些实验室的领导进行了交谈并保持着良好的关系。

segments.text: As the competition heats up, how hard is it to maintain those relationships?

translation.text: 随着竞争的加剧，维持这些关系有多难？

segments.text: It’s been okay so far. I try to pride myself in being collaborative. I’m a collaborative person.

translation.text: 到目前为止还好。我努力为自己的协作能力感到自豪。我是一个善于合作的人。

segments.text: Research is a collaborative endeavor. Science is a collaborative endeavor. It’s all good for humanity in the end if you cure terrible diseases and you come up with an incredible cure, this is net win for humanity.

translation.text: 研究是一项协作努力。科学是一项协作努力。如果你治愈了可怕的疾病并且想出了令人难以置信的治疗方法，那么最终对人类来说都是有好处的，这对人类来说是净胜利。

segments.text: And the same with energy, all of the things that I’m interested in helping solve with AI. So I just want that technology to exist in the world and be used for the right things and the benefits of that, the productivity benefits of that being shared for the benefit of everyone.

translation.text: 能源也是如此，我有兴趣通过人工智能帮助解决所有问题。所以我只是希望这项技术存在于世界上，并被用于正确的事情，并从中获得好处，为每个人的利益而共享其生产力的好处。

segments.text: So I try to maintain good relations with all the leading lab people. They’re very interesting characters, many of them as you might expect.

translation.text: 因此，我努力与实验室的所有领导人员保持良好的关系。他们都是非常有趣的角色，其中很多都如你所料。

segments.text: Yep.

translation.text: 是的。

segments.text: But yeah, I’m on good terms I hope with pretty much all of them. And I think that’s going to be important when things get even more serious than they are now, that there are those communication channels and that’s what will facilitate cooperation or collaboration if that’s what is required, especially on things like safety.

translation.text: 但是，是的，我希望我和他们所有人的关系都很好。我认为，当事情变得比现在更加严重时，这些沟通渠道将很重要，如果需要的话，这将促进合作或协作，尤其是在安全等方面。

segments.text: Yeah, I hope there’s some collaboration on stuff that’s less high stakes and in so doing, serves as a mechanism for maintaining friendships and relationships. So for example, I think the internet would love it if you and Elon somehow collaborate on creating a video game, that kind of thing.

translation.text: 是的，我希望在风险较低的事情上进行一些合作，这样可以作为维持友谊和关系的机制。举例来说，我认为如果你和埃隆以某种方式合作创建视频游戏之类的事情，互联网会喜欢它。

segments.text: I think that enables camaraderie and good terms. And also you two are legit gamers, so it’s just fun to to create some-

translation.text: 我认为这可以带来友情和良好的关系。而且你们两个都是合法的游戏玩家，所以创造一些东西很有趣-

segments.text: Yeah, that would be awesome. And we’ve talked about that in the past and it may be a cool thing that we can do.

translation.text: 是的，那太棒了。我们过去已经讨论过这一点，我们可以做的这可能是一件很酷的事情。

segments.text: And I agree with you, it’d be nice to have side projects in a way where one can just lean into the collaboration aspect of it and it’s a a win-win for both sides and it builds up that collaborative muscle.

translation.text: 我同意你的观点，如果能有一些副业项目，让人们可以专注于它的协作方面，这对双方来说都是双赢的，并且可以增强协作能力。

segments.text: I see the scientific endeavor as that side project for humanity and I think Google DeepMind has been really pushing that. I would love to see other labs do more scientific stuff and then collaborate because it just seems like it’s easier to collaborate on the big scientific questions.

translation.text: 我将科学事业视为人类的副业项目，并且我认为 Google DeepMind 一直在真正推动这一目标。我很高兴看到其他实验室做更多的科学工作，然后进行合作，因为在重大科学问题上合作似乎更容易。

segments.text: I agree and I would love to see a lot of people, all of the other labs talk about science, but I think we’re really the only ones using it for science and doing that.

translation.text: 我同意，我很乐意看到很多人，所有其他实验室都在谈论科学，但我认为我们确实是唯一将其用于科学并这样做的人。

segments.text: And that’s why projects like AlphaFold are so important to me. And I think to our mission is to show how AI can be clearly used in a very concrete way for the benefit of humanity.

translation.text: 这就是为什么像 AlphaFold 这样的项目对我来说如此重要。我认为我们的使命是展示如何以非常具体的方式明确使用人工智能来造福人类。

segments.text: And also, we spun out companies like Isomorphic off the back of Alpha Fold to do drug discovery and it’s going really well and you can think of build additional AlphaFold type systems to go into chemistry space to help accelerate drug design.

translation.text: 而且，我们在 Alpha Fold 的基础上剥离了像 Isomorphic 这样的公司来进行药物发现，进展非常顺利，你可以考虑构建额外的 AlphaFold 类型系统进入化学领域，以帮助加速药物设计。

segments.text: And the examples I think we need to show and society needs to understand are where AI can bring these huge benefits.

translation.text: 我认为我们需要展示、社会需要理解的例子是人工智能可以带来这些巨大好处的地方。

segments.text: Well, from the bottom of my heart, thank you for pushing the scientific efforts forward with rigor, with fun, with humility, all of it.

translation.text: 好吧，我从心底里感谢你们以严谨、有趣、谦虚的态度推动科学事业的发展。

segments.text: I just love to see and still talking about P equals NP, it’s just incredible. So I love it. There’s been seemingly a war for talent.

translation.text: 我只是喜欢看到并且仍然谈论 P 等于 NP，这真是令人难以置信。所以我喜欢它。似乎存在着一场人才争夺战。

segments.text: Some of it is meme, I don’t know. What do you think about Meta buying up talent with huge salaries and the heating up of this battle for talent?

translation.text: 有些是模因，我不知道。对于Meta高薪收购人才，以及这场人才争夺战的升温，您有何看法？

segments.text: I should say that I think a lot of people see DeepMind as a really great place to do cutting-edge work for the reasons that you’ve outlined.

translation.text: 我应该说，我认为很多人都将 DeepMind 视为进行前沿工作的好地方，原因正如您所概述的。

segments.text: There’s this vibrant scientific culture.

translation.text: 这里有充满活力的科学文化。

segments.text: Yeah. Well look, of course there’s a strategy that Meta is taking right now. I think that from my perspective at least, I think the people that are real believers in the mission of AGI and what it can do and understand the real consequences, both good and bad from that and what that responsibility entails, I think they’re mostly doing it to be like myself,

translation.text: 是的。好吧，Meta 现在当然正在采取一个策略。我认为至少从我的角度来看，我认为那些真正相信 AGI 使命和它能做什么、了解真正后果的人，无论是好还是坏，以及这种责任所带来的后果，我认为他们大多是为了像我自己一样这样做，

segments.text: to be on the frontier of that research so they can help influence the way that goes and steward that technology safely into the world. And Meta right now are not at the frontier.

translation.text: 处于该研究的前沿，这样他们就可以帮助影响研究的方向，并管理该技术安全地进入世界。而 Meta 目前还没有处于前沿。

segments.text: Maybe they’ll manage to get back on there and it’s probably rational what they’re doing from their perspective because they’re behind and they need to do something.

translation.text: 也许他们会设法回到那里，从他们的角度来看，他们所做的事情可能是合理的，因为他们落后了，他们需要做点什么。

segments.text: But I think there’s more important things than just money. Of course one has to pay people their market rates and all of these things and that continues to go up.

translation.text: 但我认为还有比钱更重要的东西。当然，人们必须向人们支付市场价格以及所有这些费用，而且这种情况还在继续上升。

segments.text: And I was expecting this because more and more people are finally realizing, leaders of companies, what I’ve always known for 30 plus years now, which is that AGI is the most important technology probably that’s ever going to be invented.

translation.text: 我对此有所期待，因为越来越多的人、公司领导者终于意识到，我 30 多年来一直知道的一点是，AGI 可能是有史以来最重要的技术。

segments.text: So in some senses, it’s rational to be doing that. But I also think there’s a much bigger question. People in AI these days are very well paid.

translation.text: 所以从某种意义上说，这样做是合理的。但我也认为还有一个更大的问题。现在人工智能领域的人薪水很高。

segments.text: I remember when we were starting out back in 2010, I didn’t even pay myself a couple of years because it wasn’t enough money. We couldn’t raise any money, and these days, interns are being paid the amount that we raised as our first entire seed round.

translation.text: 我记得 2010 年我们刚开始创业时，有几年我什至没有给自己发工资，因为钱不够。我们无法筹集到任何资金，而如今，实习生的工资是我们在第一轮种子轮融资中筹集到的金额。

segments.text: So it’s pretty funny. And I remember the days where I used to have to work for free and almost pay my own way to do an internship.

translation.text: 所以这很有趣。我还记得以前我必须免费工作，几乎要自己掏钱去实习的日子。

segments.text: Right now, it’s all the other around, but that’s just how it is. It’s the new world. But I think that we’ve been discussing what happens post- AGI and energy systems are solved and so on, what is even money going to mean?

translation.text: 现在，情况完全不同，但事实就是如此。这是新世界。但我认为我们一直在讨论通用人工智能和能源系统解决后会发生什么等等，甚至金钱意味着什么？

segments.text: So I think in the economy and we’re going to have much bigger issues to work through and how does the economy function in that world and companies?

translation.text: 因此，我认为在经济领域，我们将有更大的问题需要解决，经济在这个世界和公司中如何运作？

segments.text: So I think it’s a little bit of a side issue about salaries and things like that today.

translation.text: 所以我认为今天关于工资之类的事情有点次要问题。

segments.text: Yeah, when you’re facing such gigantic consequences and gigantic, fascinating scientific questions-

translation.text: 是的，当你面临如此巨大的后果和巨大的、令人着迷的科学问题时——

segments.text: Which may be only a few years away.

translation.text: 这可能只有几年的时间了。

segments.text: So the practicals, the pragmatic sense, if we zoom in on jobs, we can look at programmers because it seems like AI systems are currently doing incredibly well at programming and increasingly so.

translation.text: 因此，从实际情况来看，务实的感觉，如果我们放大工作，我们可以关注程序员，因为人工智能系统目前在编程方面似乎做得非常好，而且越来越好。

segments.text: So A lot of people that program for a living, love programming are worried they will lose their jobs. How worried should they be do you think, and what’s the right way to adjust to the new reality and ensure that you survive and thrive as a human in the programming world?

translation.text: 所以很多以编程为生、热爱编程的人担心他们会失去工作。您认为他们应该有多担心？适应新现实并确保您作为一个人在编程世界中生存和发展的正确方法是什么？

segments.text: Well, it’s interesting that programming, and it’s again counterintuitive to what we thought years ago, maybe that some of the skills that we think of as harder skills are turned out maybe to be the easier ones for various reasons.

translation.text: 嗯，编程很有趣，这又与我们多年前的想法相反，也许我们认为较难的一些技能由于各种原因可能变得更容易。

segments.text: But coding and maths, because you can create a lot of synthetic data and verify if that data’s correct. So because of that nature of that, it’s easier to make things like synthetic data to train from.

translation.text: 但是编码和数学，因为您可以创建大量合成数据并验证这些数据是否正确。因此，由于其本质，更容易制作诸如合成数据之类的数据来进行训练。

segments.text: It’s also an area of course we’re all interested in because as programmers to help us and get faster at it and more productive.

translation.text: 当然，这也是我们都感兴趣的一个领域，因为作为程序员可以帮助我们更快地完成工作并提高工作效率。

segments.text: So I think for the next era, like the next five, 10 years, I think what we’re going to find is people who embrace these technologies become almost at one with them, whether that’s in the creative industries or the technical industries will become superhumanly productive, I think.

translation.text: 因此，我认为在下一个时代，比如未来五年、十年，我认为我们将发现拥抱这些技术的人们几乎与它们融为一体，无论是在创意产业还是在技术产业，我认为都将变得超人的生产力。

segments.text: So the great programmers will be even better, but there’ll be even 10X even what they are today. And because there, you’ll be able to use their skills to utilize the tools to the maximum, exploit them to the maximum.

translation.text: 所以伟大的程序员会更好，但甚至会比现在高出 10 倍。因为在那里，你将能够利用他们的技能来最大限度地利用这些工具，最大限度地利用它们。

segments.text: And so I think that’s what we’re going to see in the next domain. So that’s going to cause quite a lot of change. And so that’s coming.

translation.text: 所以我认为这就是我们将在下一个领域看到的。所以这会带来很大的变化。所以这即将到来。

segments.text: A lot of people benefit from that.

translation.text: 很多人都从中受益。

segments.text: So I think one example of that is if coding becomes easier, it becomes available to many more creatives to do more.

translation.text: 因此，我认为一个例子是，如果编码变得更加容易，那么更多的创意人员就可以做更多的事情。

segments.text: But I think the top programmers will still have huge advantages as terms of specifying, going back to specifying what the architecture should be.

translation.text: 但我认为顶级程序员在指定方面仍然具有巨大的优势，回到指定架构应该是什么。

segments.text: The question should be how to guide these coding assistants in a way that’s useful and check whether the code they produce is good.

translation.text: 问题应该是如何以有用的方式指导这些编码助手并检查他们生成的代码是否良好。

segments.text: So I think there’s plenty of headroom there for the foreseeable next few years.

translation.text: 所以我认为在可预见的未来几年里还有很大的发展空间。

segments.text: So I think there’s several interesting things there. One is there’s a lot of imperative to just get better and better consistently of using these tools so they’re riding the wave of the improving models versus competing against them.

translation.text: 所以我认为那里有一些有趣的事情。一是必须不断地更好地使用这些工具，这样他们才能顺应模型改进的浪潮，而不是与模型竞争。

segments.text: But sadly, but that’s the nature of life on earth, there could be a huge amount of value to certain kinds of programming at the cutting edge and less value to other kinds.

translation.text: 但可悲的是，这就是地球生命的本质，某些前沿的编程可能具有巨大的价值，而其他类型的价值则较小。

segments.text: For example, it could be front-end web design might be more amenable to, as you’ve mentioned, to generation by AI systems and maybe for example, game engine design or something like this or back-end design or guiding systems in high-performance situations, high-performance programming type of design decisions,

translation.text: 例如，正如您所提到的，前端网页设计可能更适合由人工智能系统生成，例如游戏引擎设计或类似的东西，或者高性能情况下的后端设计或指导系统，高性能编程类型的设计决策，

segments.text: that might be extremely valuable. But it will shift where the humans are needed most and that’s scary for people to address.

translation.text: 这可能非常有价值。但它会改变最需要人类的地方，而这对人们来说是可怕的。

segments.text: Yeah, I think that’s right. Anytime where there’s a lot of disruption and change, and we’ve had this, it’s not just this time. We’ve had this in many times in human history with the internet, mobile, but before that obviously, the Industrial Revolution and it’s going to be one of those eras where there will be a lot of change.

translation.text: 是的，我认为这是对的。任何时候都会有很多干扰和变化，我们也遇到过这种情况，但不仅仅是这一次。在人类历史上，我们已经通过互联网、移动设备多次经历过这种情况，但显然在此之前是工业革命，这将是发生很多变化的时代之一。

segments.text: I think there’ll be new jobs we can’t even imagine today, just like the internet created. And then those people with the right skill sets to ride that wave will become incredibly valuable, those skills.

translation.text: 我认为将会出现我们今天无法想象的新工作，就像互联网创造的那样。然后，那些拥有正确技能来驾驭这股浪潮的人将变得非常有价值，这些技能。

segments.text: But maybe people will have to relearn or adapt a bit, their current skills. And the thing that’s going to be harder to deal with this time around is that I think what we’re going to see is something like probably 10 times the impact the Industrial Revolution had, but 10 times faster as well.

translation.text: 但也许人们将不得不重新学习或适应他们当前的技能。这次更难处理的事情是，我认为我们将看到的影响可能是工业革命的 10 倍，但速度也快 10 倍。

segments.text: So instead of a 100 years, it takes 10 years and so that’s going to make, it’s like a 100X, the impact and the speed combined.

translation.text: 因此，它需要 10 年，而不是 100 年，所以这就像 100 倍，影响和速度相结合。

segments.text: So I think going to make it more difficult for society to deal with and there’s a lot to think through and I think we need to be discussing that right now.

translation.text: 所以我认为这会让社会更加难以应对，有很多事情需要思考，我认为我们现在需要讨论这个问题。

segments.text: And I encourage top economists in the world and philosophers to start thinking about how is society going to be affected by this and what should we do?

translation.text: 我鼓励世界顶级经济学家和哲学家开始思考社会将如何受到影响以及我们应该做什么？

segments.text: Including things like universal basic provision or something like that where a lot of the increased productivity gets shared out and distributed to society and maybe in the form of services and other things where if you want more than that,

translation.text: 包括诸如普遍基本供应之类的东西，其中许多提高的生产力被分享并分配给社会，也许以服务和其他东西的形式，如果你想要更多，

segments.text: you still go and get some incredibly rare skills and things like that and make yourself unique. But there’s a basic provision that is provided.

translation.text: 你仍然会去获得一些极其罕见的技能和类似的东西，让自己变得独一无二。但提供了一个基本规定。

segments.text: And if you think of government as a technology, there’s also interesting questions, not just in the economics, but just politics.

translation.text: 如果你将政府视为一种技术，那么就会存在一些有趣的问题，不仅在经济方面，而且在政治方面。

segments.text: How do you design a system that’s responding to the rapidly changing times such that you can represent the different pain that people feel from the different groups and how do you reallocate resources in a way that addresses that pain and represents the hope and the pain and the fears of different people in

translation.text: 如何设计一个能够响应快速变化的时代的系统，以便能够代表不同群体的人们所感受到的不同痛苦，以及如何以一种能够解决这种痛苦并代表不同人群的希望、痛苦和恐惧的方式重新分配资源。
