segments.text: a way that doesn’t lead to division? Because politicians are often really good at fueling the division and using that to get elected, defining the other and then saying that’s bad.

translation.text: 一种不会导致分裂的方式？因为政客们往往非常擅长助长分歧，并利用分歧来当选，定义对方，然后说那很糟糕。

segments.text: And based on that, I think that’s often counterproductive to leveraging a rapidly changing technology to help the world flourish.

translation.text: 基于此，我认为这对于利用快速变化的技术来帮助世界繁荣往往会适得其反。

segments.text: So we almost need to improve our political systems as well rapidly, if you think of them as a technology.

translation.text: 因此，如果你将政治体系视为一项技术，我们几乎也需要迅速改善它们。

segments.text: Definitely. And I think we’ll need new governance structures, institutions probably to help with this transition. So I think political philosophy and political science is going to be key to that.

translation.text: 确实。我认为我们需要新的治理结构和机构来帮助实现这一转变。所以我认为政治哲学和政治学将是实现这一目标的关键。

segments.text: But I think the number one thing, first of all is to create more abundance of resources. So that’s the number one thing.

translation.text: 但我认为第一件事，首先是创造更丰富的资源。所以这是第一件事。

segments.text: Increase productivity, get more resources, maybe eventually get out of the zero-sum situation. Then the second question is how to use those resources and distribute those resources.

translation.text: 提高生产力，获得更多资源，也许最终摆脱零和局面。那么第二个问题就是如何使用这些资源并分配这些资源。

segments.text: But yeah, you can’t do that without having that abundance first.

translation.text: 但是，是的，如果没有足够的资源，你就无法做到这一点。

segments.text: You mentioned to me the book, The Maniac by Benjamin Labatut, a book on first of all about you.

translation.text: 你向我提到了本杰明·拉巴图（Benjamin Labatut）的《疯子》（The Maniac）一书，这本书首先是关于你的。

segments.text: There’s a bio about you.

translation.text: 有一份关于你的简历。

segments.text: Strange, yeah.

translation.text: 奇怪，是的。

segments.text: Yes, sure. It’s unclear how much is fiction, how much is reality. But I think the central figure that is John von Neumann, I would say it’s a haunting and beautiful exploration of madness and genius and let’s say the double-edged sword of discovery.

translation.text: 是的，当然。分不清有多少是虚构的，有多少是现实。但我认为中心人物是约翰·冯·诺依曼，我想说这是对疯狂和天才的一次令人难忘的美丽探索，可以说是发现的双刃剑。

segments.text: And for people who don’t know, John von Neumann is a legendary mind. He contributed to quantum mechanics. He was on the Manhattan Project.

translation.text: 对于那些不知道的人来说，约翰·冯·诺依曼是一位传奇人物。他对量子力学做出了贡献。他参与了曼哈顿计划。

segments.text: He is widely considered to be the father of or pioneer the modern computer and AI and so on. So many people say he’s one of the smartest humans ever, which is fascinating.

translation.text: 他被广泛认为是现代计算机和人工智能等的之父或先驱。很多人说他是有史以来最聪明的人之一，这很有趣。

segments.text: And what’s also fascinating is he’s a person who saw nuclear science and physics become the atomic bomb, so you got to see ideas become a thing that has a huge amount of impact on the world.

translation.text: 同样令人着迷的是，他见证了核科学和物理学成为原子弹，所以你必须看到想法成为对世界产生巨大影响的事物。

segments.text: He also foresaw the same thing for computing, and that’s a little bit again, beautiful and haunting aspect of the book.

translation.text: 他还预见到计算也会发生同样的事情，这又是这本书美丽而令人难以忘怀的方面。

segments.text: Then taking a leap forward and looking at this, at least it all AlphaZero, AlphaGo AlphaZero big moment that maybe John von Neumann’s thinking was brought to reality.

translation.text: 然后向前迈进看看这一点，至少这一切都是AlphaZero、AlphaGo AlphaZero的伟大时刻，也许约翰·冯·诺依曼的想法被变成了现实。

segments.text: So I guess the question is what do you think if you got to hang out with John von Neumann now, what would he say about what’s going on?

translation.text: 所以我想问题是，如果你现在和约翰·冯·诺依曼一起出去玩，你怎么想，他会对正在发生的事情说些什么？

segments.text: Well, that would be an amazing experience. He’s a fantastic mind. And I also love the way he spent a lot of his time at Princeton at the Institute of Advanced Studies, a very special place for thinking.

translation.text: 嗯，那将是一次奇妙的经历。他的头脑非常出色。我也喜欢他在普林斯顿大学高级研究院度过的大部分时间，这是一个非常特别的思考场所。

segments.text: And it’s amazing how much of a polymath he was and the spread of things he helped invent, including of course the Von Neumann architecture that all the modern computers are based on.

translation.text: 令人惊讶的是，他是一位博学多才的人，他帮助发明的东西得以传播，当然包括所有现代计算机所基于的冯·诺依曼架构。

segments.text: And he had amazing foresight. I think he would’ve loved where we are today, and I think he would’ve really enjoyed AlphaGo being, he did game theory.

translation.text: 他有惊人的远见。我认为他会喜欢我们今天的处境，我认为他会非常喜欢 AlphaGo，他研究博弈论。

segments.text: I think he foresaw a lot of what would happen with learning machines, systems that are grown, I think he called it rather than programmed.

translation.text: 我认为他预见到了学习机器和成长的系统会发生的很多事情，我认为他称之为而不是编程。

segments.text: I’m not sure how even maybe he wouldn’t even be that surprised. There’s the fruition of what I think he already foresaw in the 1950s.

translation.text: 我不确定他怎么可能不会那么惊讶。我认为他在 20 世纪 50 年代就已经预见到了这一点。

segments.text: I wonder what advice he would give. He got to see the building of the atomic bomb with the Manhattan Project. I’m sure there’s interesting stuff that maybe is not talked about enough, maybe some bureaucratic aspect, maybe the influence of politicians, maybe not enough of picking up the phone and talking to people that are called enemies by the said politicians.

translation.text: 我想知道他会给出什么建议。他通过曼哈顿计划亲眼目睹了原子弹的制造。我确信有一些有趣的事情可能没有被充分讨论，也许是一些官僚方面，也许是政客的影响，也许是没有足够的拿起电话与那些被政客称为敌人的人交谈。

segments.text: There might be some deep wisdom that we just may have lost from that time actually.

translation.text: 实际上，我们可能从那时起就失去了一些深刻的智慧。

segments.text: Yeah, I’m sure there is. I read a lot of books for that time as well, Chronicle Time and some brilliant people involved.

translation.text: 是的，我确信有。我也读了很多关于那个时期的书，《编年史》和一些参与其中的杰出人物。

segments.text: But I agree with you. I think maybe there needs to be more dialogue and understanding. I hope we can learn from those times.

translation.text: 但我同意你的观点。我认为也许需要更多的对话和理解。我希望我们能从那些时代中吸取教训。

segments.text: I think the difference here is that the AI has so many, it’s a multi-use technology. Obviously we’re trying to do things like solve all diseases, help with energy and scarcity, these incredible things.

translation.text: 我认为这里的区别在于人工智能有很多，它是一种多用途技术。显然，我们正在努力做一些事情，比如解决所有疾病、帮助解决能源和稀缺问题，这些令人难以置信的事情。

segments.text: This is why all of us and myself, I started on this journey 30 plus years ago. But of course there are risks too.

translation.text: 这就是为什么我们所有人和我自己在 30 多年前开始了这段旅程。但当然也存在风险。

segments.text: And probably Von Neumann, my guess is he foresaw both. And I think he said, I think it’s to his wife, that computers would be even more impactful in the world.

translation.text: 我猜冯·诺依曼可能预见到了这两点。我想他是对他的妻子说的，计算机会对世界产生更大的影响。

segments.text: And as we just discussed, I think that’s right. I think it’s going to be 10 times at least of the Industrial Revolution.

translation.text: 正如我们刚才讨论的，我认为这是对的。我认为这至少是工业革命时期的 10 倍。

segments.text: So I think he’s right. So I think he would’ve been, I imagine, fascinated by where we are now.

translation.text: 所以我认为他是对的。所以我想他会对我们现在的处境着迷。

segments.text: And I think one of the, maybe you can correct me, but one of the takeaways from the book is that reason, as said in the book, Mad Dreams of Reason, it’s not enough for guiding humanity as we build these super powerful technology.

translation.text: 我认为其中之一，也许你可以纠正我，但这本书的要点之一是，正如《理性的疯狂梦想》一书中所说，当我们构建这些超级强大的技术时，这不足以指导人类。

segments.text: That there’s something else. There’s also a religious component, whatever God, whatever religion gives, it pulls at something in the human spirit that raw cold reason doesn’t give us.

translation.text: 那还有别的东西。还有一个宗教成分，无论上帝、无论宗教给予什么，它都会在人类精神中吸引一些原始冷酷的理性无法给予我们的东西。

segments.text: And I agree with that. I think we need to approach it with whatever you want to call it, a spiritual dimension or humanist dimension. Doesn’t have to be to do with religion, but this idea of a soul, what makes us human, this spark that we have, perhaps it’s to do with consciousness when we finally understand that, I think that has to be at the heart of the endeavor.

translation.text: 我同意这一点。我认为我们需要用任何你想称之为的东西来处理它，精神维度或人文主义维度。不一定与宗教有关，但灵魂的概念，是什么让我们成为人类，我们拥有的这种火花，也许与意识有关，当我们最终理解这一点时，我认为这必须是努力的核心。

segments.text: And technology, I’ve always seen technology as the enabler, the tools that enable us to flourish and to understand more about the world. And I’m with Feynman on this, and he used to always talk about science and art being companions.

translation.text: 至于技术，我一直将技术视为推动者，是使我们能够蓬勃发展并更多地了解世界的工具。在这一点上我同意费曼，他过去总是谈论科学和艺术是伴侣。

segments.text: You can understand it from both sides, the beauty of a flower, how beautiful it is, and also understand why the colors of the flower evolve like that.

translation.text: 你可以从两个方面来理解，一朵花的美丽，它有多美丽，也可以理解为什么花的颜色会这样演变。

segments.text: That just makes it more beautiful, just the intrinsic beauty of the flower.

translation.text: 这只会让它变得更加美丽，这就是花的内在美。

segments.text: I’ve always seen it like that. And maybe in the Renaissance times, the great discoverers then, people like Da Vinci, I don’t think he saw any difference between science and art and perhaps religion.

translation.text: 我一直都是这样看的。也许在文艺复兴时期，伟大的发现者，像达芬奇这样的人，我认为他没有看到科学和艺术，也许还有宗教之间有任何区别。

segments.text: Everything was, it’s just part of being human and being inspired about the world around us. And that’s the philosophy I tried to take.

translation.text: 一切都是，这只是人类的一部分，也是我们受到周围世界启发的一部分。这就是我试图遵循的哲学。

segments.text: And one of my favorite philosophers is Spinoza. And I think he combined that all very well, this idea of trying to understand the universe and understanding our place in it.

translation.text: 我最喜欢的哲学家之一是斯宾诺莎。我认为他很好地将这一切结合起来，试图理解宇宙并理解我们在其中的地位。

segments.text: And that was his way of understanding religion. And I think that’s quite beautiful. And for me, all of these things are related, interrelated, the technology and what it means to be human.

translation.text: 这就是他理解宗教的方式。我认为这非常漂亮。对我来说，所有这些事情都是相关的、相互关联的、技术以及人类的意义。

segments.text: And I think it’s very important though that we remember that as when we’re immersed in the technology and the research, I think a lot of researchers that I see in our field are a little bit too narrow and only understand the technology.

translation.text: 我认为非常重要的是，我们要记住，当我们沉浸在技术和研究中时，我认为我在我们的领域看到的很多研究人员都有点太狭隘了，只了解技术。

segments.text: And I think also that’s why it’s important for this to be debated by society at large. I’m very supportive of things like the AI summits that will happen and governments understanding it.

translation.text: 我认为这也是为什么整个社会对此进行辩论很重要的原因。我非常支持即将举行的人工智能峰会以及政府的理解等事情。

segments.text: And I think that’s one good thing about the chatbot era and the product era of AI is that everyday person can actually feel and interact with cutting edge AI and feel it for themselves.

translation.text: 我认为聊天机器人时代和人工智能产品时代的一件好事是，每个人都可以真正感受到尖端人工智能并与之互动，并亲自感受它。

segments.text: Yeah, because they force the technologists to have the human conversation. Yeah, for sure.

translation.text: 是的，因为他们迫使技术人员进行人际对话。是的，当然。

segments.text: Yeah.

translation.text: 是的。

segments.text: That’s the hopeful aspect of it, like you said, it’s a dual use technology that we’re forcefully integrating the entire humanity into it, into the discussion about AI because ultimately AI, AGI will be used for things that states use technologies for, which is conflict and so on.

translation.text: 这就是它充满希望的一面，就像你说的，这是一项双重用途技术，我们正在强力地将整个人类融入其中，融入到关于人工智能的讨论中，因为最终人工智能、通用人工智能将用于那些需要使用技术的事情，比如冲突等等。

segments.text: And the more we integrate humans into this picture by having chats with them, the more we will guide.

translation.text: 我们越是通过与人类聊天将人类融入到这幅图画中，我们就越能引导。

segments.text: Yeah, be able to adapt, society will be able to adapt to these technologies we’ve always done in the past with the incredible technologies we’ve invented in the past.

translation.text: 是的，能够适应，社会将能够适应我们过去一直在做的这些技术，以及我们过去发明的令人难以置信的技术。

segments.text: Do you think there will be something like a Manhattan Project where there will be an escalation of the power of this technology and states in their old way of thinking, we’ll try to use it as weapons technologies and there will be this escalation?

translation.text: 你是否认为会有像曼哈顿计划这样的事情，这种技术的力量将会升级，并以他们的旧思维方式表明，我们将尝试将其用作武器技术，并且这种升级将会发生？

segments.text: I hope not. I think that would be very dangerous to do. And I think also not the right use of the technology.

translation.text: 我希望不会。我认为这样做是非常危险的。而且我认为也没有正确使用该技术。

segments.text: I hope we’ll end up with something more collaborative if needed, more like a CERN project where it’s research-focused and the best minds in the world come together to carefully complete the final steps and make sure it’s responsibly done before deploying it to the world.

translation.text: 我希望如果需要的话，我们最终会得到更具协作性的东西，更像是一个以研究为重点的 CERN 项目，世界上最优秀的人才聚集在一起，仔细完成最后的步骤，并确保在将其部署到世界之前以负责任的方式完成。

segments.text: We’ll see. It’s difficult with the current geopolitical climate, I think, to see cooperation, but things can change. And I think at least on the scientific level, it’s important for the researchers to keep in touch and keep close to each other at least on those kinds of topics.

translation.text: 我们会看到的。我认为，在当前的地缘政治气候下，很难看到合作，但事情可以改变。我认为至少在科学层面上，研究人员至少在这些话题上保持联系和密切是很重要的。

segments.text: And I personally believe on the education side and immigration side, it would be great if both directions, people from the West immigrated to China and China, back.

translation.text: 我个人认为，在教育方面和移民方面，如果两个方向，西方人移民到中国，中国再回来，那就太好了。

segments.text: There is some family human aspect of people just intermixing and thereby those ties grow strong. So you can’t divide against each other, this old school way of thinking.

translation.text: 人们混合在一起有一些家庭人性的一面，因此这些联系会变得牢固。所以你们不能互相分裂，这是老派的思维方式。

segments.text: And so multicultural, multidisciplinary research teams working on scientific questions, that’s like the hope. Don’t let the leaders that are warmongers divide us.

translation.text: 因此，多元文化、多学科的研究团队致力于科学问题，这就是我们的希望。不要让好战的领导人分裂我们。

segments.text: I think science is the ultimately really beautiful connector.

translation.text: 我认为科学是最终真正美丽的连接器。

segments.text: Yeah, science has always been, I think, quite a very collaborative endeavor and scientists know that it’s a collective endeavor as well, and we can all learn from each other.

translation.text: 是的，我认为科学一直是一项非常协作的事业，科学家们知道这也是一项集体的事业，我们都可以互相学习。

segments.text: So perhaps it could be a vector to get a bit of cooperation.

translation.text: 所以也许它可以成为获得一些合作的载体。

segments.text: Ridiculous question, what’s your P-Doom? Probability of the human civilization destroys itself?

translation.text: 可笑的问题，你的 P-Doom 是什么？人类文明自我毁灭的可能性有多大？

segments.text: Well, look, I don’t have a P-Doom number. The reason I don’t is because I think it would imply a level of precision that is not there.

translation.text: 好吧，看，我没有 P-Doom 号码。我不这样做的原因是因为我认为这意味着不存在的精确度。

segments.text: So I don’t know how people are getting their P-Doom numbers. I think it’s a little bit of ridiculous notion because what I would say is it’s definitely non-zero and it’s probably non-negligible.

translation.text: 所以我不知道人们是如何获得他们的 P-Doom 号码的。我认为这是一个有点荒谬的想法，因为我想说的是它绝对是非零的，而且可能是不可忽略的。

segments.text: So that in itself is pretty sobering. And my view is it’s just hugely uncertain what these technologies are going to be able to do, how fast are they going to take off, how controllable are they going to be.

translation.text: 所以这本身就很发人深省。我的观点是，这些技术能够做什么、它们的发展速度有多快、它们的可控性如何，都非常不确定。

segments.text: Some things may turn out to be, and hopefully way easier than we thought, but it may be there’s some really hard problems that are harder than we guessed today, and I think we don’t know that for sure.

translation.text: 有些事情可能会变得比我们想象的要容易得多，但可能有一些非常困难的问题比我们今天猜测的更难，我认为我们不确定这一点。

segments.text: And so under those conditions of a lot of uncertainty, but huge stakes both ways.

translation.text: 因此，在充满不确定性的情况下，双方都面临着巨大的风险。

segments.text: On the one hand, we could solve all diseases, energy problems, the scarcity problem, and then travel to the stars and conscious of the stars and maximum human flourishing.

translation.text: 一方面，我们可以解决所有疾病、能源问题、稀缺问题，然后前往星星并意识到星星和人类的最大繁荣。

segments.text: On the other hand, is these P-Doom scenarios. So given the uncertainty around it and the importance of it, it’s clear to me the only rational, sensible approach is to proceed with cautious optimism.

translation.text: 另一方面，就是这些P-Doom场景。因此，考虑到它的不确定性及其重要性，我清楚唯一理性、明智的方法就是保持谨慎乐观的态度。

segments.text: So we want the benefits of course, and all of the amazing things that AI can bring. And actually, I would be really worried for humanity given the other challenges that we have, climate, aging, resources, all of that if I didn’t know something like AI was coming down the line.

translation.text: 所以我们当然想要人工智能带来的好处以及所有令人惊奇的事情。事实上，如果我不知道像人工智能这样的东西即将出现，考虑到我们面临的其他挑战，气候、老龄化、资源，所有这些，我真的会为人类担心。

segments.text: How would we solve all those other problems? I think it’s hard. So I think it could be amazingly transformative for good.

translation.text: 我们如何解决所有其他问题？我认为这很难。所以我认为它可能会带来令人惊奇的永久变革。

segments.text: But on the other hand, there are these risks that we know are there.

translation.text: 但另一方面，我们知道存在这些风险。

segments.text: But on the other hand, there are these risks that we know are there, but we can’t quite quantify. So the best thing to do is to use the scientific method to do more research to try and more precisely define those risks and of course address them.

translation.text: 但另一方面，我们知道存在这些风险，但无法完全量化。因此，最好的办法是使用科学方法进行更多研究，尝试更准确地定义这些风险，当然还要解决它们。

segments.text: And I think that’s what we’re doing. I think there probably needs to be 10 times more effort of that than there is now as we are getting closer and closer to the AGI line.

translation.text: 我认为这就是我们正在做的事情。我认为，随着我们越来越接近 AGI 线，可能需要比现在多付出 10 倍的努力。

segments.text: What would be the source of worry for you more? Would it be human-caused or AI, AGI caused? Are humans abusing that technology versus AGI itself through mechanism that you’ve spoken about, which is fascinating, deception or this kind of stuff getting better and better and better secretly and then escapes?

translation.text: 您更担心的是什么？是人为造成的还是人工智能、通用人工智能造成的？人类是否通过你所说的令人着迷的机制滥用该技术而不是 AGI 本身，是欺骗还是这种东西秘密地变得越来越好然后逃跑？

segments.text: I think they operate over different timescales and they’re equally important to address. So there’s just the common garden variety of bad actors using new technology, in this case, general purpose technology and repurposing it for harmful end.

translation.text: 我认为它们在不同的时间范围内运作，并且解决它们同样重要。因此，只有常见的不良行为者使用新技术，在这种情况下，通用技术并将其重新用于有害目的。

segments.text: And that’s a huge risk and I think that has a lot of complications because generally I’m in huge favor of open science and open source, and in fact, we did it with all our science projects like AlphaFold and all of those things for the benefit of the scientific community.

translation.text: 这是一个巨大的风险，我认为这会带来很多复杂性，因为一般来说我非常支持开放科学和开源，事实上，我们在所有科学项目（如 AlphaFold）中都做到了这一点，所有这些都是为了科学界的利益。

segments.text: But how does one restrict bad actors access to these powerful systems, whether they’re individuals or even rogue states, but enable access at the same time to good actors to maximally build on top of?

translation.text: 但是，如何限制不良行为者访问这些强大的系统，无论他们是个人还是流氓国家，但同时允许好行为者访问这些强大的系统，以最大程度地在此基础上进行构建？

segments.text: It’s pretty tricky problem that I’ve not heard a clear solution to. So there’s the bad actor use case problem, and then there’s obviously, as the systems become more agentic and closer to AGI and more autonomous, how do we ensure the guardrails and they stick to what we want them to do and under our control?

translation.text: 这是个非常棘手的问题，我还没有听到明确的解决方案。一方面是恶意行为者滥用的问题；另一方面，随着系统越来越智能体化、越来越接近 AGI，也越来越自主，我们怎样确保安全护栏有效，让系统始终按照我们的意图运行并处于我们的控制之下？

segments.text: Yeah, I tend to, maybe my mind is limited, worry more about the humans, so the bad actors. And there it could be in part how do you not put destructive technology in the hands of bad actors, but in another part from, again, geopolitical technology perspective, how do you reduce the number of bad actors in the world?

translation.text: 是的，我倾向于，也许我的思想是有限的，更多地担心人类，所以担心坏演员。一方面，如何不让破坏性技术落入坏人手中；另一方面，从地缘政治技术的角度来看，如何减少世界上坏人的数量？

segments.text: That’s also an interesting human problem.

translation.text: 这也是一个有趣的人类问题。

segments.text: Yeah, it’s a hard problem. I mean, look, we can maybe also use the technology itself to help early warning on some of the bad actor use cases, right?

translation.text: 是的，这是一个难题。我的意思是，看，我们也许还可以使用该技术本身来帮助对一些不良行为者用例进行早期预警，对吗？

segments.text: Whether that’s bio or nuclear or whatever it is, AI could be potentially helpful there as long as the AI that you’re using is itself reliable, right?

translation.text: 无论是生物、核还是其他什么，只要你使用的人工智能本身可靠，人工智能就可能有潜在的帮助，对吗？

segments.text: So it’s a sort of interlocking problem and that’s what makes it very tricky. And again, it may require some agreement internationally, at least between China and the U.S. of some basic standards.

translation.text: 所以这是一种环环相扣的问题，这也是它非常棘手的原因。同样，这可能需要国际上达成一些协议，至少在中国和美国之间就一些基本标准达成一致。

segments.text: Right.

translation.text: 正确的。

segments.text: I have to ask you about the book, The Maniac. There’s the hand of God moment, Lee Sedol’s move 78 that perhaps the last time a human did a move of pure human genius and beat AlphaGo or broke its brain.

translation.text: 我得问你关于《疯子》这本书的事。这是上帝之手的时刻，李世石的第 78 步棋可能是人类最后一次做出纯粹人类天才的棋步并击败 AlphaGo 或打破它的大脑。

segments.text: Yes.

translation.text: 是的。

segments.text: Sorry to anthropomorphize, but it’s an interesting moment because I think in so many domains it will keep happening.

translation.text: 抱歉将其拟人化，但这是一个有趣的时刻，因为我认为在很多领域它都会继续发生。

segments.text: Yeah, it’s a special moment and it was great for Lee Sedol. I think it’s in a way they were inspiring each other.

translation.text: 是的，这是一个特殊的时刻，对李世石来说很棒。我认为他们在某种程度上互相激励。

segments.text: We as a team were inspired by Lee Sedol’s brilliance and nobleness. Then maybe he got inspired by what AlphaGo was doing to then conjure this incredible inspirational moment, captured very well in the documentary about it.

translation.text: 我们作为一个团队被李世石的才华和高贵所鼓舞。然后也许他受到 AlphaGo 所做的事情的启发，然后召唤出这个令人难以置信的鼓舞人心的时刻，这在有关它的纪录片中得到了很好的捕捉。

segments.text: And I think that’ll continue in many domains where there’s this, at least again for the foreseeable future, of the humans bringing in their ingenuity and asking the right question, let’s say, and then utilizing these tools in a way that then cracks a problem.

translation.text: 我认为这种情况将在许多领域继续下去，至少在可预见的未来，人类会发挥自己的聪明才智，提出正确的问题，然后以某种方式利用这些工具来解决问题。

segments.text: Yeah. As the AI become smarter and smarter, one of the interesting questions we can ask ourselves is what makes humans special?

translation.text: 是的。随着人工智能变得越来越聪明，我们可以问自己的一个有趣的问题是，是什么让人类如此特别？

segments.text: It does feel perhaps biased that we humans are deeply special. I don’t know if it’s our intelligence, it could be something else, that other thing that’s outside the mad dreams of reason.

translation.text: 认为我们人类非常特殊，这确实让人感觉有些偏见。我不知道这是否是我们的智慧，也可能是别的东西，那些超出理性疯狂梦想之外的东西。

segments.text: I think that’s what I’ve always imagined when I was a kid and starting on this journey of I was of course fascinated by things like consciousness, did a neuroscience PhD to look at how the brain works, especially imagination and memory.

translation.text: 我想这就是我小时候一直想象的事情，开始这段旅程时，我当然对意识之类的东西很着迷，攻读了神经科学博士学位，研究大脑如何工作，尤其是想象力和记忆力。

segments.text: I focused on the hippocampus and it’s sort of going to be interesting. I always thought the best way, of course, one can philosophize about it and have thought experiments and maybe even do actual experiments like you do in neuroscience on real brains.

translation.text: 我专注于海马体，这会很有趣。当然，我一直认为最好的方法是人们可以对它进行哲学思考并进行思想实验，甚至可能像神经科学中在真实大脑上所做的那样进行实际实验。

segments.text: But in the end, I always imagine that building AI, a kind of intelligent artifact, and then comparing that to the human mind and seeing what the differences were would be the best way to uncover what’s special about the human mind, if indeed there is anything special.

translation.text: 但最后，我总是想象，构建人工智能，一种智能制品，然后将其与人类思维进行比较，看看其中的差异，这将是发现人类思维的特别之处（如果确实有什么特别的话）的最佳方法。

segments.text: And I suspect there probably is, but it’s going to be hard to… I think this journey we’re on will help us understand that and define that.

translation.text: 我怀疑可能存在，但很难……我认为我们正在进行的这段旅程将帮助我们理解并定义它。

segments.text: And there may be a difference between carbon based substrates that we are and silicon ones when they process information. One of the best definitions I like of consciousness is it’s the way information feels when we process it, right?

translation.text: 我们的碳基基板和硅基基板在处理信息时可能存在差异。我喜欢的意识最好的定义之一是它是我们处理信息时感受到的方式，对吗？

segments.text: Yeah.

translation.text: 是的。

segments.text: It could be. I mean, it’s not a very helpful scientific explanation, but I think it’s kind of interesting intuitive one. And so on this journey, this scientific journey we’re on will I think help uncover that mystery.

translation.text: 可能是。我的意思是，这不是一个非常有用的科学解释，但我认为这是一种有趣的直观解释。因此，在这次旅程中，我认为我们正在进行的科学之旅将有助于揭开这个谜团。

segments.text: Yeah. What I cannot create, I do not understand. That’s somebody you deeply admire, Richard Feynman, like you mentioned.

translation.text: 是的。我无法创造的东西，我就不明白。正如你提到的，理查德·费曼是你深深敬佩的人。

segments.text: You also reach for the Wigner’s dreams of universality that he saw in constrained domains, but also broadly generally in mathematics and so on.

translation.text: 你还可以实现维格纳的普遍性梦想，他在有限的领域看到了这种普遍性，但也广泛地在数学等领域看到了这一点。

segments.text: So many aspects on which you’re pushing towards not to start trouble at the end, but Roger Penrose.

translation.text: 你在很多方面都在努力争取不要在最后引发麻烦，但罗杰·彭罗斯。

segments.text: Yes. Okay.

translation.text: 是的。好的。

segments.text: So do you think consciousness, there’s this hard problem of consciousness, how information feels. Do you think consciousness, first of all, is a computation?

translation.text: 那么你认为意识吗？意识有一个难题，信息如何感觉。你认为意识首先是一种计算吗？

segments.text: And if is, if it’s information processing, like you said, everything is, is it something that could be modeled by a classical computer?

translation.text: 如果是的话，如果是信息处理，就像你说的，一切都是，它是可以用经典计算机建模的东西吗？

segments.text: Yeah.

translation.text: 是的。

segments.text: Or is it a quantum mechanical in nature?

translation.text: 或者它本质上是量子力学吗？

segments.text: Well, look, Penrose is an amazing thinker, one of the greatest of the modern era, and we’ve had a lot of discussions about this.

translation.text: 嗯，看，彭罗斯是一位了不起的思想家，是现代最伟大的思想家之一，我们对此进行了很多讨论。

segments.text: Of course, we cordially disagree, which is I feel like… I mean, he collaborated with a lot of good neuroscientists to see if he could find mechanisms for quantum mechanics behavior in the brain.

translation.text: 当然，我们真诚地不同意，我觉得……我的意思是，他与许多优秀的神经科学家合作，看看他是否能找到大脑中量子力学行为的机制。

segments.text: And to my knowledge, they haven’t found anything convincing yet. So my betting is that it’s mostly it is just classical computing that’s going on in the brain, which suggests that all the phenomena are modelable or mimicable by a classical computer.

translation.text: 据我所知，他们还没有找到任何令人信服的证据。所以我敢打赌，这主要是大脑中正在进行的经典计算，这表明所有现象都可以由经典计算机建模或模仿。

segments.text: But we’ll see. There may be this final mysterious things of the feeling of consciousness, the qualia, these kinds of things that philosophers debate where it’s unique to the substrate.

translation.text: 但我们会看到。可能存在意识感觉、感受性的最终神秘事物，哲学家们争论这些事物的独特之处。

segments.text: We may even come towards understanding that when if we do things like neural link or have neural interfaces to the AI systems, which I think we probably will eventually, maybe to keep up with the AI systems, we might actually be able to feel for ourselves what it’s like to compute on silicon, right?

translation.text: 我们甚至可能会认识到，当我们做诸如神经链接之类的事情或与人工智能系统建立神经接口时（我认为我们最终可能会跟上人工智能系统的步伐），我们实际上可能能够亲自感受到在硅上计算的感觉，对吗？

segments.text: And maybe that will tell us. So I think it’s going to be interesting. I had a debate once with the late Daniel Dennett about why do we think each other are conscious?

translation.text: 也许这会告诉我们。所以我认为这会很有趣。我曾与已故的丹尼尔·丹尼特争论过，为什么我们认为彼此是有意识的？

segments.text: Okay, so it’s for two reasons. One is you’re exhibiting the same behavior that I am. So that’s one thing.

translation.text: 好吧，这有两个原因。一是你表现出和我一样的行为。所以这是一回事。

segments.text: Behaviorally you seem like a conscious being if I am.

translation.text: 从行为上来说，你看起来像是一个有意识的存在，如果我是的话。

segments.text: But the second thing which is often overlooked is that we’re running on the same substrate. So if you’re behaving in the same way and we’re running on the same substrate, it’s most parsimonious to assume you’re feeling the same experience that I’m feeling.

translation.text: 但经常被忽视的第二件事是我们在同一基板上运行。因此，如果你的行为方式相同并且我们在相同的基础上运行，那么假设你的感受与我的感受相同是最节俭的。

segments.text: But with an AI that’s on silicon, we won’t be able to rely on the second part, even if it exhibits the first part, that behavior looks like a behavior of a conscious being.

translation.text: 但是对于硅上的人工智能，我们将无法依赖第二部分，即使它展示了第一部分，该行为看起来像是有意识的行为。

segments.text: It might even claim it is, but we wouldn’t know how it actually felt and it probably couldn’t know what we felt, at least in the first stages.

translation.text: 它甚至可能声称是这样，但我们不知道它的实际感受如何，它可能也不知道我们的感受，至少在第一阶段是这样。

segments.text: Maybe when we get to superintelligence and the technologies that builds, perhaps we’ll be able to bridge that.

translation.text: 也许当我们掌握超级智能和构建技术时，也许我们能够弥合这个问题。

segments.text: No, I mean that’s a huge test for radical empathy is to empathize with a different substrate.

translation.text: 不，我的意思是，这是对激进同理心的一个巨大考验，就是对不同的基质产生同理心。

segments.text: Right. Exactly. We’ve never had to confront that before.

translation.text: 正确的。确切地。我们以前从未遇到过这个问题。

segments.text: Yeah. So maybe through brain computer interfaces be able to truly empathize what it feels like to be a computer, to compute.

translation.text: 是的。因此，也许通过脑机接口能够真正体会作为一台计算机进行计算的感觉。

segments.text: Well, for information to be computed not on a carbon system.

translation.text: 好吧，对于不在碳系统上计算的信息。

segments.text: I mean, that’s deeply… Some people kind of think about that with plants, with other life forms which are different.

translation.text: 我的意思是，这很深……有些人会思考植物和其他不同生命形式的问题。

segments.text: Yes, it could be exactly.

translation.text: 是的，可能确实如此。

segments.text: Similar substrate, but sufficiently far enough on the evolutionary tree that it requires a radical empathy, but to do that with a computer.

translation.text: 类似的基础，但在进化树上足够远，需要彻底的同理心，但要用计算机来做到这一点。

segments.text: I mean, look, there are animal studies on this. Of course, higher animals like killer whales and dolphins and dogs and monkeys, they have some, and elephants, they have some aspects certainly of consciousness, right?

translation.text: 我的意思是，看，有这方面的动物研究。当然，像虎鲸、海豚、狗和猴子这样的高等动物，它们有一些，而大象，它们肯定有某些方面的意识，对吧？

segments.text: Even though they might not be that smart on an IQ sense. So we can already empathize with that and maybe even some of our systems one day, like we built this thing called DolphinGemma, which a version of our system was trained on dolphin and whale sounds, and maybe we’ll be able to build an interpreter or translator at some point which would be pretty cool.

translation.text: 尽管他们的智商可能并不那么聪明。所以我们已经可以理解这一点，甚至有一天我们的一些系统，比如我们构建了一个名为 DolphinGemma 的东西，我们系统的一个版本是用海豚和鲸鱼的声音进行训练的，也许我们将能够在某个时候构建一个口译员或笔译员，这将是非常酷的。

segments.text: What gives you hope for the future of human civilization?

translation.text: 是什么让您对人类文明的未来充满希望？

segments.text: Well, what gives me hope is that I think our almost limitless ingenuity, first of all. I think the best of us and the best human minds are incredible.

translation.text: 嗯，给我带来希望的是，我认为首先我们几乎是无限的聪明才智。我认为我们中最优秀的人和最优秀的人的思想是令人难以置信的。

segments.text: And I love meeting and watching any human that’s the top of their game, whether that’s sport or science or art, it’s just nothing more wonderful than that, seeing them in their element in flow.

translation.text: 我喜欢见到和观看任何在比赛中处于顶尖水平的人，无论是体育、科学还是艺术，没有什么比看到他们在流动的元素中更美妙的了。

segments.text: I think it’s almost limitless. Our brains are general systems, intelligent systems, so I think it’s almost limitless what we can potentially do with them.

translation.text: 我认为这几乎是无限的。我们的大脑是通用系统、智能系统，所以我认为我们可以用它们做几乎无限的事情。

segments.text: And then the other thing is our extreme adaptability. I think it’s going to be okay in terms of there’s going to be a lot of change, but look where we are now without effectively our hunter-gatherer brains.

translation.text: 另一件事是我们的极端适应能力。我认为这不会有什么问题，因为会有很多变化，但看看我们现在的处境，没有有效地利用狩猎采集者的大脑。

segments.text: How is it we can cope with the modern world, right? Flying on planes, doing podcasts, playing computer games and virtual simulations. I mean, it’s already mind blowing given that our mind was developed for hunting buffaloes on the tundra.

translation.text: 我们如何应对现代世界，对吧？乘坐飞机、播客、玩电脑游戏和虚拟模拟。我的意思是，考虑到我们的大脑是为在苔原上狩猎水牛而开发的，这已经令人震惊了。

segments.text: And so I think this is just the next step, and it’s actually kind of interesting to see how society’s already adapted to this mind blowing AI technology we have today already.

translation.text: 所以我认为这只是下一步，看到社会如何适应我们今天已经拥有的令人兴奋的人工智能技术实际上很有趣。

segments.text: It’s sort of like, “Oh, I talked to chat bots. Totally fine.”

translation.text: 有点像，“哦，我和聊天机器人交谈过。完全没问题。”

segments.text: And it’s very possible that this very podcast activity, which I’m here for, will be completely replaced by AI. I’m very replaceable and I’m waiting for it.

translation.text: 我来这里的这个播客活动很可能会被人工智能完全取代。我很容易被替代，我正在等待它。

segments.text: Not to the level that you can do it, Lex, I don’t think.

translation.text: 还没到你能做到的程度，莱克斯，我不认为。

segments.text: Thank you. That’s what we humans do to each other. We compliment.

translation.text: 谢谢。这就是我们人类对彼此所做的事情。我们恭维。

segments.text: Exactly.

translation.text: 确切地。

segments.text: All right. And I’m deeply grateful for us humans to have this infinite capacity for curiosity, adaptability, like you said, and also compassion and ability to love.

translation.text: 好的。我非常感谢我们人类拥有无限的好奇心、适应能力，就像你所说的那样，还有同情心和爱的能力。

segments.text: Exactly.

translation.text: 确切地。

segments.text: All of those human things.

translation.text: 所有这些人类的事情。

segments.text: All the things that are deeply human.

translation.text: 所有深藏于人性之中的事物。

segments.text: Well, this is a huge honor, Demis. You are one of the truly special humans in the world. Thank you so much for doing what you do and for talking today.

translation.text: 嗯，这是一个巨大的荣誉，黛米斯。你是世界上真正特别的人之一。非常感谢您今天所做的事情和讲话。

segments.text: Well, thank you very much, Lex.

translation.text: 嗯，非常感谢你，莱克斯。

segments.text: Thanks for listening to this conversation with Demis Hassabis. To support this podcast, please check out our sponsors in the description and consider subscribing to this channel.

translation.text: 感谢您收听与 德米斯·哈萨比斯 的对话。为了支持这个播客，请在描述中查看我们的赞助商并考虑订阅这个频道。

segments.text: And now let me answer some questions and try to articulate some things I’ve been thinking about. If you’d like to submit questions including in audio and video form, go to lexfridman.com/ama.

translation.text: 现在让我回答一些问题并尝试阐明我一直在思考的一些事情。如果您想以音频和视频形式提交问题，请访问 lexfridman.com/ama。

segments.text: I got a lot of amazing questions, thoughts and requests from folks. I’ll keep trying to pick some randomly and comment on it at the end of every episode.

translation.text: 我从人们那里收到了很多令人惊奇的问题、想法和要求。我会继续尝试随机挑选一些并在每集结束时对其进行评论。

segments.text: I got a note on May 21st this year that said, “Hi, Lex. 20 years ago today, David Foster Wallace delivered his famous This is Water speech at Kenyon College.

translation.text: 今年 5 月 21 日，我收到一张纸条，上面写着：“嗨，莱克斯。20 年前的今天，大卫·福斯特·华莱士在凯尼恩学院发表了他著名的《这就是水》演讲。

segments.text: What do you think of this speech?

translation.text: 您对这次演讲有何看法？

segments.text: Well, first, I think this is probably one of the greatest and most unique commencement speeches ever given, but of course, I have many favorites, including the one by Steve Jobs.

translation.text: 好吧，首先，我认为这可能是有史以来最伟大、最独特的毕业典礼演讲之一，但当然，我有很多最喜欢的演讲，包括史蒂夫·乔布斯的演讲。

segments.text: And David Foster Wallace is one of my favorite writers and one of my favorite humans. There’s a tragic honesty to his work, and it always felt as if he was engaging in a constant battle with his own mind, and the writing, his writing were kind of his notes from the front lines of that battle.

translation.text: 大卫·福斯特·华莱士是我最喜欢的作家之一，也是我最喜欢的人类之一。他的作品有一种悲剧性的诚实，总让人感觉他在与自己的思想进行一场持续的战斗，而他的写作，他的写作，就像是他在这场战斗的前线所做的笔记。

segments.text: Now onto the speech, let me quote some parts. There’s of course the parable of the fish and the water that goes, there are these two young fish swimming along and they happen to meet an older fish swimming the other way who nods at them and says, “Morning boys, how’s the water?”

translation.text: 现在进入演讲，让我引用一些部分。当然还有鱼和水流动的寓言，有两条小鱼在游，他们碰巧遇到了一条朝相反方向游的老鱼，老鱼向他们点点头说：“早上好，孩子们，水怎么样？”

segments.text: And the two young fish swim on for a bit and then eventually one of them looks over at the other and goes, “What the hell is water?”

translation.text: 两条小鱼继续游了一会儿，最后其中一条看着另一条，问道：“水到底是什么？”

segments.text: In the speech, David Foster Wallace goes on to say, “The point of the fish story is merely that the most obvious important realities are often the ones that are hardest to see and talk about.

translation.text: 大卫·福斯特·华莱士在演讲中接着说道：“鱼故事的要点在于，最明显的重要现实往往是最难看到和谈论的现实。

segments.text: Stated as an English sentence of course, this is just the banal platitude, but the fact is that in the day to day trenches of adult existence, banal platitudes can have a life or death importance, or so I wish to suggest to you in this dry and lovely morning.”

translation.text: 当然，用英语句子来说，这只是陈词滥调，但事实是，在成人生活的日复一日的战壕中，陈词滥调可能具有生死攸关的重要性，或者说我想在这个干燥而可爱的早晨向你建议。

segments.text: I have several takeaways from this parable and the speech that follows. First, I think we must question everything, and in particular, the most basic assumptions about our reality, our life, and the very nature of existence, and that this project is a deeply personal one.

translation.text: 我从这个寓言和接下来的演讲中得到了一些收获。首先，我认为我们必须质疑一切，特别是关于我们的现实、我们的生活以及存在本质的最基本的假设，并且这个项目是一个非常个人化的项目。

segments.text: In some fundamental sense, nobody can really help you in this process of discovery.

translation.text: 从某种基本意义上来说，没有人能在这个发现过程中真正帮助你。

segments.text: The call to action here, I think, from David Foster Wallace as he puts it, is to ” To be just a little less arrogant,

translation.text: 我认为，大卫·福斯特·华莱士（David Foster Wallace）所说的行动号召是“不要那么傲慢，

segments.text: to have just a little more critical awareness about myself and my certainties because a huge percentage of the stuff that I tend to be automatically certain of is it turns out totally wrong and deluded.”

translation.text: 对我自己和我的确定性有更多的批判性认识，因为我倾向于自动确定的事情中有很大一部分结果证明是完全错误和欺骗的。”

segments.text: All right, back to me. Lex speaking. Second takeaway is that the central spiritual battles of our life are not fought on a mountain top somewhere at a meditation retreat, but it’s fought in the mundane moments of daily life.

translation.text: 好吧，回到我身边。莱克斯说话了。第二个要点是，我们生活中的核心精神斗争不是在山顶冥想静修处进行的，而是在日常生活的平凡时刻进行的。

segments.text: Third takeaway is that we too easily give away our time and attention to the multitude of distractions that the world feeds us, the insatiable black holes of attention.

translation.text: 第三个要点是，我们太容易将时间和注意力浪费在世界提供给我们的众多干扰上，即贪得无厌的注意力黑洞。

segments.text: David Foster Wallace’s call to action in this case is to be deeply aware of the beauty in each moment and to find meaning in the mundane.

translation.text: 大卫·福斯特·华莱士在这种情况下呼吁采取行动，深刻认识到每一刻的美丽，并在平凡中寻找意义。

segments.text: I often quote David Foster Wallace in his advice that the key to life is to be unborable, and I think this is exactly right.

translation.text: 我经常引用大卫·福斯特·华莱士的建议，生活的关键是要变得无趣，我认为这是完全正确的。

segments.text: Every moment, every object, every experience when looked at closely enough contains within it infinite richness to explore. And since Demis Hassabis of this very podcast episode and I are such fans of Richard Feynman, allow me to also quote Mr. Feynman on this topic as well.

translation.text: 如果仔细观察的话，每一个时刻、每一个物体、每一次经历都蕴含着无限的丰富性，值得探索。由于这个播客节目的 德米斯·哈萨比斯 和我都是理查德·费曼的粉丝，请允许我在这个话题上也引用费曼先生的话。

segments.text: “I have a friend who’s an artist and has sometimes taken a view which I don’t agree with very well. He’ll hold up a flower and say, “Look how beautiful it is,” and I’ll agree.

translation.text: “我有一个朋友，他是一位艺术家，有时会提出一些我不太同意的观点。他会举起一朵花说，“看看它多漂亮”，我就会同意。

segments.text: Then he says, “I as an artist can see how beautiful this is, but you as a scientist take this all apart and it becomes a dull thing,” and I think that’s kind of nutty.

translation.text: 然后他说，“作为一名艺术家，我可以看到这有多么美丽，但作为一名科学家，你把这一切拆开，它就变得乏味了，”我认为这有点疯狂。

segments.text: First of all, the beauty that he sees is available to other people and to me too, I believe. Although I may not be quite as refined aesthetically as he is, I can appreciate the beauty of a flower.

translation.text: 首先，我相信，他所看到的美丽对其他人和我来说都是可见的。虽然我的审美可能不如他那么精致，但我却能欣赏一朵花的美丽。

segments.text: At the same time, I see much more about the flower than he sees. I could imagine the cells in there, the complicated actions inside which also have beauty.

translation.text: 与此同时，我对这朵花的了解比他多得多。我可以想象里面的细胞，里面复杂的动作也有美感。

segments.text: I mean, it’s not just beauty at this dimension at one centimeter, there’s also beauty at the smaller dimensions.”

translation.text: 我的意思是，不只是一厘米这个维度的美，还有更小的维度的美。”

segments.text: “Their inner structure, also the processes, the fact that the colors and the flower evolved in order to attract the insects to pollinate it is interesting.

translation.text: “它们的内部结构，还有过程，颜色和花朵的进化是为了吸引昆虫为其授粉，这一事实很有趣。

segments.text: It means that the insects can see the color. It adds a question. Does this aesthetic sense also exist in lower forms?

translation.text: 这意味着昆虫可以看到颜色。它增加了一个问题。这种美感在低等形态中是否也存在呢？

segments.text: Why is it aesthetic? All kinds of interesting questions, which the science knowledge only adds to the excitement, the mystery, and the awe of a flower.

translation.text: 为什么它是审美的？各种有趣的问题，科学知识只会给花朵增添兴奋、神秘和敬畏。

segments.text: It only adds.”

translation.text: 它只会增加。”

segments.text: All right, back to David Foster Wallace’s speech. He has a great story in there that I particularly enjoy. It goes, there are these two guys sitting together in a bar in the remote Alaskan wilderness.

translation.text: 好吧，回到大卫·福斯特·华莱士的演讲。他在那里有一个我特别喜欢的精彩故事。据说，这两个人坐在阿拉斯加偏远荒野的一家酒吧里。

segments.text: One of the guys is religious, the other is an atheist, and the two are arguing about the existence of God with that special intensity that comes after about the fourth beer.

translation.text: 其中一个是有宗教信仰的人，另一个是无神论者，两人正在以第四杯啤酒后那种特殊的强度争论上帝的存在。

segments.text: And the atheist says, “Look, it’s not like I don’t have actual reasons for not believing in God. It’s not like I haven’t ever experimented with the whole God and prayer thing.

translation.text: 无神论者说：“看，我并不是没有真正的理由不相信上帝。我也不是没有尝试过整个上帝和祈祷的事情。

segments.text: Just last month, I got caught away from the camp in that terrible blizzard, and I was totally lost and I couldn’t see a thing and it was 50 below.

translation.text: 就在上个月，我在一场可怕的暴风雪中离开了营地，我完全迷路了，什么也看不见，而且气温只有 50 度以下。

segments.text: So I tried it. I fell in my knees in the snow and cried out, ‘Oh God, if there is a God, I’m lost in this blizzard and I’m going to die if you don’t help me.”

translation.text: 所以我尝试了一下。我跪倒在雪地里，大声喊道：“天哪，如果有上帝的话，我会在这场暴风雪中迷路的，如果你不帮助我，我就会死掉。”

segments.text: And now back in the bar, the religious guy looks at the atheist all puzzled, “Well, then you must believe now?” he says, “After all, there you are, alive.”

translation.text: 现在回到酒吧，宗教人士困惑地看着无神论者，“那么你现在必须相信了吗？”他说：“毕竟，你还活着。”

segments.text: The atheist just rolls his eyes. “No, man. All that happened was a couple of Eskimos happened to be wandering by and showed me the way back to the camp.”

translation.text: 无神论者只是翻白眼。 “不，伙计。所发生的只是有几个爱斯基摩人碰巧路过，给我指了回营地的路。”

segments.text: All this, I think, teaches us that everything is a matter of perspective and that wisdom may arrive if we have the humility to keep shifting and expanding our perspective on the world.

translation.text: 我认为，所有这些都告诉我们，一切都取决于视角，如果我们谦虚地不断转变和扩展我们对世界的看法，智慧就可能到来。

segments.text: Thank you for allowing me to talk a bit about David Foster Wallace. He’s one of my favorite writers and he’s a beautiful soul.

translation.text: 感谢您允许我谈谈大卫·福斯特·华莱士。他是我最喜欢的作家之一，他有一个美丽的灵魂。

segments.text: If I may, one more thing I wanted to briefly comment on. I find myself to be in this strange position of getting attacked online often from all sides, including being lied about sometimes through selective misrepresentation, but often through downright lies.

translation.text: 如果可以的话，我还想简要评论一件事。我发现自己处于这种奇怪的境地，经常在网上受到来自各方的攻击，包括有时通过选择性的失实陈述而被欺骗，但通常是通过彻头彻尾的谎言。

segments.text: I don’t know how else to put it. This all breaks my heart, frankly, but I’ve come to understand that it’s the way of the internet and the cost of the path I’ve chosen.

translation.text: 我不知道还能怎么说。坦率地说，这一切让我心碎，但我开始明白，这就是互联网的方式以及我选择的道路的成本。

segments.text: There’s been days when it’s been rough on me mentally. It’s not fun being lied about, especially when it’s about things that are usually for a long time have been a source of happiness and joy for me.

translation.text: 有几天我的精神状态很不好。被谎言并不好玩，尤其是当这些事情长期以来一直是我幸福和快乐的源泉时。

segments.text: But again, that’s life.

translation.text: 但话又说回来，这就是生活。

segments.text: I’ll continue exploring the world of people and ideas with empathy and rigor, wearing my heart on my sleeve as much as I can.

translation.text: 我将继续以同理心和严谨的态度探索人和思想的世界，尽我所能地坦诚相待。

segments.text: For me, that’s the only way to live. Anyway, a common attack on me is about my time at MIT and Drexel, two great universities I love and have tremendous respect for.

translation.text: 对我来说，这是唯一的生活方式。不管怎样，对我最常见的攻击是关于我在麻省理工学院和德雷克塞尔大学的经历，这两所我热爱并非常尊重的伟大大学。

segments.text: Since a bunch of lies have accumulated online about me on these topics, to a sad and at times hilarious degree, I thought I would once more state the obvious facts about my bio for the small number of you who may care.

translation.text: 由于网上关于这些话题的关于我的谎言堆积如山，达到了令人悲伤、有时又搞笑的程度，我想我会再次为少数可能关心的人陈述有关我的简历的明显事实。

segments.text: TLDR, two things. First, as I say often, including in a recent podcast episode that somehow was listened to by many millions of people, I proudly went to Drexel University for my bachelor’s, master’s, and doctorate degrees.

translation.text: 长话短说，有两件事。首先，正如我经常说的，包括在最近的一期播客节目中，不知何故有数百万人收听，我自豪地去了德雷塞尔大学获得学士、硕士和博士学位。

segments.text: Second, I am a research scientist at MIT and have been there in a paid research position for the last 10 years.

translation.text: 其次，我是麻省理工学院的一名研究科学家，过去 10 年一直在那里担任带薪研究职位。

segments.text: Allow me to elaborate a bit more on these two things now, but please skip if this is not at all interesting.

translation.text: 现在请允许我详细说明这两件事，但如果这一点也不有趣，请跳过。

segments.text: So like I said, a common attack on me is that I have no real affiliation with MIT. The accusation, I guess, is that I’m falsely claiming an MIT affiliation because I taught a lecture there once.

translation.text: 正如我所说，对我的常见攻击是我与麻省理工学院没有真正的联系。我想，指控是我谎称自己与麻省理工学院有联系，因为我曾经在那里讲过课。

segments.text: Nope, that accusation against me is a complete lie. I have been at MIT for over 10 years in a paid research position from 2015 to today.

translation.text: 不，对我的指控完全是谎言。从 2015 年至今，我在麻省理工学院担任带薪研究职位已超过 10 年。

segments.text: To be extra clear, I’m a research scientist at MIT working in LIDS, the Laboratory for Information and Decision Systems in the College of Computing.

translation.text: 更明确地说，我是麻省理工学院计算机学院信息与决策系统实验室 LIDS 的一名研究科学家。

segments.text: For now, since I’m still at MIT, you can see me in the directory and on the various lab pages.

translation.text: 目前，由于我仍在麻省理工学院，您可以在目录和各个实验室页面上看到我。

segments.text: I have indeed given many lectures at MIT over the years, a small fraction of which I posted online. Teaching for me always has been just for fun and not part of my research work.

translation.text: 多年来，我确实在麻省理工学院做过很多讲座，其中一小部分我发布在网上。对我来说，教学只是为了好玩，而不是我研究工作的一部分。

segments.text: I personally think I suck at it, but I have always learned and grown from the experience. It’s like Feynman spoke about, if you want to understand something deeply, it’s good to try to teach it.

translation.text: 我个人认为我很糟糕，但我总是从经历中学习和成长。就像费曼说的，如果你想深入理解某件事，最好尝试去教授它。

segments.text: But like I said, my main focus has always been on research. I published many peer-reviewed papers that you can see in my Google Scholar profile.

translation.text: 但正如我所说，我的主要关注点始终是研究。我发表了许多经过同行评审的论文，您可以在我的谷歌学术个人资料中看到这些论文。

segments.text: For my first four years at MIT, I worked extremely intensively. Most weeks were 80 to 100-hour work weeks. After that, in 2019, I still kept my research scientist position, but I split my time taking a leap to pursue projects in AI and robotics outside MIT and to dedicate a lot of focus to the podcast.

translation.text: 在麻省理工学院的头四年里，我工作非常紧张。大多数周的工作时间为 80 至 100 小时。此后，在 2019 年，我仍然保留了我的研究科学家职位，但我把时间花在了麻省理工学院之外的人工智能和机器人项目上，并将大量精力投入到播客上。

segments.text: As I’ve said, I’ve been continuously surprised just how many hours preparing for an episode takes. There are many episodes of the podcast for which I have to read, write, and think for 100, 200 or more hours across multiple weeks and months.

translation.text: 正如我所说，我一直对准备一集需要花费多少时间感到惊讶。播客中有很多剧集需要我在数周和数月内花费 100、200 或更多小时来阅读、写作和思考。

segments.text: Since 2020, I have not actively published research papers. Just like the podcast, I think it’s something that’s a serious full-time effort.

translation.text: 2020年以来，我没有积极发表研究论文。就像播客一样，我认为这是一项认真的全职工作。

segments.text: But not publishing and doing full-time research has been eating at me because I love research and I love programming and building systems that test out interesting technical ideas, especially in the context of human-AI or human-robot interaction.

translation.text: 但不出版和做全职研究一直困扰着我，因为我喜欢研究，喜欢编程和构建测试有趣技术想法的系统，特别是在人与人工智能或人与机器人交互的背景下。

segments.text: I hope to change this in the coming months and years.

translation.text: 我希望在未来几个月和几年内改变这一点。

segments.text: What I’ve come to realize about myself is if I don’t publish or if I don’t launch systems that people use, I definitely feel like a piece of me is missing.

translation.text: 我对自己的认识是，如果我不发布或者不推出人们使用的系统，我肯定会觉得自己缺失了一部分。

segments.text: It legitimately is a source of happiness for me. Anyway, I’m proud of my time at MIT. I was and am constantly surrounded by people much smarter than me, many of whom have become lifelong colleagues and friends.

translation.text: 这确实是我幸福的源泉。不管怎样，我为我在麻省理工学院的时光感到自豪。我过去和现在都经常被比我聪明得多的人包围，其中许多人已经成为终生的同事和朋友。

segments.text: MIT is a place I go to escape the world, to focus on exploring fascinating questions at the cutting edge of science and engineering.

translation.text: 麻省理工学院是我逃避世界的地方，专注于探索科学和工程前沿的有趣问题。

segments.text: This, again, makes me truly happy and it does hit pretty hard on a psychological level when I’m getting attacked over this.

translation.text: 这再次让我真正感到高兴，当我因此受到攻击时，它确实在心理层面上造成了相当大的打击。

segments.text: Perhaps I’m doing something wrong. If I am, I will try to do better.

translation.text: 也许我做错了什么。如果是的话，我会努力做得更好。

segments.text: In all this discussion of academic work, I hope you know that I don’t ever mean to say that I’m an expert at anything.

translation.text: 在所有关于学术工作的讨论中，我希望你知道我并不是想说我是任何方面的专家。

segments.text: In the podcast and in my private life, I don’t claim to be smart. In fact, I often call myself an idiot and mean it.

translation.text: 在播客和我的私生活中，我并不声称自己很聪明。事实上，我经常称自己为白痴，而且是认真的。

segments.text: I try to make fun of myself as much as possible, and in general to celebrate others instead. Now to talk about Drexel University, which I also love, am proud of and am deeply grateful for my time there.

translation.text: 我会尽可能地取笑自己，并通常去庆祝别人。现在谈谈德雷克塞尔大学，我也热爱这所大学，为之感到自豪，并深深感激我在那里度过的时光。

segments.text: As I said, I went to Drexel for my bachelor’s, master’s, and doctorate degrees in computer science and electrical engineering. I’ve talked about Drexel many times, including, as I mentioned, at the end of a recent podcast, the Donald Trump episode. funny enough, that was listened to by many millions of people where I answered a question about graduate school and explained my own journey at Drexel and how grateful I am for it.

translation.text: 正如我所说，我在德雷克塞尔大学获得了计算机科学和电气工程的学士、硕士和博士学位。我曾多次谈论过德雷克塞尔，包括正如我在最近的播客节目《唐纳德·特朗普》节目结尾处提到的。有趣的是，数百万人聆听了我的演讲，我回答了有关研究生院的问题，并解释了我自己在德雷塞尔大学的经历以及我对此的感激之情。

segments.text: If it’s at all interesting to you, please go listen to the end of that episode or watch the related clip. At Drexel, I met and worked with many brilliant researchers and mentors from whom I’ve learned a lot about engineering, science and life.

translation.text: 如果您感兴趣，请去听该集的结尾或观看相关剪辑。在德雷克塞尔，我遇到了许多杰出的研究人员和导师并与他们一起工作，从他们那里我学到了很多关于工程、科学和生活的知识。

segments.text: There are many valuable things I gained from my time at Drexel. First, I took a large number of very difficult math and theoretical computer science courses.

translation.text: 我在德雷克塞尔的时光获得了许多宝贵的东西。首先，我选修了大量非常困难的数学和理论计算机科学课程。

segments.text: They taught me how to think deeply and rigorously, and also how to work hard and not give up even if it feels like I’m too dumb to find a solution to a technical problem.

translation.text: 他们教会了我如何深入、严谨地思考，以及如何努力工作，不要放弃，即使感觉自己太笨而无法找到技术问题的解决方案。

segments.text: Second, I programmed a lot during that time, mostly C, C++. I programmed robots, optimization algorithms, computer vision systems, wireless network protocols, multimodal machine learning systems, and all kinds of simulations of physical systems.

translation.text: 其次，那段时间我写了很多程序，主要是C、C++。我对机器人、优化算法、计算机视觉系统、无线网络协议、多模态机器学习系统以及各种物理系统的模拟进行了编程。

segments.text: This is where I really developed a love for programming, including, yes, Emacs And the Kinesis keyboard. I also, during that time, read a lot, I played a lot of guitar, wrote a lot of crappy poetry, and trained a lot in judo and jiu-jitsu, which I cannot sing enough praises to.

translation.text: 这就是我真正热爱编程的地方，包括 Emacs 和 Kinesis 键盘。那段时间我也读了很多书，弹了很多吉他，写了很多蹩脚的诗，还练了很多柔道和柔术，对此我赞不绝口。

segments.text: Jiu-jitsu humbled me on a daily basis throughout my twenties, and it still does to this very day whenever I get a chance to train.

translation.text: 在我二十多岁时，柔术每天都让我感到谦卑，直到今天，每当我有机会训练时，它仍然让我感到谦卑。

segments.text: Anyway, I hope that the folks who occasionally get swept up in the chanting online crowds that want to tear down others don’t lose themselves in it too much.

translation.text: 不管怎样，我希望那些偶尔被网络上高喊口号、想要拆毁别人的人卷入其中的人们不要在其中太迷失自己。

segments.text: In the end, I still think there’s more good than bad in people. But we’re all each of us a mixed bag.

translation.text: 归根结底，我仍然认为人性中的善多于恶。但我们每个人都是一个混合体。

segments.text: I know I am very much flawed. I speak awkwardly. I sometimes say stupid shit.

translation.text: 我知道我有很大的缺陷。我说话很尴尬。我有时会说些蠢话。

segments.text: I can get irrationally emotional. I can be too much of a dick when I should be kind. I can lose myself in a biased rabbit hole before I wake up to the bigger, more accurate picture of reality.

translation.text: 我可能会变得非理性的情绪化。当我应该友善时，我却变得太混蛋了。在我醒来看到更大、更准确的现实图景之前，我可能会迷失在一个有偏见的兔子洞里。

segments.text: I’m human and so are you for better or for worse, and I do still believe we’re in this whole beautiful mess together.

translation.text: 我是人类，你也是人类，无论好坏，我仍然相信我们在一起处于这一切美丽的混乱之中。

segments.text: I love you all.

translation.text: 我爱你们。
